topics.title: Techniques of mathematical modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT864 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Techniques of mathematical modeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1 — TECHNIQUES OF MATHEMATICAL MODELING

## 1.1 Core Technical Definition

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Mathematical Modeling (KTU Definition):**
> A **mathematical model** is a formal description of a real physiological system expressed in terms of mathematical entities (variables, constants, equations, functions) that captures the essential structure and behavior of the system. The *techniques* of mathematical modeling refer to the systematic procedures — compartmental analysis, differential equation formulation, electrical analog (lumped parameter) modeling, bond graph representation, and statistical/empirical identification — used to translate a biological process into a solvable mathematical framework.

In the context of **PEBMT864 — Modelling of Physiological Systems**, the modeling techniques form the *toolbox* that a biomedical engineer uses to:
1. Abstract a living system (e.g., lungs, kidney, heart, glucose–insulin axis) into a set of state variables.
2. Express the inter-relationships between these variables using **ordinary differential equations (ODEs)**, **partial differential equations (PDEs)**, or **transfer functions**.
3. Predict the response to physiological or pharmacological inputs.
4. Validate the model against experimental data.

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview — The "Recipe" Analogy

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — Cooking as Modeling**
> Think of a *physiological system* as a complex dish being prepared in a kitchen. The *ingredients* are the **state variables** (volume, pressure, concentration, temperature). The *recipe instructions* (stir, heat, mix) are the **mathematical operators** (derivatives, integrals, transfer functions). The *chef's notebook* is the **model**, and the *tasting panel* is the **validation step**.
> - If the dish is simple (boiling water), a single equation (Newton's law of cooling) suffices — a **lumped model**.
> - If the dish requires precise heat distribution (tandoor bread), we need spatial coordinates — a **distributed parameter model**.
> - If we have no idea how the recipe works, we just observe taste changes when salt is added — an **empirical / black-box model**.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU High-Yield Tip:** A model is never "true" — it is only *useful*. The art of mathematical modeling lies in choosing the *right level of complexity* that balances **physiological fidelity** against **mathematical tractability**.

## 1.3 Classification of Mathematical Modeling Techniques

| Technique | Mathematical Form | Typical Physiological Example |
|---|---|---|
| **Lumped-Parameter (ODE) Model** | $\frac{dx}{dt} = f(x,u)$ | RC circuit analog of arterial windkessel |
| **Distributed-Parameter (PDE) Model** | PDE with spatial derivatives | Oxygen diffusion in tissue, heat in limb |
| **Compartmental Model** | System of linear ODEs | Drug pharmacokinetics, tracer kinetics |
| **State-Space Model** | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$ | Multi-organ cardiovascular model |
| **Transfer-Function Model** | $G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)}$ | Pupil light reflex, neuromuscular control |
| **Bond Graph Technique** | Power-flow graph with 1-junctions | Skeletal muscle, heart mechanics |
| **Empirical / Regression Model** | $y = f(x_1,x_2,\dots)$ via regression | Heart-rate vs. workload curves |
| **Stochastic Model** | $\dot{x} = f(x) + \sigma\,\xi(t)$ | Neural spike trains, ion-channel noise |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS & KTU FORMULA SHEET

## 2.1 The Five-Stage Modeling Pipeline

Every KTU-style modeling problem in this module follows a canonical five-stage pipeline. Mastering these stages is a **board-exam guarantee**.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The Modeling Pipeline (Stage 1 → Stage 5):**
> 1. **Problem Definition** — Identify the physiological question, the inputs (forcing functions), and the outputs (responses of interest).
> 2. **System Abstraction** — Reduce the anatomy to compartments, nodes, or lumps; choose state variables.
> 3. **Equation Formulation** — Apply conservation laws (mass, charge, momentum, energy) to write governing equations.
> 4. **Solution** — Analytical (Laplace transform, eigenvalues) or numerical (Euler, Runge–Kutta) methods.
> 5. **Validation & Interpretation** — Compare simulation with physiological data; perform sensitivity analysis.

## 2.2 Core Mathematical Techniques — A KTU-Ready Summary

### A. Conservation Law (Mass / Volume / Charge Balance)

The **fundamental building block** of nearly every physiological model. Applied to any *compartment* or *lump*:

$$
\text{Rate of Accumulation} = \text{Rate of Inflow} - \text{Rate of Outflow} + \text{Rate of Generation}
$$

In ODE form for a single compartment of state $x(t)$ with input $u(t)$:

$$
\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{in}(t) - \dot{Q}_{out}(t) + G(t)
$$

If the outflow is **linear** (proportional to $x$): $\dot{Q}_{out} = \frac{x}{R}$ where $R$ is the *resistance*, and the inflow is driven by a pressure/gradient divided by a *resistance*, the system reduces to a first-order **RC analog**.

### B. The Lumped-Parameter (RC / RLC) Electrical Analog

This is **THE most-tested technique** in KTU ESE papers for this module. Every passive physiological element is mapped to an electrical component:

| Physiological Element | Electrical Analog | Symbol |
|---|---|---|
| Blood flow resistance | Resistor | $R$ |
| Vascular compliance / lung compliance | Capacitor | $C$ |
| Blood inertia (large arteries) | Inductor | $L$ |
| Pressure gradient | Voltage source | $V$ or $P$ |
| Volume / flow rate | Charge / current | $q$ or $Q$ |
| Valve (one-way) | Diode | $D$ |

For an **RC compartment** (e.g., Windkessel model of the aorta):

$$
\frac{dP(t)}{dt} + \frac{P(t)}{RC} = \frac{P_{in}(t)}{RC}
$$

For a **second-order RLC analog** (e.g., aorta with inertial blood column):

$$
LC\frac{d^{2}P}{dt^{2}} + \frac{L}{R}\frac{dP}{dt} + P(t) = P_{in}(t)
$$

### C. The Transfer-Function Approach (Frequency Domain)

Taking the **Laplace transform** of the linear ODE under zero initial conditions yields a rational function:

$$
G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{b_{m}s^{m} + b_{m-1}s^{m-1} + \cdots + b_{0}}{a_{n}s^{n} + a_{n-1}s^{n-1} + \cdots + a_{0}}
$$

The **poles** of $G(s)$ characterize the natural response modes (relaxation times, oscillation frequencies) and the **zeros** characterize the input-shaping behaviour.

### D. The State-Space Representation

A multi-variable, multi-output physiological system is compactly written as:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = A\,\mathbf{x}(t) + B\,\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

$$
\mathbf{y}(t) = C\,\mathbf{x}(t) + D\,\mathbf{u}(t)
$$

where $\mathbf{x}\in\mathbb{R}^{n}$ is the state vector, $\mathbf{u}\in\mathbb{R}^{m}$ the input, $\mathbf{y}\in\mathbb{R}^{p}$ the output, and $A,B,C,D$ are constant matrices.

### E. The Compartmental Modeling Technique

A *compartment* is a well-mixed, kinetically homogeneous pool. A classical $n$-compartment mammillary model (e.g., for iodine kinetics) is solved via:

$$
\dot{\mathbf{q}}(t) = K\,\mathbf{q}(t) + \mathbf{u}(t), \qquad \mathbf{q}(0) = \mathbf{q}_{0}
$$

where $K$ is the **rate-constant matrix** with diagonal entries $k_{ii} = -\sum_{j\ne i}k_{ij}$ and off-diagonal entries $k_{ij} = k_{i\leftarrow j}$.

## 2.3 The KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!NOTE]
> **Master this table — it covers 80% of the analytical questions in Module 1.**

| # | Technique | Governing Equation | Key Parameter | Physiological Use |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | Mass balance (general) | $\frac{dx}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{in} - \dot{Q}_{out} + G$ | $x(t)$ state | All single-pool models |
| 2 | First-order linear ODE | $\tau \frac{dy}{dt} + y = K\,u(t)$ | Time constant $\tau$, gain $K$ | Drug absorption, thermoregulation |
| 3 | RC analog (Windkessel) | $RC\frac{dP}{dt} + P = P_{in}$ | Compliance $C$, Resistance $R$ | Systemic arterial pressure |
| 4 | RLC second-order | $LC\ddot{P} + \frac{L}{R}\dot{P} + P = P_{in}$ | Inertia $L$ | Aortic input impedance |
| 5 | Transfer function | $G(s) = \frac{b_{m}s^{m}+\cdots+b_{0}}{a_{n}s^{n}+\cdots+a_{0}}$ | Poles, zeros | Frequency analysis |
| 6 | State-space | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}=A\mathbf{x}+B\mathbf{u}$ | Matrices $A,B,C,D$ | Multi-organ models |
| 7 | Two-compartment | $\dot{q}_{1}=-k_{12}q_{1}$, $\dot{q}_{2}=k_{12}q_{1}-k_{20}q_{2}$ | Rate constants $k_{ij}$ | Pharmacokinetics |
| 8 | Bond graph | Effort–flow junctions | Power variables | Energy-domain modeling |
| 9 | Diffusion (PDE) | $\frac{\partial c}{\partial t}=D\frac{\partial^{2}c}{\partial x^{2}}$ | Diffusivity $D$ | Tissue O₂ transport |
| 10 | Black-box regression | $y = \beta_{0}+\sum \beta_{i}x_{i}+\varepsilon$ | Coefficients $\beta_{i}$ | Empirical dose-response |

## 2.4 Engineering Utility

These modeling techniques are not just academic — they directly power:
- **Pacemaker / ICD design** — using ODE/RC models of cardiac electrophysiology.
- **Artificial pancreas** — using state-space models of the glucose–insulin axis.
- **Mechanical ventilator tuning** — using transfer functions of lung compliance and airway resistance.
- **Drug dosage regimen design** — using compartmental pharmacokinetics.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. STEP-BY-STEP DERIVATIONS & ANALYTICAL MATRIX

## 3.1 Worked Derivation #1 — Single-Compartment Drug Model (First-Order)

**Problem Setup.** A patient receives an IV bolus of a drug. The body behaves as a single well-mixed compartment of volume $V$ (litres). Drug mass in the compartment is $x(t)$ (mg). The elimination rate is proportional to the plasma concentration, $C(t)=x(t)/V$, with elimination rate constant $k_{e}$ (min$^{-1}$). Find $x(t)$.

**Step 1 — Apply Mass Balance.**

$$
\text{Rate of accumulation} = \text{Inflow} - \text{Outflow} + \text{Generation}
$$

$$
\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = 0 - k_{e}\,C(t)V + 0
$$

**Step 2 — Substitute the concentration relationship** $C(t) = x(t)/V$.

$$
\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = -k_{e}\,\frac{x(t)}{V}\,V
$$

The factor $V$ cancels:

$$
\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = -k_{e}\,x(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Separate variables and integrate.** With initial condition $x(0) = D$ (the bolus dose in mg):

$$
\int_{D}^{x(t)} \frac{dx'}{x'} = -\int_{0}^{t} k_{e}\,d\tau
$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate both sides.**

$$
\ln x(t) - \ln D = -k_{e}t
$$

**Step 5 — Exponentiate and write the final model.**

$$
\boxed{x(t) = D\,e^{-k_{e}t}}
$$

The **time constant** of the compartment is $\tau = 1/k_{e}$, and the **half-life** is $t_{1/2} = \ln 2 / k_{e} \approx 0.693\,\tau$.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation #2 — Two-Compartment Pharmacokinetic Model

**Problem Setup.** The body is split into a *central* compartment (plasma, $q_1$) and a *peripheral* compartment (tissues, $q_2$). Drug is eliminated only from the central compartment at rate $k_{10}$, and inter-compartmental exchange occurs at rate $k_{12}=k_{21}=k$. Initial condition: $q_1(0)=D$, $q_2(0)=0$.

**Step 1 — Mass balance on central compartment.**

$$
\dot{q}_{1}(t) = -k\,q_{1}(t) + k\,q_{2}(t) - k_{10}\,q_{1}(t)
$$

$$
\dot{q}_{1} = -(k+k_{10})q_{1} + k\,q_{2}
$$

**Step 2 — Mass balance on peripheral compartment.**

$$
\dot{q}_{2}(t) = k\,q_{1}(t) - k\,q_{2}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Collect into matrix (state-space) form.**

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix}q_{1}\\ q_{2}\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-(k+k_{10}) & k \\ k & -k\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}q_{1}\\ q_{2}\end{bmatrix}
$$

**Step 4 — Eigenvalues of the system matrix** give the two exponential decay modes.

$$
\det\!\left(\begin{bmatrix}-(k+k_{10})-\lambda & k \\ k & -k-\lambda\end{bmatrix}\right) = 0
$$

$$
[-(k+k_{10})-\lambda][-k-\lambda] - k^{2} = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
\lambda^{2} + (2k+k_{10})\lambda + k\,k_{10} = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Solve the quadratic.**

$$
\lambda_{1,2} = \frac{-(2k+k_{10}) \pm \sqrt{(2k+k_{10})^{2} - 4k\,k_{10}}}{2}
$$

For typical values ($k_{10}=0.1$ min$^{-1}$, $k=0.05$ min$^{-1}$), the discriminant is positive, giving two real negative eigenvalues $\lambda_{1},\lambda_{2}<0$, so the solution is a **bi-exponential**:

$$
q_{1}(t) = A\,e^{\lambda_{1}t} + B\,e^{\lambda_{2}t}
$$

with $A,B$ determined from initial conditions.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation #3 — Windkessel RC Analogue (Aortic Pressure)

**Problem Setup.** During diastole, the aortic valve is closed. The aorta is modeled as a single compliant compartment of compliance $C$ drained into the peripheral resistance $R$ by the aortic pressure $P(t)$.

**Step 1 — Apply volume balance.** Volume stored by compliance: $V(t)=C\,P(t)$.

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = C\frac{dP}{dt}
$$

**Step 2 — Apply flow balance.** Outflow through peripheral resistance:

$$
Q_{out} = \frac{P}{R}
$$

**Step 3 — Equate the two.** No inflow during diastole (valve closed):

$$
C\frac{dP}{dt} = -\frac{P}{R}
$$

**Step 4 — Rearrange into standard first-order form.**

$$
RC\frac{dP}{dt} + P(t) = 0
$$

**Step 5 — Laplace transform** (zero initial conditions not assumed; with $P(0)=P_{0}$):

$$
RC\,[sP(s)-P_{0}] + P(s) = 0
$$

$$
P(s)\,[RCs+1] = RC\,P_{0}
$$

$$
\boxed{P(s) = \frac{P_{0}}{s + \frac{1}{RC}}}
$$

**Step 6 — Inverse Laplace transform** to recover the time-domain diastolic decay:

$$
P(t) = P_{0}\,e^{-t/(RC)}
$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU Examiner's Insight:** The product $RC = \tau$ is the *diastolic time constant* of the arterial system. KTU questions frequently ask students to compute $\tau$, $P(t_{1/2})$, or to identify which analog component corresponds to "compliance".

## 3.4 Analytical Matrix — Choosing the Right Technique

| Physiological Phenomenon | Recommended Technique | Why? |
|---|---|---|
| Drug clearance from blood | Single/two-compartment ODE | First-order kinetics dominant |
| Aortic pressure decay | RC electrical analog | Lumped compliance + resistance |
| Heat distribution in skin | 1-D heat equation (PDE) | Spatial variation matters |
| Heart-rate variability | Stochastic / time-series | Inherent randomness |
| Skeletal muscle contraction | Bond graph | Multi-energy-domain (chemo-mech) |
| Hemodialysis urea removal | Multi-compartment | Two well-mixed pools (blood, dialysate) |
| Pupil light reflex | Transfer function | Linear small-signal approximation |
| Glucose–insulin dynamics | Non-linear state-space | Strong non-linearity, multi-variable |

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. STRUCTURAL DIAGRAMS & SCHEMATICS

## 4.1 The Canonical Modeling Pipeline (Block Diagram)

The following Mermaid block diagram captures the **end-to-end modeling workflow** that KTU expects students to recognize and reproduce:

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[Real Physiological System] --> B[Stage 1 Problem Definition]
    B --> C[Stage 2 System Abstraction]
    C --> D{Choose Modeling Technique}
    D -->|First-order dynamics| E[RC Lumped Analog]
    D -->|Multi-pool kinetics| F[Compartmental ODE]
    D -->|Multi-energy domain| G[Bond Graph]
    D -->|Spatial gradients| H[PDE Distributed Model]
    D -->|Black-box| I[Empirical Regression]
    E --> J[Stage 4 Solution]
    F --> J
    G --> J
    H --> J
    I --> J
    J --> K{Validation}
    K -->|Match| L[Accept Model]
    K -->|Mismatch| M[Refine Abstraction]
    M --> C
    L --> N[Stage 5 Interpretation and Prediction]
```

## 4.2 Two-Compartment Model Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    subgraph CentralPool
        Q1[Central Compartment q1]
    end
    subgraph PeripheralPool
        Q2[Peripheral Compartment q2]
    end
    EXT[External Environment] -->|k10 elimination| Q1
    Q1 <-->|k12 exchange| Q2
    Q1 -->|k10| EXT
```

## 4.3 Electrical Analog (Windkessel) Schematic

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    P0[Pressure Source Pin] --> R[Peripheral Resistance R]
    R --> Node1((Node))
    Node1 --> C[Compliance C]
    Node1 --> OUT[Output Pout of t]
    C --> GND[(Reference / Venous Return)]
```

## 4.4 Decision Tree — Which Technique to Use?

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S0[Identify System] --> S1{Spatial variation important?}
    S1 -->|Yes| S2[Use PDE / Distributed Model]
    S1 -->|No| S3{Number of well-mixed pools?}
    S3 -->|1| S4[Single-compartment ODE]
    S3 -->|>1| S5[Multi-compartment ODE]
    S4 --> S6{Linear?}
    S5 --> S6
    S6 -->|Yes| S7[Transfer function or State-space]
    S6 -->|No| S8[Numerical integration RK4 etc]
    S7 --> S9[Frequency analysis Poles Zeros]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU Note:** When a question says "model the lungs as an electrical circuit", the answer is *always* an RC (or RLC) lumped analog unless spatial ventilation/perfusion gradients are explicitly mentioned. When a question gives a *multi-organ* structure with drug administration, the answer is a *compartmental* model.

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 SCHEME EXAMINATION QUESTION BANK & TOPIC RECAP

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]
**Define a mathematical model. List any four techniques used for modeling physiological systems.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Definition:** A mathematical model is a precise description of a real system expressed using mathematical language — variables, parameters, and relations — that allows the system's behavior to be predicted under specified conditions.
- (1 mark) *Lumped-parameter (RC/RLC) modeling* — replaces physiological elements with electrical analogs.
- (0.5 mark) *Compartmental modeling* — represents the body as interconnected well-mixed pools governed by mass-balance ODEs.
- (0.5 mark) *State-space modeling* — expresses the system as a vector first-order ODE $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}+B\mathbf{u}$.
- (Bonus mention) *Bond graphs, transfer functions, empirical regression, distributed PDE models.*

---

### Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]
**State the conservation principle used to derive the governing equation of a single-compartment model. Write the resulting ODE.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**
- (1 mark) **Principle:** Conservation of mass (or volume, charge, energy — *whichever physical quantity the state variable represents*).
- (1 mark) **Statement:**
$$
\frac{dx(t)}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{in}(t) - \dot{Q}_{out}(t) + G(t)
$$
where $x(t)$ is the quantity being conserved, $\dot{Q}_{in}$, $\dot{Q}_{out}$ are the inflow and outflow rates, and $G(t)$ is the internal generation rate.
- (1 mark) **Typical application** (e.g., single-compartment drug model): $\dfrac{dx}{dt} = -k_e x$ giving $x(t)=D e^{-k_e t}$.

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Module Internal Choice)

### QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Lumped-Parameter / RC Approach

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1]**

**(a)** *Develop the electrical analog of the systemic arterial system during diastole as a simple RC circuit. Derive the governing differential equation for the aortic pressure and obtain the time-domain solution. (7 marks)*

**(b)** *A patient's arterial compliance is $C = 1.5$ mL/mmHg, and the peripheral resistance is $R = 1.2$ mmHg·s/mL. The aortic pressure at the start of diastole is $P_0 = 90$ mmHg. Compute (i) the time constant $\tau$, (ii) the pressure after 0.5 s, and (iii) the half-life of the diastolic decay. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Derivation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark) — System Abstraction.* During diastole, the aortic valve is closed, so the aorta behaves as a *single elastic chamber* (compliance $C$) that empties into the systemic resistance $R$.

*Step 2 (1 mark) — Define state variable.* Let $P(t)$ be the aortic pressure; volume stored is $V(t)=C\,P(t)$.

*Step 3 (1 mark) — Mass/volume balance.*

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = -\frac{P(t)}{R}
$$

*Step 4 (1 mark) — Substitute compliance relation.*

$$
C\frac{dP}{dt} = -\frac{P}{R}
$$

*Step 5 (1 mark) — Rearrange to standard form.*

$$
RC\frac{dP}{dt} + P(t) = 0, \qquad P(0) = P_0
$$

*Step 6 (1 mark) — Solve the ODE.* Separation of variables:

$$
\int_{P_0}^{P(t)}\frac{dP'}{P'} = -\int_{0}^{t}\frac{d\tau}{RC}
$$

$$
\boxed{P(t) = P_0\,e^{-t/(RC)}}
$$

*Step 7 (1 mark) — Physical interpretation.* The system behaves as a first-order low-pass filter with time constant $\tau = RC$. The exponential decay describes the diastolic runoff of blood from the aorta to the periphery.

---

**(b) Numerical Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $C=1.5$ mL/mmHg, $R=1.2$ mmHg·s/mL, $P_0=90$ mmHg.

*(i) Time constant (2 marks):*

$$
\tau = RC = (1.2)(1.5) = 1.8 \text{ s}
$$

*(ii) Pressure at $t = 0.5$ s (2.5 marks):*

$$
P(0.5) = 90\,e^{-0.5/1.8} = 90\,e^{-0.2778}
$$

$$
e^{-0.2778} \approx 0.7575
$$

$$
\boxed{P(0.5) \approx 68.2 \text{ mmHg}}
$$

*(iii) Half-life of the decay (2.5 marks):*

$$
t_{1/2} = \tau \ln 2 = 1.8 \times 0.6931 \approx 1.25 \text{ s}
$$

---

### QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Compartmental Approach

> **[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, Module 1, Alternative]**

**(a)***Formulate the two-compartment pharmacokinetic model for a drug administered as an IV bolus, with elimination occurring only from the central compartment. Write the system of governing ODEs in matrix form and identify the system matrix. (7 marks)*

**(b)***For a two-compartment model, the rate constants are $k_{12}=0.08$ min$^{-1}$, $k_{21}=0.05$ min$^{-1}$, and the elimination rate constant $k_{10}=0.10$ min$^{-1}$. The IV bolus dose is $D=100$ mg and is delivered into the central compartment. Compute the eigenvalues (natural decay rates) of the system. (7 marks)*

---

**Model Solution:**

**(a) Formulation (7 marks):**

*Step 1 (1 mark) — Define state variables.* Let $q_1(t)$ = drug mass in the central compartment (mg) and $q_2(t)$ = drug mass in the peripheral compartment (mg).

*Step 2 (1 mark) — Central compartment balance.* Drug leaves the central pool by distribution to the periphery (rate $k_{12}\,q_1$) and by elimination (rate $k_{10}\,q_1$); it returns from the periphery at rate $k_{21}\,q_2$:

$$
\dot{q}_{1} = -k_{12}q_{1} - k_{10}q_{1} + k_{21}q_{2} = -(k_{12}+k_{10})q_{1} + k_{21}q_{2}
$$

*Step 3 (1 mark) — Peripheral compartment balance.* Drug enters from the central pool and leaves back to it:

$$
\dot{q}_{2} = k_{12}q_{1} - k_{21}q_{2}
$$

*Step 4 (1 mark) — Matrix form.*

$$
\frac{d}{dt}\begin{bmatrix}q_{1}\\ q_{2}\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-(k_{12}+k_{10}) & k_{21}\\ k_{12} & -k_{21}\end{bmatrix}\begin{bmatrix}q_{1}\\ q_{2}\end{bmatrix}, \quad \begin{bmatrix}q_{1}(0)\\ q_{2}(0)\end{bmatrix}=\begin{bmatrix}D\\ 0\end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 5 (1 mark) — System matrix identification.*

$$
\boxed{A = \begin{bmatrix}-(k_{12}+k_{10}) & k_{21}\\ k_{12} & -k_{21}\end{bmatrix}}
$$

*Step 6 (1 mark) — State that solution is a sum of two exponentials (multi-exponential decay), with the two eigenvalues of $A$ giving the two natural decay rates $\alpha$ and $\beta$.*

*Step 7 (1 mark) — Discussion.* A two-compartment model captures the *distribution phase* (fast initial drop) and the *elimination phase* (slow terminal decline) — the bi-exponential character that is ubiquitous in real pharmacokinetic data.

---

**(b) Eigenvalue Computation (7 marks):**

Given: $k_{12}=0.08$, $k_{21}=0.05$, $k_{10}=0.10$ (all min$^{-1}$); $D=100$ mg.

*Step 1 (1.5 marks) — Construct the system matrix.*

$$
A = \begin{bmatrix}-(0.08+0.10) & 0.05\\ 0.08 & -0.05\end{bmatrix} = \begin{bmatrix}-0.18 & 0.05\\ 0.08 & -0.05\end{bmatrix}
$$

*Step 2 (1.5 marks) — Characteristic polynomial.*

$$
\det(A - \lambda I) = (-0.18-\lambda)(-0.05-\lambda) - (0.05)(0.08) = 0
$$

$$
(0.18+\lambda)(0.05+\lambda) - 0.004 = 0
$$

Expanding:

$$
\lambda^{2} + 0.23\lambda + 0.009 - 0.004 = 0
$$

$$
\lambda^{2} + 0.23\lambda + 0.005 = 0
$$

*Step 3 (2 marks) — Solve the quadratic.*

$$
\lambda = \frac{-0.23 \pm \sqrt{(0.23)^{2} - 4(0.005)}}{2}
$$

$$
= \frac{-0.23 \pm \sqrt{0.0529 - 0.0200}}{2}
$$

$$
= \frac{-0.23 \pm \sqrt{0.0329}}{2}
$$

$$
= \frac{-0.23 \pm 0.1814}{2}
$$

*Step 4 (2 marks) — Final eigenvalues.*

$$
\boxed{\lambda_1 = \frac{-0.23 + 0.1814}{2} = -0.0243 \text{ min}^{-1}}
$$

$$
\boxed{\lambda_2 = \frac{-0.23 - 0.1814}{2} = -0.2057 \text{ min}^{-1}}
$$

Both eigenvalues are **real and negative**, confirming stable bi-exponential decay. The slow eigenvalue $|\lambda_1|^{-1} \approx 41$ min corresponds to the **terminal elimination half-life**, while the fast eigenvalue $|\lambda_2|^{-1}\approx 4.86$ min corresponds to the **distribution phase**.

---

## 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning

> [!WARNING]
> **Common Mark-Loss Pitfalls in Module 1 Modeling Questions:**
> 1. **Forgetting units** — The examiner deducts 1 mark if the time constant or eigenvalue is reported *without units* (e.g., write "$\tau=1.8$ s", not just "1.8").
> 2. **Confusing $R$ and $C$** — In RC analogs, $C$ is compliance (volume/pressure), NOT concentration. A common slip is to write the storage relation as $V = C/P$ instead of $V = C\,P$.
> 3. **Sign errors in state-space matrix** — Off-diagonal elements of the system matrix $A$ must carry the **correct sign convention**; the *outflow* from pool 1 appears with a **negative** sign on the diagonal.
> 4. **Skipping the abstraction step** — A 7-mark question typically allocates 1 mark for clearly identifying the state variable and the model boundaries. Always state "let $x(t)$ be …" before writing the equation.
> 5. **Forgetting initial conditions** — When asked for a *time-domain* solution, present the *full* solution with $x(0)=x_0$; without it, 1 mark is lost.
> 6. **Choosing the wrong technique** — A common mistake is to attempt a *transfer-function* approach on a *non-linear* problem. Always check linearity first.

---

## 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Techniques of Mathematical Modeling**

- **Mathematical modeling** is the translation of a real physiological system into a solvable mathematical framework.
- The **five-stage pipeline** is: *Problem Definition → Abstraction → Equation Formulation → Solution → Validation*.
- The **mass-balance equation** $\frac{dx}{dt} = \dot{Q}_{in}-\dot{Q}_{out}+G$ is the *fundamental* governing relation for any lumped compartment.
- **Electrical analog mapping** is the most-tested KTU technique: $R$ ↔ resistance, $C$ ↔ compliance, $L$ ↔ inertia, $V$ ↔ pressure gradient, $I$ ↔ flow.
- The **Windkessel RC model** yields $P(t)=P_0 e^{-t/(RC)}$ during diastole with time constant $\tau = RC$.
- **Transfer functions** $G(s) = Y(s)/U(s)$ are obtained by Laplace-transforming linear ODEs and characterize the system via poles and zeros.
- **State-space form** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x}+B\mathbf{u}$, $\mathbf{y}=C\mathbf{x}+D\mathbf{u}$ is the canonical multi-variable representation; eigenvalues of $A$ are the natural decay rates.
- **Compartmental models** are solved as coupled linear ODEs with a rate-constant matrix $K$; the diagonal of $K$ has negative sums of off-diagonal entries.
- **Distributed-parameter (PDE) models** are needed when spatial gradients dominate (e.g., diffusion, heat conduction).
- **Bond graphs** unify multi-energy-domain systems (chemical, mechanical, electrical, hydraulic) using effort–flow junctions.
- **Empirical / black-box models** ($y=f(x)+\varepsilon$) are used when mechanistic knowledge is insufficient; they rely on regression of experimental data.
- The product $RC$ has units of **time** — verify dimensional consistency in every derivation.
- A model is **useful, not true** — choose the lowest-complexity representation that captures the physiology of interest.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Classification of models and their characteristics
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT864 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Classification of models and their characteristics

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

In the context of **Physiological System Modelling**, a *model* is a mathematical, physical, or logical representation of a biological system (cell, tissue, organ, or whole organism) that approximates its input–output behaviour, internal state dynamics, and structure under defined assumptions.

**Classification of Models** refers to the systematic categorization of these representations based on:
- The mathematical structure used (linear, nonlinear, stochastic).
- The way parameters are distributed in space (lumped vs distributed).
- The way variables evolve in time (static, dynamic, steady-state, transient).
- The level of physiological detail captured (black-box, grey-box, white-box).
- The methodology of construction (empirical, mechanistic, hybrid).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:**
> Module 1 of *Modelling of Physiological Systems* explicitly demands a clear understanding of the **taxonomy of models**, the **assumptions** behind each class, and the **trade-offs** between model fidelity, computational cost, and clinical interpretability.

## 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English View)

Imagine you want to predict how a patient will respond to a new drug dose.

| Real-world Analogy | Model Equivalent |
|---|---|
| A 3D printed plastic replica of the human heart | **Physical Model** |
| A set of differential equations describing blood flow | **Mathematical Model** |
| Plotting "Heart Rate vs. Age" from a dataset without knowing *why* | **Empirical / Black-Box Model** |
| Building the heart from known physics of muscle, valves, vessels | **Mechanistic / White-Box Model** |
| Measuring only the input and output of the lungs | **Input–Output (Lumped) Model** |
| Tracking pressure at *every point* inside an artery | **Distributed Parameter Model** |

> [!NOTE]
> **Core Takeaway:** A model is never the system itself — it is a *purpose-built abstraction*. Different physiological questions demand different model classes. Choosing the wrong class is the single most common reason for failed model validation in biomedical engineering.

## 1.3 Why Classification Matters in Biomedical Engineering

The classification framework is **not a theoretical exercise**. It directly impacts:
- **Regulatory submissions** (FDA / CDSCO accept specific model classes — e.g., compartmental PK models).
- **Real-time safety** (a real-time ICU monitor needs a *lumped, deterministic* model, not a *distributed, stochastic* one).
- **Personalization** (white-box models can be re-parameterized per patient; black-box ones cannot easily).
- **Hardware choice** (lumped models run on microcontrollers; distributed models need HPC clusters).

---

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Primary Classification Axes

The KTU-recognized primary axes of classification are as follows. Each is described with the underlying logic and its physiological relevance.

### 2.1.1 Physical (Analog) vs Mathematical (Abstract) Models
- **Physical Models:** Tangible replicas (e.g., a silicone lung simulator, a hydraulic cardiovascular mock loop). Used for testing devices, training surgeons, and in-vitro validation.
- **Mathematical Models:** Sets of equations, rules, or logic. The dominant form in modern physiological modelling.

### 2.1.2 Static vs Dynamic Models
- **Static Model:** Output depends only on the *current* input. No memory of past states.
  $$\ y = f(u)$$
- **Dynamic Model:** Output depends on *current and past* inputs/states. Includes derivatives or delays.
  $$\ y(t) = f\!\left(u(t),\; u'(t),\; u(t-\tau),\; \text{state}(t)\right)$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physiological truth:** Almost *every* physiological system is inherently dynamic (heart rhythm, glucose regulation, breathing). Static models are only valid at *steady state*.

### 2.1.3 Steady-State vs Transient Models
- **Steady-State:** All derivatives equal zero; the system has settled.
  $$\ \frac{dx}{dt} = 0$$
- **Transient:** Captures the system's response *en route* to (or away from) equilibrium.

### 2.1.4 Linear vs Nonlinear Models
- **Linear:** Satisfies superposition ($f(a u_1 + b u_2) = a f(u_1) + b f(u_2)$). Easy analytical solutions.
- **Nonlinear:** No superposition. Requires numerical integration. *All physiologically faithful models are nonlinear* because of saturation (e.g., oxygen-hemoglobin dissociation), valves (unidirectional flow), and threshold firing (neurons).

### 2.1.5 Time-Invariant vs Time-Varying
- **Time-Invariant:** Parameters do not change with time.
  $$\ \dot{x} = A x + B u$$
- **Time-Varying:** Parameters drift (e.g., aging arteries, disease progression).
  $$\ \dot{x} = A(t)\, x + B(t)\, u$$

### 2.1.6 Lumped vs Distributed Parameter Models
- **Lumped (0-D):** All variables are functions of *time only*. Space is collapsed into a single node (e.g., a whole organ treated as one compartment).
- **Distributed (1-D, 2-D, 3-D):** Variables are functions of *time AND space*. Requires partial differential equations (PDEs).
  $$\ \frac{\partial P}{\partial t} + \frac{\partial Q}{\partial x} = 0 \quad \text{(1-D blood flow in an artery)}$$

### 2.1.7 Deterministic vs Stochastic Models
- **Deterministic:** Same input always gives the same output. No randomness.
  $$\ x_{n+1} = f(x_n)$$
- **Stochastic:** Includes random terms, useful for modeling biological noise, ion-channel gating, and patient variability.
  $$\ x_{n+1} = f(x_n) + \eta_n, \quad \eta_n \sim \mathcal{N}(0,\sigma^2)$$

### 2.1.8 Black-Box, Grey-Box, White-Box
- **Black-Box (Empirical / Data-Driven):** No internal physiology represented. Pure input-output mapping. Examples: ANN, polynomial regression of heart rate.
- **Grey-Box (Hybrid):** Partial structure is known (e.g., a compartmental skeleton), but parameters are fitted from data.
- **White-Box (Mechanistic / First-Principles):** Built entirely from physical/physiological laws. Examples: Hodgkin–Huxley neuron model, Windkessel cardiovascular model.

### 2.1.9 Continuous-Time vs Discrete-Time vs Discrete-Event
- **Continuous-Time:** State evolves continuously (differential equations).
- **Discrete-Time:** State updated at fixed intervals (difference equations).
  $$\ x[k+1] = A x[k] + B u[k]$$
- **Discrete-Event:** State changes only when events occur (e.g., ECG waveform, neuronal spike train).

### 2.1.10 Scale of Physiological Organization
| Scale | Typical Models |
|---|---|
| Molecular | Molecular dynamics, enzyme kinetics (Michaelis–Menten) |
| Cellular | Hodgkin–Huxley, ion-channel Markov models |
| Tissue | Reaction-diffusion PDEs (cardiac electrophysiology) |
| Organ | Windkessel, respiratory mechanics |
| System | Closed-loop baroreflex, glucose–insulin regulation |
| Whole-body | Pharmacokinetic (PBPK) models |

## 2.2 KTU High-Yield Formula & Concept Sheet

| Concept | Mathematical Form | Physiological Example | Unit / Domain |
|---|---|---|---|
| Static (Algebraic) | $y = f(u)$ | Steady-state lung compliance | Pressure $\div$ Volume |
| Dynamic (ODE) | $\dot{x} = f(x,u)$ | RC circuit of an artery | States in arbitrary units |
| Distributed (PDE) | $\partial P / \partial t = D\,\partial^2 P / \partial x^2$ | Oxygen diffusion in tissue | Pressure, concentration |
| Linear ODE | $\dot{x} = A x + B u$ | Linearized glucose response | State vector |
| Nonlinear ODE | $\dot{V} = -k V^2$ | Turbulent flow approximation | Volume flow |
| Deterministic | $x_{n+1} = f(x_n)$ | Drug infusion schedule | mg/mL |
| Stochastic | $x_{n+1} = f(x_n) + \eta_n$ | Ion-channel noise | Probability density |
| Lumped | $P(t)$ only | Single-compartment PK | mmHg |
| Distributed | $P(x,t)$ | Arterial wave propagation | mmHg at location x |
| Black-box | $y = \text{ANN}(u)$ | Sleep stage classifier from EEG | Label / score |
| White-box | $C \frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{V}{R} = P(t)$ | Linear Windkessel | mL, mmHg, s |
| Steady-state condition | $dx/dt = 0$ | End-tidal CO₂ equilibrium | mmHg, ppm |

> [!NOTE]
> **LaTeX Tip:** In exam scripts, use `\dot{x}` for time derivatives and `\partial` for partial derivatives. Avoid raw ASCII like `dx/dt` — the KTU examiner penalizes sloppy notation.

## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Drug Development (Pharma):** White-box, lumped, deterministic PK/PD models are FDA-accepted for dose justification.
- **Implantable Devices (e.g., Pacemaker):** Need real-time, deterministic, low-order ODE models for closed-loop control.
- **Surgical Simulators (VR/AR):** Use distributed-parameter, nonlinear, real-time models (e.g., SOFA framework).
- **Wearable AI (Smartwatch ECG):** Use black-box or grey-box discrete-time models on edge hardware.
- **Digital Twins of Patients:** Combine white-box organ models with stochastic patient-specific parameter estimation.

---

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Worked Derivation 1: Lumped RC Model of an Artery (Linear, White-Box, 0-D)

**Goal:** Derive the governing ODE for the classic *Windkessel* model of an arterial segment.

**Step 1 — Identify the physical components.**
- A resistor $R$ models the resistance of small peripheral vessels (afterload).
- A capacitor $C$ models the compliance (elasticity) of the aorta.
- An inlet flow $Q_{in}(t)$ is supplied by the heart (left ventricle).
- The outlet flow is $Q_{out}(t)$.

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of volume (state variable = volume $V$ in the aorta).**

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = Q_{in}(t) - Q_{out}(t)
$$

**Step 3 — Convert volume to pressure using the compliance definition.**

$$
C = \frac{dV}{dP} \quad \Rightarrow \quad dV = C\,dP
$$

Therefore, the rate of change of pressure is

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{1}{C}\,\frac{dV}{dt} = \frac{1}{C}\bigl(Q_{in}(t) - Q_{out}(t)\bigr)
$$

**Step 4 — Use Ohm's law (linear hydraulic analogy) for the resistor.**

$$
Q_{out}(t) = \frac{P(t)}{R}
$$

**Step 5 — Substitute back.**

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = \frac{1}{C}\,Q_{in}(t) - \frac{P(t)}{R\,C}
$$

**Step 6 — Rearrange into the canonical first-order linear ODE form.**

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{1}{R\,C}\,P(t) = \frac{1}{C}\,Q_{in}(t)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Classification outcome of this derivation:** The Windkessel model is **lumped (0-D)**, **linear**, **deterministic**, **continuous-time**, **dynamic**, and **white-box (mechanistic)**. This is the canonical KTU illustration of a 2-element Windkessel.

## 3.2 Worked Derivation 2: Time-Constant & Steady-State Response

**Step 1 — Solve the homogeneous part** $Q_{in}(t) = 0$.

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} = -\frac{P}{R\,C}
$$

This is a first-order separable ODE. Separate variables:

$$
\frac{dP}{P} = -\frac{dt}{R\,C}
$$

Integrate both sides:

$$
\ln \vert P \vert = -\frac{t}{R\,C} + K
$$

Exponentiate:

$$
P(t) = P_0\,e^{-t/(R\,C)}
$$

**Step 2 — Identify the time constant.**

$$
\tau = R\,C \quad \text{(seconds)}
$$

**Step 3 — Steady-state response to a constant input** $Q_{in} = Q_0$.

At equilibrium, $dP/dt = 0$, so

$$
0 = \frac{1}{C}\,Q_0 - \frac{P_{ss}}{R\,C} \quad \Rightarrow \quad P_{ss} = R\,Q_0
$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Insight:** The product $R\,C$ has units of *seconds* and is the **physiological time constant**. A young healthy adult has $\tau \approx 1\text{ s}$; a stiffened (arteriosclerotic) aorta has a smaller $C$ and therefore a *faster* pressure decay — which is clinically measurable.

## 3.3 Worked Derivation 3: Linearization of a Nonlinear Static Model (Michaelis–Menten Enzyme Kinetics)

**Step 1 — Start with the nonlinear (white-box) Michaelis–Menten equation.**

$$
v = \frac{V_{max}\,S}{K_m + S}
$$

Here, $v$ is the reaction velocity and $S$ is the substrate concentration.

**Step 2 — Identify the operating point** $(S_0, v_0)$.

$$
v_0 = \frac{V_{max}\,S_0}{K_m + S_0}
$$

**Step 3 — Compute the partial derivative** (slope) at the operating point.

$$
\frac{\partial v}{\partial S}\bigg\vert_{S_0} = \frac{V_{max}\,K_m}{(K_m + S_0)^2}
$$

**Step 4 — Write the first-order Taylor linearization.**

$$
v \approx v_0 + \left.\frac{\partial v}{\partial S}\right\vert_{S_0}\,(S - S_0)
$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why this matters in KTU context:** Linearization is the *gateway* from a nonlinear physiological model to a tractable linear ODE suitable for control engineering analysis (Bode plots, root locus, stability margins).

## 3.4 Analytical Comparison Matrix (KTU Quick Reference)

| Property | Lumped | Distributed | Black-Box | White-Box |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Space dependence | No | Yes | N/A | Optional |
| Math structure | ODE | PDE | None (data-driven) | ODE/PDE |
| Parameter meaning | Physiological | Spatially resolved | None | Physiological |
| Computational cost | Low | High | Low–Medium | Medium–High |
| Data demand | Low | High | Very High | Low–Medium |
| Interpretability | High | High | Low | Very High |
| Personalization | Easy | Hard | Hard | Medium |
| Typical use | Organ-level loop | Wave propagation | ECG/EEG classification | Mechanistic insight |

---

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
# 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

## 4.1 Master Taxonomy of Physiological Models

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A[PHYSIOLOGICAL MODEL] --> B[Physical Analog]
    A --> C[Mathematical Abstract]
    B --> B1[Hydraulic Mock Loop]
    B --> B2[Silicone Organ Phantom]
    C --> D[Static]
    C --> E[Dynamic]
    D --> D1[Steady State]
    E --> E1[Transient]
    E --> E2[Steady State Equilib]
    C --> F[Linear]
    C --> G[Nonlinear]
    F --> F1[Superposition Holds]
    G --> G1[Saturation Valves Thresholds]
    C --> H[Lumped 0D]
    C --> I[Distributed 1D 2D 3D]
    C --> J[Deterministic]
    C --> K[Stochastic]
    C --> L[Black Box]
    C --> M[Grey Box]
    C --> N[White Box]
    C --> O[Continuous Time]
    C --> P[Discrete Time]
    C --> Q[Discrete Event]
    A --> R[Scale of Organization]
    R --> R1[Molecular]
    R --> R2[Cellular]
    R --> R3[Tissue]
    R --> R4[Organ]
    R --> R5[System]
    R --> R6[Whole Body PBPK]
```

## 4.2 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a Closed-Loop Cardiovascular Model

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    A[Left Ventricle Input Q in t] --> B[RC Windkessel Compliance C]
    B --> C[Arterial Pressure P t]
    C --> D[Peripheral Resistance R]
    D --> E[Venous Return Q out t]
    E --> F[Right Heart Pump]
    F --> G[Pulmonary Loop]
    G --> A
    C --> H[Baroreceptor Sensor]
    H --> I[Medullary Control Center]
    I --> J[Sympathetic Vagal Output]
    J --> A
    C --> K[Display Monitor mmHg]
    C --> L[Data Logger for Model Validation]
```

> [!NOTE]
> **Reading the diagram:** The central loop (A → B → C → D → E → F → G → A) is the **white-box mechanistic core** of the cardiovascular system. The block C → H → I → J → A is the **grey-box feedback layer**. Blocks K and L are the **observers / virtual sensors** — they make the model useful clinically.

## 4.3 Decision Flowchart: Choosing the Right Model Class

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    S[Start Physiological Question] --> Q1{Is full physiology known?}
    Q1 -- Yes --> Q2{Is spatial variation critical?}
    Q1 -- No --> B1[Use Black Box ML]
    Q2 -- Yes --> D1[Use Distributed PDE]
    Q2 -- No --> Q3{Is real-time required?}
    Q3 -- Yes --> L1[Use Lumped Linear ODE]
    Q3 -- No --> L2[Use Lumped Nonlinear ODE]
    B1 --> END[Selected Model Class]
    D1 --> END
    L1 --> END
    L2 --> END
```

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Examiner's View:** This decision tree is exactly the kind of structured reasoning expected in a 14-mark descriptive answer. Memorize the question-flow logic, not just the definitions.

---

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
# 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question 1 [KTU University Exam – May 2024, Model Paper]
**Differentiate between lumped and distributed parameter models. Give one physiological example of each.**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

| Aspect | Lumped Parameter Model | Distributed Parameter Model |
|---|---|---|
| Definition | Variables depend only on time | Variables depend on time and space |
| Governing equation | Ordinary differential equation (ODE) | Partial differential equation (PDE) |
| Spatial detail | None — organ treated as a single point | Continuous spatial variation |
| Computational load | Low (analytical or simple numerical) | High (requires FEM / FDM) |
| Physiological example | 2-element Windkessel arterial model | 1-D pulse-wave propagation in aorta |
| Example equation | $C\,\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{P}{R} = Q_{in}(t)$ | $\frac{\partial P}{\partial t} = -c\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} - \frac{P}{R\,C}$ |

*(1 mark for clear definitions, 1 mark for example, 1 mark for contrast in equation form.)*

---

### Question 2 [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Retest]
**What is a stochastic model? Why are stochastic formulations sometimes preferred over deterministic ones in physiological modelling?**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A **stochastic model** is one in which at least one variable or parameter is treated as a *random process*, producing probabilistic — rather than fixed — outputs for a given input. Mathematically, the state update is

$$
x_{n+1} = f(x_n) + \eta_n, \quad \eta_n \sim \mathcal{N}(\mu,\sigma^2)
$$

**Reasons stochastic models are preferred in physiological contexts:**
1. **Biological noise is inherent** — ion-channel gating, neuronal firing, and heart-rate variability are intrinsically random.
2. **Patient variability** — populations show large inter-subject spread; deterministic single-value predictions are clinically unsafe.
3. **Unmeasured inputs** — stochastic disturbance terms absorb the effect of variables not included in the model.
4. **Risk assessment** — stochastic models yield *probability distributions* of outcomes, which is essential for FDA-grade safety claims.

*(1 mark for definition with equation, 2 marks for the four reasons.)*

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — KTU ESE Module Internal Choice

### Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Main Paper]

**(a) Classify physiological models along any FOUR major axes, giving a physiological example and the governing equation for each class. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

The four major classification axes selected are: *(i) static vs dynamic, (ii) linear vs nonlinear, (iii) lumped vs distributed, (iv) deterministic vs stochastic.*

**Axis 1 — Static vs Dynamic**
- *Static:* output depends only on present input. Example: instantaneous lung compliance $V = C \cdot P$ at a fixed breath-hold.
- *Dynamic:* output depends on past and present. Example: arterial pressure $C\,\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{P}{R} = Q_{in}(t)$.

**Axis 2 — Linear vs Nonlinear**
- *Linear:* superposition holds. Example: linearized glucose–insulin response near basal.
- *Nonlinear:* superposition fails. Example: Michaelis–Menten enzyme kinetics $v = \frac{V_{max}\,S}{K_m + S}$, with saturation at high $S$.

**Axis 3 — Lumped vs Distributed**
- *Lumped (0-D):* single ODE. Example: Windkessel model above.
- *Distributed (1-D):* PDE in space. Example: 1-D pressure wave in a long artery

$$
\frac{\partial P}{\partial t} + c\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial x} = -\frac{P}{R\,C}
$$

where $c$ is pulse-wave velocity.

**Axis 4 — Deterministic vs Stochastic**
- *Deterministic:* fixed output for fixed input. Example: scheduled drug infusion PK model.
- *Stochastic:* random term included. Example: ion-channel gating

$$
\frac{dV}{dt} = -\bigl[g_{Na}\,m^3 h (V - V_{Na}) + g_K\,n^4 (V - V_K)\bigr] + \sigma\,\xi(t)
$$

where $\xi(t)$ is white noise.

*(1.5 marks per axis, 1 mark for example physiology, 0.5 mark for the equation — for a total of 4 × 1.75 ≈ 7 marks.)*

---

**(b) With a neat block diagram, describe the closed-loop cardiovascular system model. Identify which blocks are white-box, grey-box, and black-box. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Block Diagram (textual — draw the same in the exam):**

```
Left Ventricle (Q_in)  →  Aortic Compliance C  →  Arterial Pressure P(t)
            ↑                                                    ↓
  Sympathetic/Vagal Out ←  Baroreflex Controller  ←  Baroreceptor Sensor
            ↑                                                    ↓
            └────────────  Peripheral Resistance R  ←────────────┘
                                                                  ↓
                                              Venous Return → Right Heart
```

**Block-by-Block Classification:**

| Block | Class | Justification |
|---|---|---|
| Left Ventricle (elastance) | **White-box** | First-principles time-varying elastance $E(t)$ |
| Aortic compliance C | **White-box** | $C = dV/dP$ is a physical property of tissue |
| Peripheral resistance R | **Grey-box** | Poiseuille's law gives structure; R is patient-fitted |
| Baroreceptor sensor | **White-box** | Known stretch–firing relationship (linear in operating range) |
| Baroreflex controller | **Grey-box** | Known medullary structure; gains fitted from data |
| Heart-rate variability noise | **Stochastic** | Biological noise term $\eta(t)$ |
| Full closed-loop simulator | **Hybrid white/grey/black** | Mixture of all three |

**Governing equation of the central loop (derived form):**

$$
C\,\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{P}{R} = Q_{in}(t) - f_{baro}(P)
$$

where $f_{baro}(\cdot)$ is the baroreflex feedback function.

*(3 marks for diagram, 2 marks for classification table, 2 marks for the equation and narrative.)*

---

### Question B (14 Marks) — Alternative Choice [KTU University Exam – July 2024, Supplementary]

**(a) Derive the governing equation of the 2-element Windkessel model. Classify the resulting model along the five primary axes. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Derivation (5 steps — full marks require all 5):*

**Step 1:** Volume balance in the aorta — $\frac{dV}{dt} = Q_{in} - Q_{out}$.

**Step 2:** Compliance definition — $C = dV/dP$, so $\frac{dV}{dt} = C\,\frac{dP}{dt}$.

**Step 3:** Resistor relation — $Q_{out} = P/R$.

**Step 4:** Substitute and rearrange:

$$
C\,\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{P}{R} = Q_{in}(t)
$$

**Step 5:** Canonical form:

$$
\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{1}{RC}\,P = \frac{1}{C}\,Q_{in}(t)
$$

with time constant $\tau = RC$ and steady-state $P_{ss} = R\,Q_0$.

**Five-axis classification:**

| Axis | Class |
|---|---|
| Physical vs Mathematical | Mathematical |
| Static vs Dynamic | Dynamic (first-order ODE) |
| Linear vs Nonlinear | Linear |
| Lumped vs Distributed | Lumped (0-D) |
| Deterministic vs Stochastic | Deterministic |
| Black/Grey/White | White-box (mechanistic) |

*(3 marks for full derivation, 2 marks for the five-axis table, 2 marks for the steady-state and time-constant interpretation.)*

---

**(b) Compare compartmental, black-box, and distributed parameter models for a real physiological application (e.g., insulin–glucose regulation). Justify which one you would choose for (i) an ICU real-time controller and (ii) a population-level epidemiological study. (7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Application:** Insulin–glucose regulation in diabetes.

| Model Class | Structure | Strength | Weakness |
|---|---|---|---|
| **Compartmental (lumped, white-box)** | 1, 2, or 3 ODEs for plasma glucose, insulin, gut absorption | Physiologically interpretable; parameters have units | Oversimplifies tissue heterogeneity |
| **Black-box (e.g., RNN, ARX)** | Pure data-driven input → output mapping | Captures complex nonlinearity | No physiological meaning; needs huge datasets |
| **Distributed PDE** | Glucose + insulin concentration fields in tissue | Spatially accurate | Computationally heavy; rarely needed clinically |

**For (i) ICU real-time artificial pancreas (≈1 s control loop):**
- **Choose: Compartmental grey-box model** — e.g., the Bergman minimal model extended with a meal compartment.
- **Reason:** Runs in milliseconds on a microcontroller; parameters can be re-identified per patient; FDA-accepted (e.g., UVA/Padova simulator).

**For (ii) Population-level epidemiological study of type-2 diabetes:**
- **Choose: Compartmental ODE at population scale + stochastic variability term.**
- **Reason:** Captures *average* dynamics plus inter-patient distribution; integrates with public-health ODE/SDE frameworks.

*(2 marks for the comparison table, 2 marks for ICU choice + justification, 2 marks for population choice + justification, 1 mark for stating the underlying equations.)*

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark Deductions:**
> 1. **Confusing "static" with "steady-state".** Static means *no dynamics at all* (algebraic). Steady-state means *dynamics exist but derivatives are zero*. Many students swap these and lose 1–2 marks per occurrence.
> 2. **Writing "the model is nonlinear" without naming the nonlinearity** (saturation, threshold, multiplicative coupling). Always *name* it.
> 3. **Forgetting units** in the time-constant derivation $\tau = R\,C$. KTU examiners deduct 0.5 marks for missing SI units.
> 4. **Drawing compartmental boxes without arrows** — arrows are mandatory; static boxes do not constitute a valid compartmental model in KTU evaluation.
> 5. **Confusing discrete-event with discrete-time.** Discrete-event is *event-triggered* (e.g., an ECG R-peak); discrete-time is *clock-triggered* (e.g., sample at 1 Hz). Examiners frequently test this distinction.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!NOTE]
> **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist (Module 1 — Classification):**

- **A model is an abstraction**, never the real system; classification precedes selection.
- **Five primary axes:** physical↔mathematical, static↔dynamic, linear↔nonlinear, lumped↔distributed, deterministic↔stochastic.
- **Three interpretability tiers:** black-box (data-driven), grey-box (hybrid), white-box (mechanistic).
- **Three time formulations:** continuous-time (ODE/PDE), discrete-time (difference equation), discrete-event (event-driven).
- **Physiological systems are inherently** dynamic, nonlinear, time-varying, and partially stochastic — pure linear or steady-state models are *approximations*, not truths.
- **The Windkessel model** is the canonical KTU illustration of a lumped, linear, white-box, dynamic, deterministic physiological model.
- **Time constant** $\tau = R\,C$ governs the *speed* of arterial pressure decay; clinically diagnostic of arterial stiffness.
- **Michaelis–Menten** is the canonical example of a nonlinear static model; its linearization around an operating point is a common exam derivation.
- **Compartmental models** = lumped + white/grey-box + deterministic; used in PK/PD and ICU control.
- **Distributed PDE models** are reserved for *spatial* phenomena — wave propagation, tissue oxygen diffusion, electrophysiology.
- **Choose the model class by the question**, not the other way round — KTU examiners test this *decision-making*, not just definitions.
- **In exam answers, always state:** (i) the axis, (ii) the physiological example, (iii) the governing equation, and (iv) the units.
- **LaTeX conventions in KTU scripts:** use `\dot{x}` for $\dot{x}$, `\partial` for $\partial$, `\tau` for $\tau$, `\infty` for $\infty$, `\geq` for $\geq$, `\sim` for $\sim$.
- **Avoid the trap of "more detail = better model"** — a 3-ODE grey-box model correctly identified will outperform a 50-state PDE in a real clinical setting.

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Formulation of mathematical model
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT864 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module Module 1 > - **Topic:** Formulation of mathematical model ## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ### 1.1 Formal Definition (KTU 2024 Syllabus Terminology) A **mathematical model** is a description of a system using mathematical concepts and language. The **formulation of a mathematical model** is the systematic process of translating a real-world physical, chemical, or biological phenomenon into a set of mathematical equations (algebraic, differential, integral, or stochastic) that quantitatively represents the relationships among the variables governing the system. In the context of **physiological systems**, formulation involves representing the human body — or a part of it (e.g., the cardiovascular system, renal system, endocrine system) — through mathematical relations derived from the laws of physics, chemistry, and biology. The goal is to predict, analyze, and control physiological behavior under both normal and pathological conditions. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 High-Yield Definition:** A mathematical model is a precise, unambiguous representation of a system expressed in mathematical terms, derived from first principles (conservation laws) and/or empirical data, used to simulate, predict, and understand the dynamic behavior of physiological processes. ### 1.2 Intuitive Analogy (Plain-English Understanding) > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Water Tank Model"** > > Imagine a leaking water tank with an inlet pipe. You want to predict the water level at any future time without actually observing it. To do this, you would: > 1. Identify the **variables** (inflow rate, outflow rate, tank volume, water level). > 2. Apply a **physical law** (conservation of mass: *what goes in must come out or accumulate*). > 3. Write an **equation** (Rate of change of water = Inflow − Outflow). > 4. **Solve** the equation for the unknown (water level as a function of time). > > This entire process — from observing a real tank to writing the final equation — is the **formulation of a mathematical model**. In physiology, the "tank" is replaced by organs (heart, lungs, kidneys) and the "water" is replaced by blood, oxygen, glucose, or drugs. ### 1.3 Purpose of Mathematical Modeling in Physiology | **Engineering/Scientific Purpose** | **Physiological Application** | |---|---| | Predict system response to inputs | Predict blood glucose response to a meal | | Understand cause–effect relationships | Understand how insulin deficiency causes diabetes | | Optimize system performance | Optimize drug dosage regimen for a patient | | Test hypotheses non-invasively | Simulate cardiac output under varying heart rates | | Replace expensive/dangerous experiments | Simulate drug toxicity before clinical trials | > [!IMPORTANT] > **Syllabus Highlight:** A physiological system is treated as a **dynamical system** whose state evolves with time. The model formulation must capture the **structure** (anatomical connections), **function** (physiological laws), and **dynamics** (time evolution) of the system. ## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ### 2.1 The Systematic Steps of Model Formulation The formulation of any mathematical model follows a well-defined sequence of logical steps. Mastering these steps is **critical for KTU 2024 Scheme examinations**, as questions on the modeling methodology are frequently asked. **Step 1 — Problem Definition and System Identification** - Clearly state the physiological problem (e.g., "Determine drug concentration in blood plasma over time"). - Identify the **system boundaries** — what is *inside* the model and what is *outside* (environment). - Define the **inputs** (stimuli, drug dose, food intake) and **outputs** (responses, concentrations, pressures). **Step 2 — Identification of Variables and Parameters** - **State variables** ($x_1, x_2, \ldots, x_n$): Quantities that describe the system's current state (e.g., blood volume, glucose concentration). - **Parameters** ($k_1, k_2, \ldots$): Constants that characterize the system (e.g., elimination rate constant, vessel compliance). - **Inputs** ($u_1, u_2, \ldots$): External stimuli applied to the system. - **Outputs** ($y_1, y_2, \ldots$): Measurable responses of the system. **Step 3 — Assumptions and Simplifications** - Make the problem mathematically tractable by assuming: - Lumped (well-mixed) compartments instead of spatial distributions. - Constant body temperature, pH, and other environmental factors. - Linearity (when valid) for superposition and analytical solutions. - Instantaneous mixing within compartments. - **Every assumption must be physically justified** — examiners award marks for justification. **Step 4 — Application of Governing Laws** - Apply the relevant **conservation laws** (mass, energy, momentum) and **constitutive relations** (e.g., Ohm's law for fluid flow, Fick's law for diffusion, Hooke's law for elasticity). - The most fundamental law in physiological modeling is the **Conservation of Mass**: $$\frac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{gen}} - \dot{m}_{\text{cons}}$$ where $M$ is the mass of the substance in the compartment, and the right-hand side represents the net rate of accumulation. **Step 5 — Mathematical Representation** - Express the governing laws as **differential equations**, **algebraic equations**, or **integral equations**. - A general **lumped-parameter dynamic model** is written in state-space form: $$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = f\bigl(\mathbf{x}(t),\, \mathbf{u}(t),\, t\bigr)$$ $$\mathbf{y}(t) = g\bigl(\mathbf{x}(t),\, \mathbf{u}(t),\, t\bigr)$$ **Step 6 — Solution and Analysis** - Solve the equations analytically (for linear systems) or numerically (for nonlinear systems). - Analyze stability, sensitivity, and steady-state behavior. **Step 7 — Validation and Refinement** - Compare model predictions with experimental/clinical data. - Refine assumptions, parameters, or structure until the model adequately represents reality. ### 2.2 Classification of Mathematical Models | **Classification Criterion** | **Model Type** | **Physiological Example** | |---|---|---| | Time dependence | Static (steady-state) vs. Dynamic (time-varying) | Steady-state cardiac output vs. Transient response to exercise | | Spatial dependence | Lumped (0-D) vs. Distributed (1-D, 2-D, 3-D) | Whole-organ glucose model vs. Spatially varying oxygen in tissue | | Linearity | Linear vs. Nonlinear | Linear drug pharmacokinetics vs. Nonlinear enzyme kinetics (Michaelis–Menten) | | Determinism | Deterministic vs. Stochastic | Deterministic glucose model vs. Stochastic ion channel gating | | Knowledge basis | White-box (mechanistic) vs. Black-box (empirical) vs. Grey-box (hybrid) | First-principles cardiovascular model vs. Neural network ECG classifier | | Number of compartments | Single-compartment vs. Multi-compartment | One-compartment drug model vs. Two-compartment pharmacokinetic model | | Domain | Continuous-time vs. Discrete-time | Continuous drug infusion vs. Oral dosing at discrete intervals | ### 2.3 The Conservation of Mass — The Backbone of Physiological Modeling > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU High-Yield Principle:** Almost every physiological model (drug kinetics, thermal regulation, fluid balance, gas exchange) is built upon the **conservation of mass** applied to one or more compartments. For a **single well-mixed compartment** with volume $V$ and concentration $C$ of a substance: $$V \frac{dC}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{gen}} - \dot{m}_{\text{cons}}$$ If the volume $V$ is **constant** and there is **no generation or consumption** within the compartment: $$\frac{dC}{dt} = \frac{1}{V}\bigl(\dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}}\bigr)$$ ### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | **Equation / Concept** | **Mathematical Form** | **Variables & Units** | **Physiological Meaning** | |---|---|---|---| | Conservation of Mass (general) | $\frac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{gen}} - \dot{m}_{\text{cons}}$ | $M$ [mg], $\dot{m}$ [mg/min] | Net rate of accumulation in a compartment | | Single Compartment, Constant Volume | $V\frac{dC}{dt} = F_{\text{in}}C_{\text{in}} - F_{\text{out}}C$ | $V$ [L], $C$ [mg/L], $F$ [L/min] | Drug or metabolite balance | | First-Order Elimination | $\frac{dC}{dt} = -kC$ | $k$ [min⁻¹] | Concentration decays exponentially | | First-Order Solution | $C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$ | $C_0$ [mg/L] | Concentration at time $t$ | | Half-Life | $t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}$ | $t_{1/2}$ [min] | Time for concentration to halve | | Two-Compartment Model (general) | $\frac{dC_1}{dt} = -k_{12}C_1 + k_{21}C_2 - k_e C_1$
$\frac{dC_2}{dt} = k_{12}C_1 - k_{21}C_2$ | $k_{ij}$ [min⁻¹] | Inter-compartmental transfer rates | | Steady-State Condition | $\frac{dC}{dt} = 0 \Rightarrow C_{\text{ss}} = \frac{\text{Input Rate}}{\text{Clearance}}$ | $C_{\text{ss}}$ [mg/L] | Concentration when input = output | | Michaelis–Menten Kinetics | $v = \frac{V_{\max}\, C}{K_m + C}$ | $V_{\max}$ [mg/min], $K_m$ [mg/L] | Nonlinear enzyme/metabolic rate | | Fick's Law of Diffusion | $J = -D \frac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | $J$ [mol/(m²·s)], $D$ [m²/s] | Diffusive flux across a membrane | | Ohm's Law Analogy (Fluid Flow) | $Q = \frac{\Delta P}{R}$ | $Q$ [mL/s], $\Delta P$ [mmHg], $R$ [mmHg·s/mL] | Blood flow through a vascular bed | | Linear State-Space Model | $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = A\mathbf{x} + B\mathbf{u}$
$\mathbf{y} = C\mathbf{x} + D\mathbf{u}$ | $A, B, C, D$ are matrices | Compact representation of linear systems | > [!NOTE] > **Engineering Utility:** The compartmental modeling framework developed here is directly used in **pharmacokinetic–pharmacodynamic (PK/PD) modeling** by the pharmaceutical industry to design drug dosing schedules, in **artificial organ design** (e.g., dialyzers, heart-lung machines), and in **clinical decision support systems** for personalized medicine. ## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ### 3.1 Derivation 1: Single-Compartment Drug Model with First-Order Elimination **Problem Statement:** A patient is given an intravenous bolus injection of a drug at time $t=0$. The initial plasma concentration is $C_0$ [mg/L]. The drug is eliminated from the body at a rate proportional to its current concentration (first-order kinetics). Formulate the mathematical model and derive the concentration profile $C(t)$. **Step 1 — Define the system and variables:** - System: The entire body treated as a single, well-mixed compartment. - State variable: $C(t)$ = drug concentration in plasma [mg/L]. - Parameter: $k$ = first-order elimination rate constant [min⁻¹]. - Assumption: Instantaneous mixing; constant volume of distribution $V_d$. **Step 2 — Apply the Conservation of Mass:** $$\frac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}} + \dot{m}_{\text{gen}} - \dot{m}_{\text{cons}}$$ Since the drug is administered as a single bolus (no continuous infusion), $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = 0$. There is no endogenous generation, so $\dot{m}_{\text{gen}} = 0$. The only output is elimination, and no consumption occurs within the compartment in the chemical sense, so $\dot{m}_{\text{cons}} = 0$ (elimination is treated as outflow). Thus: $$\frac{dM}{dt} = - \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$$ **Step 3 — Express mass and outflow in terms of concentration:** The mass of drug in the body is $M = V_d \cdot C$. The rate of elimination is $\dot{m}_{\text{out}} = k \cdot C \cdot V_d$ (first-order kinetics: rate $\propto$ amount present). Substituting: $$\frac{d(V_d C)}{dt} = - k V_d C$$ Since $V_d$ is constant: $$V_d \frac{dC}{dt} = - k V_d C$$ **Step 4 — Simplify:** $$\frac{dC}{dt} = -k C$$ **Step 5 — Solve the first-order linear ODE:** Separate variables: $$\frac{dC}{C} = -k\, dt$$ Integrate both sides from $t=0$ (where $C = C_0$) to time $t$ (where $C = C(t)$): $$\int_{C_0}^{C(t)} \frac{dC}{C} = -k \int_{0}^{t} dt$$ $$\ln C(t) - \ln C_0 = -kt$$ $$\ln \left(\frac{C(t)}{C_0}\right) = -kt$$ **Step 6 — Final solution:** $$\boxed{C(t) = C_0\, e^{-kt}}$$ **Step 7 — Derive the half-life:** The half-life $t_{1/2}$ is the time at which $C(t_{1/2}) = \frac{C_0}{2}$: $$\frac{C_0}{2} = C_0 e^{-k t_{1/2}}$$ $$\frac{1}{2} = e^{-k t_{1/2}}$$ $$\ln\left(\frac{1}{2}\right) = -k t_{1/2}$$ $$-\ln 2 = -k t_{1/2}$$ $$\boxed{t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{k}}$$ --- ### 3.2 Derivation 2: Two-Compartment Model (Central and Peripheral) **Problem Statement:** A drug distributes between a central compartment (blood plasma and well-perfused organs) and a peripheral compartment (fat, muscle, poorly perfused tissues). Formulate the model. **Step 1 — Define compartments and parameters:** - Compartment 1 (Central): Amount of drug $X_1$ [mg], volume $V_1$ [L]. - Compartment 2 (Peripheral): Amount of drug $X_2$ [mg], volume $V_2$ [L]. - $k_{12}$: Transfer rate constant from Compartment 1 → Compartment 2 [min⁻¹]. - $k_{21}$: Transfer rate constant from Compartment 2 → Compartment 1 [min⁻¹]. - $k_e$: Elimination rate constant from Compartment 1 [min⁻¹]. **Step 2 — Apply Conservation of Mass to Compartment 1:** Rate of accumulation in Compartment 1 = Rate in from Compartment 2 − Rate out to Compartment 2 − Rate of elimination. $$\frac{dX_1}{dt} = k_{21} X_2 - k_{12} X_1 - k_e X_1$$ **Step 3 — Apply Conservation of Mass to Compartment 2:** Rate of accumulation in Compartment 2 = Rate in from Compartment 1 − Rate out to Compartment 1. $$\frac{dX_2}{dt} = k_{12} X_1 - k_{21} X_2$$ **Step 4 — Write the coupled system in matrix (state-space) form:** $$\begin{aligned} \frac{d}{dt} \begin{bmatrix} X_1 \\ X_2 \end{bmatrix} &= \begin{bmatrix} -(k_{12} + k_e) & k_{21} \\ k_{12} & -k_{21} \end{bmatrix} \begin{bmatrix} X_1 \\ X_2 \end{bmatrix} \end{aligned}$$ **Step 5 — Steady-state analysis:** At steady state, $\frac{dX_1}{dt} = \frac{dX_2}{dt} = 0$. Setting the equations to zero: $$0 = k_{21} X_2^{ss} - (k_{12} + k_e) X_1^{ss}$$ $$0 = k_{12} X_1^{ss} - k_{21} X_2^{ss}$$ From the second equation: $X_2^{ss} = \frac{k_{12}}{k_{21}} X_1^{ss}$. Substituting into the first equation: $$0 = k_{21} \cdot \frac{k_{12}}{k_{21}} X_1^{ss} - (k_{12} + k_e) X_1^{ss} = k_{12} X_1^{ss} - (k_{12} + k_e) X_1^{ss} = -k_e X_1^{ss}$$ This gives $X_1^{ss} = 0$ (and hence $X_2^{ss} = 0$), which makes physical sense: **with no continuous infusion, the drug eventually disappears from the body.** --- ### 3.3 Derivation 3: Steady-State Concentration under Continuous Infusion **Problem Statement:** A drug is administered by continuous intravenous infusion at a constant rate $R$ [mg/min]. The drug follows first-order elimination kinetics. Derive the steady-state plasma concentration. **Step 1 — Model formulation:** $$\frac{dM}{dt} = R - k M$$ **Step 2 — Solve the linear ODE:** Separate variables (using integrating factor or direct method): $$\frac{dM}{dt} + k M = R$$ The general solution is the sum of the homogeneous and particular solutions. The homogeneous solution is $M_h(t) = A e^{-kt}$. A particular (steady-state) solution is $M_p = \frac{R}{k}$. Thus: $$M(t) = A e^{-kt} + \frac{R}{k}$$ **Step 3 — Apply the initial condition $M(0) = 0$ (no drug initially):** $$0 = A + \frac{R}{k} \implies A = -\frac{R}{k}$$ **Step 4 — Final solution:** $$\boxed{M(t) = \frac{R}{k}\left(1 - e^{-kt}\right)}$$ **Step 5 — Steady-state limit (as $t \to \infty$):** $$\boxed{M_{ss} = \frac{R}{k}, \quad C_{ss} = \frac{R}{k\, V_d}}$$ **Interpretation:** The drug accumulates in the body until the rate of elimination ($k \cdot M_{ss}$) exactly equals the rate of infusion ($R$). The system reaches **~98% of steady state in approximately 4 half-lives** ($4 \times t_{1/2}$). --- ### 3.4 Analytical Matrix: Comparison of Model Types | **Feature** | **Static (Algebraic)** | **Dynamic Lumped (ODE)** | **Dynamic Distributed (PDE)** | |---|---|---|---| | Independent variable | None (or parameters only) | Time $t$ | Time $t$ and space $x$ | | Typical equation | $y = f(x)$ | $\frac{dy}{dt} = f(y, u, t)$ | $\frac{\partial y}{\partial t} = D \frac{\partial^2 y}{\partial x^2}$ | | Solution technique | Direct substitution | Analytical / Numerical ODE methods (Euler, RK4) | Finite difference / Finite element | | Physiological example | Steady-state oxygen consumption | Blood glucose after a meal | Oxygen diffusion in tissue slab | | Complexity | Low | Moderate | High | ## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ### 4.1 General Model Formulation Workflow ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Identify Real-World Physiological Problem] --> B[Define System Boundaries and Variables] B --> C[List All Assumptions and Simplifications] C --> D[Apply Conservation Laws: Mass, Energy, Momentum] D --> E[Derive Governing Differential or Algebraic Equations] E --> F[Solve Equations: Analytical or Numerical] F --> G[Validate Predictions Against Experimental Data] G --> H{Model Valid?} H -- Yes --> I[Deploy Model for Prediction and Control] H -- No --> J[Refine Assumptions or Add Complexity] J --> D ``` ### 4.2 Two-Compartment Pharmacokinetic Model Block Diagram ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Central Compartment: Blood Plasma] -- k12 --> B[Peripheral Compartment: Tissues] B -- k21 --> A A -- ke out of body --> C[Elimination: Liver or Kidney] D[External Source: IV Bolus or Infusion] --> A ``` ### 4.3 Block-Level Functional Architecture of a General Physiological Model ```mermaid flowchart TD subgraph INPUT_BLOCK IN1[Input Stimulus u1: Drug Dose] IN2[Input Stimulus u2: Meal Intake] IN3[Input Stimulus u3: Exercise] end subgraph SYSTEM_BLOCK S1[State Variables: Concentrations, Volumes, Pressures] S2[Governing Equations: Conservation Laws] S3[Parameters: Rate Constants, Compliances, Resistances] S1 --- S2 S2 --- S3 S3 --- S1 end subgraph OUTPUT_BLOCK OUT1[Output y1: Plasma Concentration] OUT2[Output y2: Blood Pressure] OUT3[Output y3: Cardiac Output] end IN1 --> S1 IN2 --> S1 IN3 --> S1 S2 --> OUT1 S2 --> OUT2 S2 --> OUT3 ``` ### 4.4 Compartmental Model with State-Space Representation ```mermaid flowchart LR U[Input Vector u] --> SUM[Input Matrix B multiplication] X[State Vector x] --> A[System Matrix A] A --> XDOT[Time Derivative x dot] SUM --> XDOT XDOT --> X X --> C[Output Matrix C] C --> Y[Output Vector y] U --> D[Feedforward Matrix D] D --> Y ``` ### 4.5 Model Classification Tree ```mermaid flowchart TD M[Mathematical Model] --> M1[Static vs Dynamic] M --> M2[Lumped vs Distributed] M --> M3[Linear vs Nonlinear] M --> M4[Deterministic vs Stochastic] M1 --> M1A[Static: Algebraic Equations] M1 --> M1B[Dynamic: ODE or PDE] M2 --> M2A[Lumped: Compartment Model] M2 --> M2B[Distributed: Spatial Model] M3 --> M3A[Linear: Superposition Holds] M3 --> M3B[Nonlinear: ODE with Products or Powers] M4 --> M4A[Deterministic: No Random Inputs] M4 --> M4B[Stochastic: Random Variables Present] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set A]** Define a mathematical model. List any four essential steps involved in the formulation of a mathematical model of a physiological system. **Model Answer:** A **mathematical model** is a representation of a real-world system using mathematical equations and relationships that quantitatively describe the system's behavior under specified conditions. **Four essential steps in the formulation of a physiological model:** 1. **Problem Definition:** Clearly identify the physiological system to be modeled and the specific question to be answered (e.g., drug concentration profile, blood pressure response). 2. **Assumption Making:** Simplify the system by assuming well-mixed compartments, constant temperature, linearity where valid, and neglecting higher-order effects. 3. **Application of Governing Laws:** Apply conservation of mass, energy, or momentum along with constitutive relations (Fick's law, Ohm's analogy, Michaelis–Menten kinetics) to derive governing equations. 4. **Solution and Validation:** Solve the equations analytically or numerically, then compare predictions with experimental/clinical data to validate the model. --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** What is the difference between a **lumped-parameter** model and a **distributed-parameter** model? Give one physiological example of each. **Model Answer:** | **Aspect** | **Lumped-Parameter Model** | **Distributed-Parameter Model** | |---|---|---| | Spatial variation | Neglected (system treated as uniform) | Explicitly included as a function of space | | Mathematical form | Ordinary Differential Equations (ODEs) | Partial Differential Equations (PDEs) | | Complexity | Low to moderate | High; requires numerical methods | | **Physiological Example** | One-compartment drug model: drug concentration assumed uniform throughout plasma | Oxygen diffusion in a tissue slab: concentration varies with depth $x$ and time $t$, described by Fick's second law | --- ### 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks Each) — Module Internal Choice --- #### **Question A (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – June 2024, Model Paper Set A]** **(a)** Derive the mathematical model for a **single-compartment system** with first-order elimination, starting from the conservation of mass principle. Clearly state all assumptions. (7 marks) **(b)** A patient receives an IV bolus of a drug. The initial plasma concentration is 20 mg/L and the elimination rate constant is $k = 0.05$ min⁻¹. Calculate the **(i)** drug concentration after 30 minutes, **(ii)** the half-life of the drug, and **(iii)** the time required to reach 2 mg/L. (7 marks) **Model Solution:** **(a) Derivation of Single-Compartment Model:** 1. **Assumptions:** - The body is treated as a single, well-mixed compartment of constant volume $V_d$. - Drug is administered as an instantaneous bolus at $t = 0$. - Elimination follows first-order kinetics: rate of elimination $\propto$ concentration. - No endogenous generation or additional intake of the drug. 2. **Conservation of Mass:** $$\frac{dM}{dt} = \dot{m}_{\text{in}} - \dot{m}_{\text{out}}$$ With no input, $\dot{m}_{\text{in}} = 0$, and $\dot{m}_{\text{out}} = kM$ (first-order): $$\frac{dM}{dt} = -kM$$ 3. **Convert to concentration:** $M = V_d C$, $V_d$ constant: $$V_d \frac{dC}{dt} = -k V_d C \implies \frac{dC}{dt} = -kC$$ 4. **Solution with initial condition $C(0) = C_0$:** $$C(t) = C_0 e^{-kt}$$ --- **(b) Numerical Calculations:** Given: $C_0 = 20$ mg/L, $k = 0.05$ min⁻¹. **(i) Concentration at $t = 30$ min:** $$C(30) = 20 \times e^{-0.05 \times 30} = 20 \times e^{-1.5} = 20 \times 0.2231 = 4.462 \text{ mg/L}$$ **(ii) Half-life:** $$t_{1/2} = \frac{\ln 2}{k} = \frac{0.693}{0.05} = 13.86 \text{ min}$$ **(iii) Time to reach $C = 2$ mg/L:** $$2 = 20 \times e^{-0.05\, t}$$ $$e^{-0.05\, t} = \frac{2}{20} = 0.1$$ $$-0.05\, t = \ln(0.1) = -2.3026$$ $$t = \frac{2.3026}{0.05} = 46.05 \text{ min}$$ --- #### **Question B (14 Marks) [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023, Model Paper Set B]** **(a)** Explain the general **conservation of mass equation** for a well-mixed compartment with inflow, outflow, generation, and consumption. Define all terms and state the units. Derive the steady-state concentration when the inflow is constant and outflow is proportional to concentration. (7 marks) **(b)** A drug is infused continuously at a rate of 10 mg/min into a patient. The elimination rate constant is $k = 0.1$ min⁻¹ and the volume of distribution is $V_d = 40$ L. Find: **(i)** the steady-state plasma concentration, **(ii)** the time to reach 95% of steady state, and **(iii)** the loading dose required to achieve the steady-state concentration immediately.
