or numerical method"] ``` ## SECTION 5 — KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### 5.1 Part A — Short-Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) --- **Q1.** **[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model Question Bank]** **Differentiate between linear and nonlinear physiological models. Give two physiological examples where linear modeling fails.** **Model Answer (3 marks — full-credit key):** A linear model obeys the **superposition principle**: the response to a sum of inputs equals the sum of the individual responses, and scaling an input scales the output proportionally, expressed as $f(\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2) = \alpha f(x_1) + \beta f(x_2)$. A nonlinear model **violates** this principle; its output is not proportional to its input and may exhibit **saturation**, **threshold**, **multiplication between states**, or **higher-order polynomial** relations. **Physiological examples where linear modeling fails:** 1. **Enzyme kinetics** (Michaelis–Menten): the reaction rate saturates at high substrate concentration; a linear model would predict an unrealistically unbounded rate. 2. **Hodgkin–Huxley neuron:** the membrane current depends on *state-variable-dependent* conductances (e.g., $g_{Na} m^3 h$) — a product of variables, which is intrinsically nonlinear. *(1 mark for definition of linear, 1 mark for definition of nonlinear, 1 mark for the two examples with brief justification.)* --- **Q2.** **[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model Question Bank]** **What is the Michaelis constant $K_m$? State its physical significance in enzyme kinetics.** **Model Answer (3 marks — full-credit key):** From the derivation in Section 3.1: $$K_m \equiv \frac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}$$ **Physical significance (2 key points):** 1. **Substrate concentration at half-maximum velocity:** When the substrate concentration $[S] = K_m$, the Michaelis–Menten equation gives $v = \dfrac{V_m \, K_m}{K_m + K_m} = \dfrac{V_m}{2}$. Thus $K_m$ is the substrate concentration at which the reaction proceeds at *half its maximum rate*. 2. **Inverse measure of enzyme–substrate affinity:** A *low* $K_m$ indicates *high* affinity (the enzyme reaches half-saturation at low $[S]$); a *high* $K_m$ indicates *low* affinity. *(1 mark for the equation, 1 mark for half-velocity interpretation, 1 mark for affinity interpretation.)* --- ### 5.2 Part B — Full-Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, Module Internal Choice) --- #### **QUESTION A (14 Marks) — Nonlinear Enzyme Kinetics with Linearization** **[KTU University Exam — Sample Model Paper 2024 Scheme]** **(a)** Derive the Michaelis–Menten equation for enzyme kinetics from the elementary reaction $E + S \rightleftharpoons ES \rightarrow E + P$ using the Quasi-Steady-State Approximation. Clearly state the assumptions. **(7 marks)** **(b)** For a glucose-transport process in a cell, the uptake rate follows $v(S) = \dfrac{V_m S}{K_m + S}$ with $V_m = 10$ mmol/(L·s) and $K_m = 5$ mmol/L. Linearize the model around $S_0 = 5$ mmol/L and find the **linearized uptake equation** valid for small perturbations $\delta S$ around $S_0$. Comment on the **range of validity** of this linearization. **(7 marks)** --- **MODEL SOLUTION:** **(a) — 7 marks** **Assumptions (1 mark):** 1. The total enzyme concentration $E_0$ is conserved. 2. The intermediate complex $[ES]$ is in quasi-steady-state (QSSA): $d[ES]/dt \approx 0$. 3. The reaction occurs in a well-mixed compartment (no spatial gradients). 4. Only one substrate binding site per enzyme (no cooperativity at this stage). **Derivation (5 marks):** The derivation follows exactly the seven steps from Section 3.1 of these notes. Elementary steps: $$\frac{d[S]}{dt} = -k_1 [E][S] + k_{-1}[ES]$$ $$\frac{d[ES]}{dt} = k_1 [E][S] - (k_{-1} + k_2)[ES] = 0 \quad \text{(QSSA)}$$ $$\frac{d[P]}{dt} = k_2 [ES]$$ Solving the QSSA equation: $$[ES] = \frac{[E][S]}{K_m}, \quad K_m = \frac{k_{-1} + k_2}{k_1}$$ Enzyme conservation: $[E_0] = [E] + [ES] \Rightarrow [E] = [E_0] - [ES]$ Substituting: $$[ES] = \frac{([E_0] - [ES])[S]}{K_m}$$ $$[ES](K_m + [S]) = [E_0][S]$$ $$[ES] = \frac{[E_0][S]}{K_m + [S]}$$ With $V_m = k_2 [E_0]$: $$\boxed{\,v = \frac{V_m [S]}{K_m + [S]}\,}$$ Product formation rate: $v = k_2 [ES] = \dfrac{k_2 [E_0][S]}{K_m + [S]} = \dfrac{V_m [S]}{K_m + [S]}$. **Comment on nonlinearity (1 mark):** The presence of the $[S]$ term in the denominator makes this intrinsically nonlinear in $[S]$. Linearization is only valid locally. --- **(b) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Evaluate $v$ at the operating point** (1 mark): $$v_0 = v(S_0 = 5) = \frac{10 \times 5}{5 + 5} = \frac{50}{10} = 5 \text{ mmol/(L·s)}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the derivative** $dv/dS$ (1 mark): $$\frac{dv}{dS} = \frac{V_m (K_m + S) - V_m S}{(K_m + S)^2} = \frac{V_m K_m}{(K_m + S)^2}$$ **Step 3 — Evaluate the derivative at $S_0 = 5$** (1 mark): $$\left.\frac{dv}{dS}\right\vert_{S_0 = 5} = \frac{10 \times 5}{(5 + 5)^2} = \frac{50}{100} = 0.5 \text{ s}^{-1}$$ **Step 4 — Write the linearized equation** (2 marks): $$v \approx v_0 + \left.\frac{dv}{dS}\right\vert_{S_0} \delta S$$ $$v \approx 5 + 0.5\, \delta S \quad \text{where} \quad \delta S = S - 5 \text{ mmol/L}$$ **Step 5 — Comment on validity** (2 marks): The linear approximation is valid only when $\vert \delta S \vert \ll K_m$ (i.e., $\vert \delta S \vert \ll 5$ mmol/L). At $S = 15$ mmol/L (i.e., $\delta S = 10$), the linearized model gives $v_{lin} = 5 + 0.5 \times 10 = 10$ mmol/(L·s), whereas the true nonlinear model gives $v_{true} = 10 \times 15 / 20 = 7.5$ mmol/(L·s) — an error of **33%**. The error grows as $\mathcal{O}((\delta S)^2)$ and becomes unacceptable beyond $\vert \delta S \vert \approx K_m$. --- #### **QUESTION B (14 Marks) — Logistic Growth with Stability Analysis** **(a)** Derive the analytic solution of the logistic differential equation $\dot{N} = r N(1 - N/K)$ with initial condition $N(0) = N_0$. Identify the two equilibrium points and classify their stability using the Jacobian. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A bacterial culture grows with $r = 0.5 \text{ h}^{-1}$ and $K = 10^6$ cells/mL. Initially $N_0 = 10^4$ cells/mL. **(i)** Find the time $t^*$ at which the population reaches **90%** of the carrying capacity. **(ii)** Linearize the system around $N = 0.5 K$ and write the linearized differential equation for $\delta N$. Determine whether this equilibrium is **stable**. **(7 marks)** --- **MODEL SOLUTION:** **(a) — 7 marks** **Step 1 — Equilibrium points** (1 mark): Setting $\dot{N} = 0$: $$r N(1 - N/K) = 0 \;\Rightarrow\; N_1^* = 0, \quad N_2^* = K$$ **Step 2 — Jacobian of the 1D system** (1 mark): For a scalar ODE, the Jacobian is just the derivative $d\dot{N}/dN$: $$J(N) = \frac{d}{dN}\!\left[r N(1 - N/K)\right] = r - \frac{2rN}{K} = r\!\left(1 - \frac{2N}{K}\right)$$ **Step 3 — Classify stability** (2 marks): - At $N_1^* = 0$: $J(0) = r > 0$ → **unstable** equilibrium (eigenvalue positive). - At $N_2^* = K$: $J(K) = r(1 - 2) = -r < 0$ → **stable** equilibrium (eigenvalue negative). **Step 4 — Analytic solution** (3 marks): Following Section 3.3: $$\boxed{\,N(t) = \frac{K}{1 + \left(\dfrac{K - N_0}{N_0}\right) e^{-r t}}\,}$$ --- **(b) — 7 marks** **(i) Time to reach 90% of $K$** (3 marks): Set $N(t^*) = 0.9 K$: $$0.9 K = \frac{K}{1 + A e^{-r t^*}}, \quad \text{where } A = \frac{K - N_0}{N_0} = \frac{10^6 - 10^4}{10^4} = 99$$ $$\frac{1}{0.9} = 1 + 99 e^{-r t^*}$$ $$99 e^{-r t^*} = \frac{1}{0.9} - 1 = \frac{0.1}{0.9} = \frac{1}{9}$$ $$e^{-r t^*} = \frac{1}{891}$$ $$-r t^* = \ln(1/891) = -6.792$$ $$t^* = \frac{6.792}{0.5} \approx 13.58 \text{ h}$$ **(ii) Linearization around $N = 0.5 K$** (4 marks): $$J(0.5K) = r\left(1 - \frac{2 \times 0.5K}{K}\right) = r(1 - 1) = 0$$ Since the linearized eigenvalue is **zero**, the linearization is **marginally stable** (degenerate case). The full nonlinear analysis shows $N = 0.5K$ lies between the unstable fixed point $N=0$ and the stable fixed point $N=K$; the **nonlinear trajectory** moves monotonically from $N=0$ to $N=K$ passing through $0.5K$. For small $\delta N$ around $0.5K$, the linearized equation is: $$\dot{\delta N} \approx 0 \cdot \delta N \Rightarrow \delta N(t) \approx \text{constant}$$ To get a *better* local approximation, expand to **second order**: $$\dot{\delta N} \approx -\frac{r}{K}(\delta N)^2$$ This shows that for $\delta N > 0$ the perturbation **decays** ($\dot{\delta N} < 0$), and for $\delta N < 0$ the perturbation **grows** — a hallmark of a *saddle-node-like* transient. Physically, $0.5K$ is the inflection point of the S-shaped growth curve where growth rate is maximum. --- ### 5.3 KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning > [!WARNING] > **Common places where students LOSE marks in this topic:** > > 1. **Forgetting the QSSA assumption** in the Michaelis–Menten derivation — examiners deduct up to **2 marks** if the assumption is not explicitly stated. > 2. **Confusing $K_m$ (Michaelis constant) with $K_d$ (dissociation constant).** $K_m = (k_{-1}+k_2)/k_1$, which equals $K_d$ only when $k_2 \ll k_{-1}$. The examiner expects the *full* expression. > 3. **Linearization without specifying the operating point** — the linearized model is meaningless without $(x_0, y_0)$. > 4. **Sign error in Jacobian entries** — carefully check the partial derivative sign convention. > 5. **Forgetting to convert units** — minutes vs. hours, mM vs. mol/L. > 6. **In the logistic problem**, students often mis-evaluate $t^*$ by inverting the wrong fraction; remember that $N(t^*)/K = 0.9$, not $N(t^*) = 0.9 \times N_0$. > 7. **Not commenting on the validity range of linearization** — examiners specifically award marks for the "range of validity" discussion (typically 1–2 marks). --- ### 5.4 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!NOTE] > **Rapid-Revision Checklist (Last-Minute Glance Before Exam):** > > - **Nonlinear = violates superposition.** If $f(\alpha x_1 + \beta x_2) \neq \alpha f(x_1) + \beta f(x_2)$, the system is nonlinear. > - **Four physiological nonlinearities to memorize:** saturation, threshold, sigmoidal, multiplicative. > - **Michaelis–Menten** is the canonical saturation nonlinearity: $v = V_m S/(K_m + S)$. $K_m$ = substrate concentration at half-maximum velocity; $V_m$ = maximum velocity at enzyme saturation. > - **Hill equation** models cooperativity: $\theta = P^n / (P_{50}^n + P^n)$. Hemoglobin has $n \approx 2.8$. > - **Logistic equation** models bounded growth: $N(t) = K / [1 + ((K - N_0)/N_0) e^{-rt}]$. Two equilibria: $N = 0$ unstable, $N = K$ stable. > - **Van der Pol** is the canonical limit-cycle oscillator: $\ddot{x} - \mu(1 - x^2)\dot{x} + x = 0$. $\mu > 0$ gives a stable limit cycle. > - **Linearization** uses first-order Taylor expansion around an equilibrium: $f(x) \approx f(x_0) + f'(x_0)(x - x_0)$. > - **Jacobian** for a 2D system is a $2 \times 2$ matrix of partial derivatives; its eigenvalues determine local stability. > - **Stability rule:** $\text{Re}(\lambda) < 0$ ⇒ stable; $\text{Re}(\lambda) > 0$ ⇒ unstable; pure imaginary ⇒ center / Hopf bifurcation. > - **Range of validity** of linearization: $\vert \delta x \vert \ll$ the local "length-scale" of $f$ (often $K_m$ or $K$); error grows as $(\delta x)^2$. > - **Numerical method of choice** for stiff nonlinear ODEs in physiology: 4th-order Runge–Kutta with adaptive step size. > - **Units to remember:** $V_m$ in mmol/(L·s); $K_m$ in mmol/L or mol/L; $r$ in s$^{-1}$ or h$^{-1}$; $R = 8.314$ J/(mol·K); $F = 96485$ C/mol; $T = 310$ K. > - **Physiological examples to cite** (KTU loves these): insulin–glucose regulation (saturable uptake), hemoglobin–O$_2$ binding (Hill), action potential (Hodgkin–Huxley), cardiac pacemaker (Van der Pol), tumour growth (logistic).

topics.title: Time-varying Modeling
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT864 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module Module 1
> - **Topic:** Time-varying Modeling

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Time-varying modeling** is the mathematical representation of a system in which the system's parameters, coefficients, or internal dynamics explicitly depend on the independent variable — most commonly **time ($t$)**. In the context of physiological systems, a time-varying model is one whose governing differential equations, transfer characteristics, or state-transition matrices are functions of $t$ (i.e., the system is **non-stationary**).

> [!NOTE]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition:**
> A time-varying (or non-stationary) system is a system whose output response to a given input depends not only on the current and past values of the input but also on the *absolute time* at which the input is applied. Mathematically, if $y(t)$ is the response to input $x(t)$, the operator $\mathcal{H}$ satisfies:
> $$\mathcal{H}\{x(t)\} \neq y(t - t_0) \quad \text{when input is shifted to } x(t - t_0)$$

Formally, a system is **time-varying** if the system's response to a time-shifted input is NOT identical to the time-shifted response of the original system.

### 1.2 Conceptual Analogy — The Adaptive Steering Wheel

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Intuitive Analogy (The Adaptive Driver):**
> Imagine a car whose steering sensitivity changes with your **speed**.
> - At **low speeds** (e.g., parking), the steering is **highly sensitive** — a small turn of the wheel produces a large wheel angle.
> - At **high speeds** (e.g., highway), the steering is **less sensitive** — the same wheel input produces a smaller wheel angle.
>
> The "input → output" relationship (steering input → wheel response) is *different* at 10 km/h vs 100 km/h. The car is a **time-varying system** (with speed as a slowly varying parameter). A **constant-coefficient** (time-invariant) model of this car would be valid only at a single fixed speed.

In the human body, the "steering sensitivity" of:
- The **pupil** to light changes as ambient brightness shifts.
- The **heart** to vagal tone changes during exercise, sleep, or stress.
- The **kidney** to filtration pressure changes as hormones (ADH, aldosterone) fluctuate.

These are all **time-varying physiological systems**.

### 1.3 Why Physiological Systems Are Almost Always Time-Varying

| Physiological Subsystem | Why It Is Time-Varying |
|---|---|
| Cardiovascular | Heart rate, vascular resistance, and baroreflex gain change with posture, exercise, emotion |
| Respiratory | Lung compliance and chemoreceptor sensitivity change with sleep stage |
| Endocrine | Hormone clearance rates change with liver/kidney function, age, and feedback state |
| Neuromuscular | Motor unit recruitment and firing patterns adapt with fatigue and learning |
| Metabolic | Insulin sensitivity varies with meal timing, activity, and circadian rhythm |

### 1.4 Stationary vs. Non-Stationary — A Clear Contrast

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Distinction (Exam Favorite):**
> - **Time-Invariant (LTI) System:** $\mathcal{H}\{x(t-\tau)\} = y(t-\tau)$ — shift-invariance holds.
> - **Time-Varying (LTV) System:** $\mathcal{H}\{x(t-\tau)\} \neq y(t-\tau)$ — the system "remembers" the absolute time.

### 1.5 Physical Constants & Standard Metrics in Time-Varying Models

| Metric | Symbol | Typical Physiological Range |
|---|---|---|
| Heart rate | $f_H$ | **60–200 beats/min** (rest → exercise) |
| Respiratory rate | $f_R$ | **12–40 breaths/min** (rest → exercise) |
| Cardiac output | $CO$ | **5–25 L/min** |
| Time constant of vascular adaptation | $\tau_{vc}$ | **5–30 seconds** |
| Baroreflex loop delay | $T_d$ | **~1–2 seconds** |
| Pupillary response latency | $T_p$ | **200–300 ms** |

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Mathematical Framework

### 2.1.1 Linear Time-Varying (LTV) Systems — General Form

The most general linear input-output relation for an LTV system is given by the **time-varying convolution integral**:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t, \tau) \, x(\tau) \, d\tau$$

where $h(t, \tau)$ is the **time-varying impulse response** — the response observed at time $t$ due to an impulse applied at time $\tau$.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> For an **LTI** system, $h(t, \tau) = h(t - \tau)$ — the impulse response depends only on the elapsed time, not on absolute time.
> For an **LTV** system, $h(t, \tau)$ is a function of **two independent time arguments** — the impulse response itself evolves with time.

### 2.1.2 State-Space Representation of LTV Systems

The state-space description of a continuous-time LTV system is:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}(t) \, \mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}(t) \, u(t)$$

$$\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}(t) \, \mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{D}(t) \, u(t)$$

where:
- $\mathbf{A}(t)$ is the **time-varying system matrix** (governs internal dynamics)
- $\mathbf{B}(t)$ is the **time-varying input matrix**
- $\mathbf{C}(t)$ is the **time-varying output matrix**
- $\mathbf{D}(t)$ is the **time-varying feedthrough matrix**

For LTI systems, $\mathbf{A}(t) = \mathbf{A}$, $\mathbf{B}(t) = \mathbf{B}$, etc. (constant).

### 2.1.3 Solution via the State-Transition Matrix

The solution of the homogeneous LTV equation is:

$$\mathbf{x}(t) = \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0) \, \mathbf{x}(t_0)$$

where $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0)$ is the **state-transition matrix**, satisfying:

$$\frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0)}{\partial t} = \mathbf{A}(t) \, \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0), \quad \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t_0, t_0) = \mathbf{I}$$

The complete forced solution is:

$$\mathbf{x}(t) = \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0) \, \mathbf{x}(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, \tau) \, \mathbf{B}(\tau) \, u(\tau) \, d\tau$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key Property of LTV Systems:**
> In general, $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0) \neq \exp\!\big(\mathbf{A}(t - t_0)\big)$. The state-transition matrix is obtained by **Peano-Baker series** or **numerical integration** of the matrix ODE. Only for **commuting** $\mathbf{A}(t)$ matrices does a simple exponential formula hold.

## 2.2 Test for Time-Variance (The Time-Shift Test)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Test:** Apply a time-shifted input $x(t - t_0)$ to the system. If the output is the correspondingly shifted $y(t - t_0)$, the system is time-invariant. Otherwise, it is time-varying.

**Worked Test Examples:**

| System Equation | Time-Invariant? | Reason |
|---|---|---|
| $y(t) = t \cdot x(t)$ | **No** | Multiplier $t$ depends on absolute time |
| $y(t) = x(t) + 3$ | **No** | Constant bias depends on time-shifted input's "new" origin |
| $y(t) = x(2t)$ | **No** | Time-scaling depends on absolute time |
| $y(t) = \int_0^t e^{-a(t-\tau)} x(\tau) d\tau$ | **Yes** | Depends only on $t - \tau$ |
| $y(t) = \int_0^t e^{-a(t-\tau)} \cos(\omega(t)\tau) x(\tau) d\tau$ | **No** | $\omega(t)$ varies with time |

## 2.3 Why LTV Models Are Essential in Physiology — Structural Reasoning

1. **Homeostasis is dynamic, not static.** Set-points (e.g., body temperature $\approx 37^\circ$C) drift with circadian rhythm, infection, and exercise.
2. **Feedback gains adapt.** Baroreflex gain reduces during sustained hypertension; chemoreceptor sensitivity decreases during chronic hypoxia.
3. **Compartmental volumes change.** Blood volume, lung volume, and tissue fluid shift with posture and hydration.
4. **Enzyme induction.** Cytochrome P450 enzymes are induced by drugs, altering pharmacokinetic constants over days.
5. **Neural plasticity.** Synaptic efficacy (Hebbian learning) evolves with activity.

## 2.4 Methods of Time-Varying System Analysis

| Method | Description | Physiological Application |
|---|---|---|
| **Time-varying impulse response $h(t,\tau)$** | Direct 2-D characterization | Drug distribution in tissues |
| **Wigner-Ville distribution** | Joint time-frequency energy density | EEG/HRV spectral analysis |
| **Short-Time Fourier Transform (STFT)** | Sliding window Fourier analysis | Heart rate variability (HRV) |
| **Wavelet transform** | Multi-resolution time-frequency analysis | Evoked potentials, gait cycles |
| **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** | Online parameter tracking | Adaptive blood pressure estimation |
| **Kalman filter (time-varying)** | Optimal state estimation with evolving model | Glucose-insulin monitoring (CGM) |
| **Volterra / Wiener series** | Nonlinear time-varying kernels | Auditory system, pupil reflex |
| **Hidden Markov Models (HMM)** | Discrete state switching | Sleep staging, ECG arrhythmia detection |

## 2.5 KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Master These Equations — They Appear in Nearly Every KTU Exam:**

| # | Equation | Physical Meaning | Conditions |
|---|---|---|---|
| 1 | $y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t, \tau) x(\tau) \, d\tau$ | Time-varying convolution | General LTV |
| 2 | $h_{\text{LTV}}(t, \tau) \neq h(t - \tau)$ | LTV impulse response (2-D) | LTV only |
| 3 | $h_{\text{LTI}}(t, \tau) = h(t - \tau)$ | LTI impulse response (1-D) | LTI only |
| 4 | $\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}(t)\mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}(t)u(t)$ | LTV state equation | Continuous time |
| 5 | $\mathbf{x}(t) = \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0)\mathbf{x}(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, \tau)\mathbf{B}(\tau)u(\tau) d\tau$ | General LTV solution | Initial cond. at $t_0$ |
| 6 | $\dot{\boldsymbol{\Phi}}(t, t_0) = \mathbf{A}(t) \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0)$ | STM ODE | $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t_0, t_0) = \mathbf{I}$ |
| 7 | $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0) = \mathbf{I} + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mathbf{A}(\sigma_1) d\sigma_1 + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mathbf{A}(\sigma_1) \int_{t_0}^{\sigma_1} \mathbf{A}(\sigma_2) d\sigma_2 d\sigma_1 + \cdots$ | Peano-Baker series | Exact, no commutativity needed |
| 8 | $\dfrac{dy}{dt} + a(t) y = b(t) u(t)$ | First-order LTV ODE | Scalar case |
| 9 | $\mu(t) = \exp\!\left(-\int_{t_0}^{t} a(\sigma) d\sigma\right)$ | Integrating factor | Used to solve #8 |
| 10 | $S_{xy}^{\text{Wigner}}(t, f) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} x\!\left(t + \tfrac{\tau}{2}\right) x^{*}\!\left(t - \tfrac{\tau}{2}\right) e^{-j 2\pi f \tau} d\tau$ | Wigner-Ville distribution | Bilinear, time-frequency |

## 2.6 Real-World Engineering Utility

- **Wearable health monitors** (Apple Watch, Fitbit): use time-varying Kalman filters to track heart rate, blood oxygenation, and atrial fibrillation.
- **Anesthesia depth monitors** (BIS): use time-varying autoregressive models on EEG.
- **Artificial pancreas**: uses a time-varying physiological model of glucose-insulin dynamics, with patient-specific parameter adaptation.
- **Hearing aids**: employ adaptive filters whose coefficients track changes in the acoustic environment.
- **Closed-loop ICU ventilators**: continuously re-identify patient lung mechanics (compliance, resistance) which are time-varying.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Solution of the First-Order LTV ODE

We wish to solve the scalar LTV differential equation:

$$\frac{dy(t)}{dt} + a(t) \, y(t) = b(t) \, u(t)$$

with initial condition $y(t_0) = y_0$.

**Step 1 — Rearrange to standard form.**

$$\frac{dy}{dt} = -a(t) y + b(t) u$$

**Step 2 — Multiply both sides by the integrating factor $\mu(t)$.**

Multiply throughout by:

$$\mu(t) = \exp\!\left( \int_{t_0}^{t} a(\sigma) \, d\sigma \right)$$

This choice is made because:

$$\frac{d\mu}{dt} = a(t) \cdot \mu(t)$$

**Step 3 — Recognize the left-hand side as an exact derivative.**

Since $\mu(t) \dfrac{dy}{dt} + \dfrac{d\mu}{dt} y = \dfrac{d}{dt}\big[ \mu(t) y(t) \big]$, the equation becomes:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\big[ \mu(t) y(t) \big] = \mu(t) \, b(t) \, u(t)$$

**Step 4 — Integrate both sides from $t_0$ to $t$.**

$$\mu(t) y(t) - \mu(t_0) y(t_0) = \int_{t_0}^{t} \mu(\sigma) \, b(\sigma) \, u(\sigma) \, d\sigma$$

Since $\mu(t_0) = \exp(0) = 1$:

$$\mu(t) y(t) = y(t_0) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mu(\sigma) \, b(\sigma) \, u(\sigma) \, d\sigma$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $y(t)$.**

$$y(t) = \frac{y(t_0)}{\mu(t)} + \frac{1}{\mu(t)} \int_{t_0}^{t} \mu(\sigma) \, b(\sigma) \, u(\sigma) \, d\sigma$$

Substituting the explicit form of $\mu(t)$:

$$\boxed{\, y(t) = y(t_0) \exp\!\left( -\int_{t_0}^{t} a(\sigma) d\sigma \right) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \exp\!\left( -\int_{\sigma}^{t} a(\xi) d\xi \right) b(\sigma) u(\sigma) \, d\sigma \,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Physical Interpretation:**
> - The **first term** is the *natural response* — the initial condition decays with a time-varying rate determined by $a(t)$.
> - The **second term** is the *forced response* — a weighted integral of $u(\sigma)$ with a time-varying kernel.
> - Compare with LTI case: $y(t) = y_0 e^{-a(t-t_0)} + \int_{t_0}^{t} e^{-a(t-\sigma)} b \, u(\sigma) d\sigma$.

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Peano-Baker Series for the State-Transition Matrix

We wish to compute $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0)$ satisfying:

$$\frac{\partial \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0)}{\partial t} = \mathbf{A}(t) \, \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0), \quad \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t_0, t_0) = \mathbf{I}$$

**Step 1 — Integrate the matrix ODE formally.**

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0) = \mathbf{I} + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mathbf{A}(\sigma_1) \, \boldsymbol{\Phi}(\sigma_1, t_0) \, d\sigma_1$$

**Step 2 — Substitute $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(\sigma_1, t_0)$ recursively.**

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}(\sigma_1, t_0) = \mathbf{I} + \int_{t_0}^{\sigma_1} \mathbf{A}(\sigma_2) \, \boldsymbol{\Phi}(\sigma_2, t_0) \, d\sigma_2$$

Substituting back:

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0) = \mathbf{I} + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mathbf{A}(\sigma_1) \left[ \mathbf{I} + \int_{t_0}^{\sigma_1} \mathbf{A}(\sigma_2) \boldsymbol{\Phi}(\sigma_2, t_0) \, d\sigma_2 \right] d\sigma_1$$

**Step 3 — Expand and iterate.**

$$\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0) = \mathbf{I} + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mathbf{A}(\sigma_1) d\sigma_1 + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mathbf{A}(\sigma_1) \int_{t_0}^{\sigma_1} \mathbf{A}(\sigma_2) d\sigma_2 \, d\sigma_1 + \cdots$$

**Step 4 — Write the infinite series (Peano-Baker series).**

$$\boxed{\, \boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0) = \mathbf{I} + \sum_{k=1}^{\infty} \int_{t_0}^{t} \int_{t_0}^{\sigma_1} \cdots \int_{t_0}^{\sigma_{k-1}} \mathbf{A}(\sigma_1) \mathbf{A}(\sigma_2) \cdots \mathbf{A}(\sigma_k) \, d\sigma_k \cdots d\sigma_2 \, d\sigma_1 \,}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Crucial Note:**
> The matrix product $\mathbf{A}(\sigma_1)\mathbf{A}(\sigma_2)$ is **ordered** — it depends on the *order* of time arguments. This is why LTV systems do not have a closed-form exponential solution in general. For LTI, $\mathbf{A}(t) = \mathbf{A}$ (constant), and the series collapses to $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0) = e^{\mathbf{A}(t - t_0)}$.

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Time-Varying Convolution from Impulse Decomposition

**Setup:** We approximate an arbitrary input $x(t)$ as a sum of weighted, time-shifted impulses:

$$x(t) \approx \sum_{k} x(\tau_k) \, \Delta\tau \, \delta(t - \tau_k)$$

**Step 1 — Response to an impulse at time $\tau$.**

By definition, the response at observation time $t$ to an impulse applied at time $\tau$ is $h(t, \tau)$. So the contribution from the impulse at $\tau_k$ is:

$$y_k(t) = x(\tau_k) \, \Delta\tau \, h(t, \tau_k)$$

**Step 2 — Superpose (linearity).**

$$y(t) = \sum_{k} x(\tau_k) \, h(t, \tau_k) \, \Delta\tau$$

**Step 3 — Take the limit as $\Delta\tau \to 0$.**

The sum becomes the Riemann integral:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t, \tau) \, x(\tau) \, d\tau$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Why two time arguments?**
> In LTI, $h(t, \tau) = h(t - \tau)$ — the *elapsed time* is what matters. In LTV, the *absolute time* $\tau$ at which the impulse arrives also matters because the system's properties may have changed since $t_0$.

## 3.4 Worked Example — Pharmacokinetic Model with Time-Varying Clearance

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem (KTU-style):**
> A drug is administered intravenously. Its plasma concentration $C(t)$ satisfies:
> $$\frac{dC}{dt} = -\frac{C(t)}{\tau_p(t)}, \quad C(0) = C_0$$
> where the elimination time constant $\tau_p(t) = \tau_0 (1 + 0.1 t)$ (increases linearly due to enzyme induction). Find $C(t)$.

**Step 1 — Identify the form.**

This is a first-order LTV ODE with $a(t) = 1/\tau_p(t) = \dfrac{1}{\tau_0 (1 + 0.1 t)}$ and $b(t) = 0$ (no forcing, homogeneous).

**Step 2 — Apply the integrating factor.**

$$\mu(t) = \exp\!\left( \int_0^t \frac{d\sigma}{\tau_0 (1 + 0.1 \sigma)} \right)$$

**Step 3 — Compute the integral.**

Let $u = 1 + 0.1 \sigma$, $du = 0.1 \, d\sigma$, so $d\sigma = 10 \, du$:

$$\int_0^t \frac{d\sigma}{\tau_0 (1 + 0.1\sigma)} = \frac{10}{\tau_0} \int_{1}^{1+0.1t} \frac{du}{u} = \frac{10}{\tau_0} \ln(1 + 0.1 t)$$

**Step 4 — Form $\mu(t)$.**

$$\mu(t) = \exp\!\left( \frac{10}{\tau_0} \ln(1 + 0.1 t) \right) = (1 + 0.1 t)^{10/\tau_0}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for $C(t)$.**

Since the system is homogeneous, $C(t) = C_0 / \mu(t)$:

$$\boxed{\, C(t) = \frac{C_0}{(1 + 0.1 t)^{10/\tau_0}} \,}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Insight:**
> Compared to a constant-$\tau$ exponential decay $C_0 e^{-t/\tau_0}$, this **power-law decay** falls off *more slowly* at long times because the clearance rate $1/\tau_p(t) = 1/[\tau_0(1+0.1t)]$ decreases over time as the body "learns" to eliminate the drug more slowly — a classic sign of enzyme induction.

## 3.5 Worked Example — Cardiovascular Heart-Rate Response to Exercise

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Problem:**
> Heart rate $H(t)$ (beats/min) responds to a step increase in workload $W(t) = W_0 \, u(t)$ according to:
> $$\frac{dH}{dt} + \frac{H(t)}{\tau(t)} = k(t) W(t), \quad H(0) = H_{\text{rest}}$$
> with $\tau(t) = 30 + 5 \sin(\pi t / 60)$ seconds (circadian modulation) and $k(t) = 0.5 + 0.05 t$. Find $H(t)$ for $t > 0$ when $W_0 = 100$ W.

**Step 1 — Compute the integrating factor.**

$$\int_0^t \frac{d\sigma}{\tau(\sigma)} = \int_0^t \frac{d\sigma}{30 + 5 \sin(\pi \sigma / 60)}$$

For small oscillations ($\sin \approx 0$), this is approximately $t/30$. We will use the linearized form for tractability.

**Step 2 — Linearize $\tau(t) \approx 30$ s (slowly varying).**

Then $1/\tau \approx 1/30$ s$^{-1}$, and:

$$H(t) \approx H(0) e^{-t/30} + \int_0^t e^{-(t-\sigma)/30} \, k(\sigma) \, W_0 \, d\sigma$$

**Step 3 — Substitute $k(\sigma) = 0.5 + 0.05 \sigma$ and integrate.**

$$H(t) = H_{\text{rest}} e^{-t/30} + 100 \int_0^t e^{-(t-\sigma)/30} (0.5 + 0.05 \sigma) \, d\sigma$$

$$= H_{\text{rest}} e^{-t/30} + 100 e^{-t/30} \int_0^t e^{\sigma/30} (0.5 + 0.05 \sigma) \, d\sigma$$

**Step 4 — Evaluate the integral.**

Let $I = \int_0^t e^{\sigma/30} (0.5 + 0.05\sigma) d\sigma$.

Using integration by parts:

$$I = \big[ 15 e^{\sigma/30} (0.5 + 0.05\sigma) \big]_0^t - \int_0^t 15 e^{\sigma/30} (0.05) d\sigma$$

$$= 15 e^{t/30}(0.5 + 0.05 t) - 7.5 - 0.75 (e^{t/30} - 1)$$

$$= e^{t/30} \big(7.5 + 0.75 t - 0.75\big) - 6.75$$

$$= e^{t/30}(6.75 + 0.75 t) - 6.75$$

**Step 5 — Combine and simplify.**

$$H(t) = H_{\text{rest}} e^{-t/30} + 100 \big[ (6.75 + 0.75 t) - 6.75 e^{-t/30} \big]$$

$$\boxed{\, H(t) = H_{\text{rest}} e^{-t/30} + 675 + 75 t - 675 e^{-t/30} \,}$$

$$\boxed{\, H(t) = (H_{\text{rest}} - 675) e^{-t/30} + 675 + 75 t \,}$$

**Step 6 — Interpret.**

- $H_{\text{rest}} \approx 70$ bpm.
- As $t \to \infty$ (with our linearization), the exponential decays, and $H(t) \to 675 + 75 t$ — an *unbounded* growth, indicating the linearization fails at long times (real $H$ saturates at $\sim 180$ bpm).
- The **time-varying gain $k(t)$** causes the heart rate to keep climbing rather than plateauing, capturing the slow sympathetic adaptation that a constant-gain (LTI) model would miss.

## 3.6 Time-Varying AR Model (Used in HRV and EEG)

A widely used time-varying model in physiological signal processing is the **time-varying autoregressive (TVAR) model**:

$$x(n) = \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k(n) \, x(n - k) + e(n)$$

where $a_k(n)$ are **time-varying AR coefficients** tracked online via **Recursive Least Squares (RLS)** or **Kalman filtering**. The time-varying power spectral density is:

$$P(n, f) = \frac{\sigma_e^2(n)}{\left\vert 1 - \sum_{k=1}^{p} a_k(n) e^{-j 2\pi f k} \right\vert^2}$$

This is used to track **heart rate variability** dynamics, **EEG band power** during anesthesia, and **respiratory sinus arrhythmia** modulation in real time.

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

## 4.1 Time-Varying Modeling Framework — Block Diagram

> [!NOTE]
> The Mermaid diagram below illustrates the conceptual architecture of a time-varying physiological modeling pipeline — from raw measurement to adaptive parameter estimation and clinical decision support.

```mermaid

    B --> C[Preprocessing\nFiltering, Artifact Removal]

    D --> E[Parameter Tracking\nRLS / Kalman Filter]

```

    A --> B
    D --> B
    B --> E[Gain Update\nK(k) = P(k-1) phi / lambda + phi^T P phi]

# SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

## 5.1 Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

### Question A1
**[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023, Model QP, PEBMT864]**

> **"Define a time-varying system. How does it differ from a time-invariant system? Give one physiological example of a time-varying system."**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

A system is called **time-varying** if its parameters or characteristics change with the independent variable (typically time $t$), so that the output response depends on the *absolute time* at which the input is applied. Mathematically, if $y(t) = \mathcal{H}\{x(t)\}$, the system is time-varying if $\mathcal{H}\{x(t - t_0)\} \neq y(t - t_0)$ for any shift $t_0 \neq 0$.

A **time-invariant** (LTI) system, in contrast, satisfies $\mathcal{H}\{x(t - t_0)\} = y(t - t_0)$ — the response to a shifted input is the correspondingly shifted output, regardless of when the input is applied.

**Physiological example:** The **baroreflex control of heart rate** is time-varying because the baroreflex gain reduces during sustained hypertension and changes with posture, sleep, and exercise — the same blood pressure input produces a different heart-rate response at different times. **(3 marks)**

---

### Question A2
**[KTU University Exam — June 2024, Model QP, PEBMT864]**

> **"Write the state-space representation of a linear time-varying (LTV) system. How does it differ from the LTI state-space form?"**

**Model Answer (3 marks):**

The state-space representation of a continuous-time **LTV** system is:

$$\dot{\mathbf{x}}(t) = \mathbf{A}(t) \, \mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{B}(t) \, u(t)$$

$$\mathbf{y}(t) = \mathbf{C}(t) \, \mathbf{x}(t) + \mathbf{D}(t) \, u(t)$$

The matrices $\mathbf{A}(t)$, $\mathbf{B}(t)$, $\mathbf{C}(t)$, $\mathbf{D}(t)$ are **explicit functions of time**.

For an **LTI** system, the same equations hold but with $\mathbf{A}(t) = \mathbf{A}$ (constant), $\mathbf{B}(t) = \mathbf{B}$, etc. The time-varying matrices reflect changing dynamics (e.g., adaptation, learning, fatigue), while the constant matrices in LTI reflect fixed physics (e.g., $RC$ circuit with fixed components). **(3 marks)**

---

## 5.2 Part B Questions (14 Marks — Internal Choice)

### Question Choice A

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEBMT864, Module 1]**

> **A.** (a) Derive the general solution of the first-order linear time-varying differential equation
> $$\dfrac{dy(t)}{dt} + a(t) y(t) = b(t) u(t), \quad y(t_0) = y_0$$
> using the integrating factor method. Clearly state the integrating factor and the role of each term in the final solution. **(7 marks)**
>
> **A.** (b) A patient's drug plasma concentration satisfies
> $$\dfrac{dC}{dt} = -\dfrac{C(t)}{\tau_p(t)}, \quad C(0) = C_0$$
> where $\tau_p(t) = \tau_0 (1 + \alpha t)$ and $\alpha > 0$ (a linear increase in elimination time constant due to enzyme induction). Solve for $C(t)$ and comment on the difference from the LTI case. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Rearrange to standard form** [1 mark]

$$\frac{dy}{dt} + a(t) y = b(t) u$$

**Step 2: Choose the integrating factor** [1 mark]

$$\mu(t) = \exp\!\left( \int_{t_0}^{t} a(\sigma) d\sigma \right)$$

This is chosen so that $\dfrac{d\mu}{dt} = a(t) \mu(t)$.

**Step 3: Multiply through by $\mu(t)$** [1 mark]

$$\mu \frac{dy}{dt} + a(t) \mu y = \mu b(t) u \implies \frac{d}{dt}\big[ \mu(t) y(t) \big] = \mu(t) b(t) u(t)$$

**Step 4: Integrate from $t_0$ to $t$** [1 mark]

$$\mu(t) y(t) - \mu(t_0) y(t_0) = \int_{t_0}^{t} \mu(\sigma) b(\sigma) u(\sigma) d\sigma$$

Since $\mu(t_0) = 1$:

$$\mu(t) y(t) = y_0 + \int_{t_0}^{t} \mu(\sigma) b(\sigma) u(\sigma) d\sigma$$

**Step 5: Solve for $y(t)$** [1 mark]

$$y(t) = y_0 \exp\!\left( -\int_{t_0}^{t} a(\sigma) d\sigma \right) + \int_{t_0}^{t} \exp\!\left( -\int_{\sigma}^{t} a(\xi) d\xi \right) b(\sigma) u(\sigma) d\sigma$$

**Step 6: Physical interpretation** [2 marks]

- The **first term** is the *natural response* — initial condition $y_0$ decays with a time-varying rate $a(t)$.
- The **second term** is the *forced response* — a convolution with time-varying kernel $\exp\!\left( -\int_{\sigma}^{t} a(\xi) d\xi \right) b(\sigma)$.
- For LTI ($a(t) = a$ constant, $b(t) = b$): the kernel reduces to $b e^{-a(t - \sigma)}$, the classical convolution.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Identify the LTV parameters** [1 mark]

This is a homogeneous first-order LTV equation with:

$$a(t) = \frac{1}{\tau_p(t)} = \frac{1}{\tau_0 (1 + \alpha t)}, \quad b(t) = 0$$

**Step 2: Compute the integrating factor** [2 marks]

$$\mu(t) = \exp\!\left( \int_0^t \frac{d\sigma}{\tau_0 (1 + \alpha \sigma)} \right)$$

Let $u = 1 + \alpha \sigma$, $du = \alpha d\sigma$:

$$\int_0^t \frac{d\sigma}{\tau_0 (1 + \alpha \sigma)} = \frac{1}{\alpha \tau_0} \ln(1 + \alpha t)$$

$$\therefore \mu(t) = (1 + \alpha t)^{1/(\alpha \tau_0)}$$

**Step 3: Apply the homogeneous solution formula** [1 mark]

For a homogeneous equation, the solution is:

$$C(t) = C_0 \exp\!\left( -\int_0^t a(\sigma) d\sigma \right) = \frac{C_0}{\mu(t)}$$

**Step 4: Write the explicit result** [1 mark]

$$\boxed{\, C(t) = \frac{C_0}{(1 + \alpha t)^{1/(\alpha \tau_0)}} \,}$$

**Step 5: Compare with the LTI case** [2 marks]

- **LTI case** ($\alpha = 0$, $\tau_p = \tau_0$): $C(t) = C_0 e^{-t/\tau_0}$ — pure exponential decay with **constant half-life** $T_{1/2} = \tau_0 \ln 2$.
- **LTV case** ($\alpha > 0$): power-law decay $C(t) = C_0 (1 + \alpha t)^{-1/(\alpha \tau_0)}$.
- The concentration decays **slower** at long times because the elimination time constant $\tau_p$ is *growing* (enzyme induction makes the body clear the drug less effectively per unit time).
- The **instantaneous half-life** at time $t$ is $T_{1/2}(t) = \tau_p(t) \ln 2 = \tau_0 (1 + \alpha t) \ln 2$ — it increases linearly with time.
- This phenomenon cannot be captured by a constant-parameter (LTI) exponential model, demonstrating the necessity of time-varying modeling in pharmacokinetics.

---

### Question Choice B

**[KTU University Exam — Model Paper, PEBMT864, Module 1 — Alternative]**

> **B.** (a) Explain the **time-varying convolution integral** for a linear time-varying system. Show how it reduces to the standard LTI convolution when $h(t, \tau) = h(t - \tau)$. **(7 marks)**
>
> **B.** (b) The pupillary light reflex is modeled as a first-order LTV system:
> $$T(t) \dfrac{dD}{dt} + D(t) = k(t) L(t), \quad D(0) = D_0$$
> where $D(t)$ is pupil diameter (mm), $L(t)$ is luminance (cd/m²), and the time constant $T(t) = T_0 (1 + 0.05 t)$ accounts for fatigue. If $L(t) = L_0$ (constant) and $k(t) = k_0$ (constant), solve for $D(t)$. **(7 marks)**

#### Model Solution — Part (a) [7 marks]

**Step 1: State the LTV convolution integral** [2 marks]

The general LTV system is described by the two-argument impulse response $h(t, \tau)$, which gives the output at time $t$ due to an impulse applied at time $\tau$. For an input $x(t)$:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t, \tau) \, x(\tau) \, d\tau$$

**Step 2: Justify via impulse decomposition** [1 mark]

Decompose $x(t)$ into shifted impulses: $x(t) \approx \sum_k x(\tau_k) \Delta\tau \, \delta(t - \tau_k)$. By linearity, the response to each impulse is $x(\tau_k) \Delta\tau \, h(t, \tau_k)$. Summing and taking the limit yields the integral.

**Step 3: Reduction to LTI** [2 marks]

If the system is time-invariant, the impulse response depends only on the *elapsed time*: $h(t, \tau) = h(t - \tau)$. Substituting:

$$y(t) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(t - \tau) \, x(\tau) \, d\tau$$

Let $u = t - \tau$, $du = -d\tau$:

$$y(t) = \int_{+\infty}^{-\infty} h(u) \, x(t - u) \, (-du) = \int_{-\infty}^{+\infty} h(u) \, x(t - u) \, du$$

This is the **standard LTI convolution** $y(t) = (h * x)(t)$.

**Step 4: Distinguish via the time-shift test** [2 marks]

For an LTI system, the response to $x(t - t_0)$ is $y(t - t_0)$. For an LTV system, the response to $x(t - t_0)$ is $y_{t_0}(t) = \int h(t, \tau) x(\tau - t_0) d\tau$, which in general is **not** equal to $y(t - t_0)$. The "memory" is not just of elapsed time but of absolute time.

#### Model Solution — Part (b) [7 marks]

**Step 1: Identify the LTV coefficients** [1 mark]

Divide through by $T(t)$:

$$\frac{dD}{dt} + \frac{1}{T(t)} D = \frac{k_0}{T(t)} L_0, \quad D(0) = D_0$$

So $a(t) = 1/T(t) = 1/[T_0(1 + 0.05 t)]$ and $b(t) u(t) = k_0 L_0 / [T_0(1 + 0.05 t)]$.

**Step 2: Compute the integrating factor** [2 marks]

$$\mu(t) = \exp\!\left( \int_0^t \frac{d\sigma}{T_0(1 + 0.05 \sigma)} \right)$$

Let $w = 1 + 0.05 \sigma$, $dw = 0.05 d\sigma$:

$$\int_0^t \frac{d\sigma}{T_0(1 + 0.05 \sigma)} = \frac{20}{T_0} \ln(1 + 0.05 t)$$

$$\therefore \mu(t) = (1 + 0.05 t)^{20/T_0}$$

**Step 3: Form the exact differential and integrate** [2 marks]

$$\frac{d}{dt}\big[ (1 + 0.05 t)^{20/T_0} \, D(t) \big] = (1 + 0.05 t)^{20/T_0} \cdot \frac{k_0 L_0}{T_0(1 + 0.05 t)} = \frac{k_0 L_0}{T_0} (1 + 0.05 t)^{(20 - T_0)/T_0}$$

Integrating from $0$ to $t$:

$$(1 + 0.05 t)^{20/T_0} D(t) - D_0 = \frac{k_0 L_0}{T_0} \int_0^t (1 + 0.05 \sigma)^{(20 - T_0)/T_0} d\sigma$$

**Step 4: Evaluate the integral** [1 mark]

For typical $T_0 \approx 0.2$ s, $(20 - T_0)/T_0 \approx 99$. Using $\int_0^t (1 + 0.05\sigma)^n d\sigma = \frac{20}{0.05(n+1)}[(1+0.05t)^{n+1} - 1]$:

$$= \frac{400}{n+1}\big[ (1 + 0.05 t)^{n+1} - 1 \big]$$

**Step 5: Final solution** [1 mark]

$$D(t) = \frac{D_0}{(1 + 0.05 t)^{20/T_0}} + \frac{20 k_0 L_0}{T_0} \cdot \frac{(1 + 0.05 t)^{20/T_0} - 1}{(1 + 0.05 t)^{20/T_0}}$$

The first term is the natural decay; the second is the forced response approaching the steady-state value $20 k_0 L_0 / T_0$ as $t \to \infty$ (with time-varying approach rate).

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:**
> 1. **Confusing "time-varying" with "nonlinear":** A system can be linear AND time-varying. They are independent properties. A system like $y = t \cdot x$ is linear (in $x$) but time-varying (in $t$).
> 2. **Forgetting that $h(t, \tau)$ has TWO arguments:** Writing $h(t - \tau)$ automatically assumes LTI. Always write $h(t, \tau)$ for LTV.
> 3. **Assuming $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0) = e^{\mathbf{A}(t - t_0)}$:** This formula holds ONLY when $\mathbf{A}$ is constant (LTI). For LTV, use the Peano-Baker series.
> 4. **Sign error in integrating factor:** The correct form is $\mu(t) = \exp(\int_{t_0}^{t} a(\sigma) d\sigma)$, NOT $\exp(-\int a d\sigma)$. The negative sign appears after dividing $y$ by $\mu$.
> 5. **Forgetting initial-condition term:** The complete LTV solution has BOTH the natural-response term AND the forced-response integral. Many students write only one.
> 6. **Treating time-varying systems with transfer functions:** The classical transfer function $H(s)$ is only defined for LTI systems. For LTV, use the Wigner-Ville distribution or time-frequency analysis.

---

## 5.3 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Rapid Revision Checklist — Time-Varying Modeling:**

- **Definition:** A system whose parameters OR impulse response depends on the absolute time $t$ (not just the elapsed time).
- **Time-Shift Test:** Apply $x(t - t_0)$; if output $\neq y(t - t_0)$, the system is time-varying.
- **Two-Argument Impulse Response:** $h(t, \tau)$ is the cornerstone of LTV systems. It collapses to $h(t - \tau)$ for LTI.
- **State-Space Form:** $\dot{\mathbf{x}} = \mathbf{A}(t)\mathbf{x} + \mathbf{B}(t)u$; the state-transition matrix is found via Peano-Baker series, not matrix exponential.
- **Integrating Factor Method:** $\mu(t) = \exp(\int_{t_0}^t a(\sigma) d\sigma)$; multiply the LTV ODE by $\mu$ to get an exact derivative.
- **Solution Structure:** $y(t) = $ (decaying initial condition) + (time-varying convolution integral).
- **Physiological Drivers:** Adaptation, fatigue, learning, enzyme induction, hormonal cycles, circadian rhythms.
- **Analysis Tools:** STFT, wavelets, Wigner-Ville, RLS, Kalman filter, time-varying AR models.
- **Real-World Tools:** Wearable monitors, anesthesia depth indices (BIS), artificial pancreas, hearing aids, ICU ventilators.
- **Watch out for:**
  - Linear $\neq$ time-invariant (linearity and time-invariance are INDEPENDENT properties).
  - $\boldsymbol{\Phi}(t, t_0) \neq e^{\mathbf{A}(t - t_0)}$ in general (use Peano-Baker).
  - Transfer functions $H(s)$ do NOT exist for LTV systems — use time-frequency methods.
  - The "instantaneous half-life" or "instantaneous time constant" of an LTV system depends on $t$.

> [!NOTE]
> **One-Line Memory Aid for Exam:**
> *"If the system's parameters change with the clock, it is time-varying. If only the *shift* matters, it is time-invariant. Physiological systems are time-varying almost always."*

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT864 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 1
> - **Topic:** Mathematical approach

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# MODULE 1: THE MATHEMATICAL APPROACH TO PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS

## 1.1 Core Definition and Scope

**Mathematical approach** in the context of physiological systems modelling is the disciplined, quantitative methodology that uses mathematical language, equations, and logical structures to represent, analyze, and predict the behaviour of living systems and their sub-components (organs, tissues, cells, and biomolecular networks).

In KTU 2024 Scheme terminology, the mathematical approach is defined as the systematic application of **conservation laws** (mass, energy, momentum, charge), **kinetic relations**, and **constitutive equations** to translate a biological/medical hypothesis into a solvable mathematical form — typically ordinary differential equations (ODEs), partial differential equations (PDEs), integral equations, or stochastic formulations.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Anchor:** The "Mathematical approach" topic in Module 1 establishes the philosophical and procedural framework on which all subsequent physiological models (cardiovascular, respiratory, renal, endocrine) are built. Mastery of this topic is *prerequisite* to every other module.

> [!NOTE]
> **Definition (Formal):** A **mathematical model** of a physiological system is a set of equations, together with associated boundary and initial conditions, whose solution quantitatively reproduces or predicts the input–output behaviour of a real biological system within an acceptable error tolerance.

---

## 1.2 Intuitive Overview – A Real-World Analogy

Imagine you are a **civil engineer** designing the water-supply network of a city. You do not just lay pipes randomly. You first *draw a map* of all reservoirs, pipes, valves, taps, and consumption points. Then you *write equations* — for every junction, the water entering must equal the water leaving (conservation of mass). For every pipe, the flow rate depends on pressure difference and pipe resistance (a constitutive law — analogous to Ohm's law in circuits or Poiseuille's law in blood vessels).

**The mathematical approach to physiological systems is exactly this — except the city is a human body, the pipes are blood vessels, the reservoirs are heart chambers and lungs, and the consumers are the cells of various organs.**

In other words:

| Civil Engineering Network | Physiological Counterpart |
|---|---|
| City water supply | Circulatory / respiratory / renal system |
| Reservoir | Heart chamber, lung, bladder, tissue compartment |
| Pipe | Blood vessel, bronchus, nephron tubule |
| Valve | Heart valve, sphincter, capillary bed |
| Tap (consumer) | Tissue metabolism, gas exchange, filtration |
| Pressure | Blood pressure, alveolar pressure, transmembrane potential |
| Flow rate Q | Cardiac output, ventilation rate, GFR |

Once the "map" is built, we write down the equations. The equations **do not lie** — they force us to be precise. If a model fails to predict reality, it tells us either our assumptions are wrong or our parameter values are wrong. This is the *power* of the mathematical approach: it converts vague biological intuition into testable, falsifiable predictions.

---

## 1.3 Why Use a Mathematical Approach? (The Engineering Justification)

1. **Quantification:** Converts qualitative biological knowledge ("the kidney filters blood") into numbers ("GFR ≈ 125 mL/min under baseline conditions").
2. **Prediction:** Forecasts system response to perturbations (drugs, exercise, disease, surgery) without risky human experiments.
3. **Hypothesis Testing:** A model that disagrees with data forces revision of biological assumptions.
4. **Optimization:** Enables design of treatments, drug dosing schedules, and prosthetics (e.g., artificial pancreas, dialysis machines).
5. **Reduction of Animal/Human Trials:** Computer-simulated models can screen interventions ethically before in-vivo testing.
6. **Integration:** Combines multi-scale information (molecular → cellular → organ → whole body) into a single coherent framework.

> [!NOTE]
> **Historical note:** The modern mathematical treatment of physiology began with the **Frank–Starling mechanism of the heart (1914–1918)**, the **Hodgkin–Huxley model of the nerve action potential (1952)** — which earned a Nobel Prize — and **Guyton's classic model of circulatory regulation (1972)**. The mathematical approach is therefore not new; what is new in the 2024 KTU scheme is the *systematic pedagogical treatment* of these ideas.

---

## 1.4 The Three Pillars of the Mathematical Approach

Every physiological model, no matter how complex, is built from exactly three building blocks:

**(a) Conservation Laws** — What cannot be created or destroyed.
- Conservation of mass (continuity equation)
- Conservation of energy (first law of thermodynamics)
- Conservation of momentum (Newton's second law in fluids — Navier–Stokes)
- Conservation of charge (Nernst–Planck equation in excitable membranes)

**(b) Constitutive Laws** — How the system responds.
- Ohm's law (V = IR) for current in circuits and resistance vessels
- Hooke's law (σ = E·ε) for elastic tissues (arteries, lungs, muscles)
- Fick's law (J = -D ∂C/∂x) for diffusion across membranes
- Michaelis–Menten kinetics for enzyme/substrate reactions
- Hill's equation for muscle contraction
- Henderson–Hasselbalch equation for blood pH

**(c) Geometric/Kinematic Constraints** — The shape and connectivity.
- Vessel lengths, radii, branching angles
- Compartment volumes
- Anatomical connectivity (series vs parallel organs)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **The KTU board examiner's litmus test:** When asked "What is the mathematical approach?", a top-grade answer should mention **(1) conservation laws, (2) constitutive/kinetic relations, and (3) system/geometry constraints**, and then describe how these combine into a *system of equations*. Memorize this triad.

---

## 1.5 Key Terminology You Must Know

| Term | Meaning | Physiological Example |
|---|---|---|
| **State variable** | Quantity that fully describes system status at any instant | Blood pressure, blood glucose, lung volume |
| **Input / Forcing function** | External stimulus driving the system | Drug infusion rate, exercise load |
| **Output / Response** | Measured effect of input | Heart rate change, oxygen uptake |
| **Parameter** | Constant property of the system | Vascular resistance, lung compliance |
| **Initial condition** | State at t = 0 | Baseline blood pressure = 120/80 mmHg |
| **Boundary condition** | Constraint at system edges | Atmospheric pressure = 760 mmHg at airway opening |
| **Lumped parameter** | Spatial variation ignored (whole organ treated uniformly) | Treating the entire left ventricle as one uniform chamber |
| **Distributed parameter** | Spatial variation is explicit (function of position and time) | Oxygen tension along a capillary from arteriole to venule |
| **Steady state** | All time derivatives = 0 | Resting cardiac output ≈ 5 L/min constant for hours |
| **Transient** | Time-varying behaviour before steady state | Heart rate rising from 70 to 150 bpm at start of exercise |
| **Linear system** | Superposition holds; response ∝ input | Small-signal behaviour of pulmonary stretch receptors |
| **Nonlinear system** | Superposition fails | Frank–Starling curve (stroke volume saturates) |
| **Deterministic** | No randomness; same input → same output | Single-compartment drug PK model |
| **Stochastic** | Random fluctuations included | Ion-channel gating noise in single neurons |
| **Black-box model** | Internal structure unknown; only input–output fitted | ARMAX model of heart rate variability |
| **White-box model** | Equations derived from first principles | Guyton's circulatory model |
| **Grey-box model** | Hybrid; some parameters from data | Pharmacokinetic models with fitted clearance |
| **Empirical model** | Fitted to data; no physical meaning required | Polynomial regression of dose–response curve |
| **Mechanistic model** | Derived from physical/biological mechanisms | Hodgkin–Huxley nerve membrane model |

---

## 1.6 What This Module Prepares You For

By the end of the mathematical-approach section of Module 1, you should be able to:

- Identify whether a given physiological problem requires a **lumped** or **distributed**, **static** or **dynamic**, **linear** or **nonlinear**, **deterministic** or **stochastic** model.
- Apply **mass-balance and energy-balance equations** to a single well-mixed compartment.
- Recognize the standard form of **first-order**, **second-order**, and **nth-order** ODEs commonly encountered in physiology.
- Distinguish between **time-domain** and **frequency-domain** representations.
- State the assumptions behind commonly cited physiological models.
- Build, from a verbal problem statement, a set of governing equations in state-space or transfer-function form.

# DEEP THEORETICAL ANALYSIS — THE MATHEMATICAL APPROACH

## 2.1 The General Mathematical Modelling Cycle (Step-by-Step)

The mathematical approach is *not* a single action; it is a **cyclic workflow**. The KTU 2024 syllabus expects students to be able to *describe and execute* this cycle. The standard six-step methodology is:

**Step 1 — Problem Definition and Scope Setting**
- State the biological/clinical question precisely.
- Decide the level of detail (molecular, cellular, organ, whole-body).
- Identify the boundary of the system (what is "inside" vs "outside").

**Step 2 — Conceptual Model and Assumption Listing**
- Draw a block diagram / compartmental schematic.
- List every assumption explicitly (e.g., "well-mixed compartment", "constant temperature", "Newtonian fluid").
- This step converts a fuzzy biological problem into a *mathematically tractable* one.

**Step 3 — Mathematical Formulation**
- Apply conservation laws → governing differential/integral equations.
- Apply constitutive laws → algebraic relations closing the equations.
- Specify initial and boundary conditions.

**Step 4 — Solution of the Mathematical Model**
- Analytical (closed-form) solution when possible.
- Numerical solution (Euler, Runge–Kutta, finite-difference, finite-element) otherwise.
- Use of software (MATLAB/Simulink, Python/SciPy, COMSOL).

**Step 5 — Validation and Verification**
- **Verification:** Is the equation solved correctly? (Math check.)
- **Validation:** Does the solution match real physiological data? (Reality check.)
- Sensitivity analysis on parameters.

**Step 6 — Interpretation and Iteration**
- Translate numerical results back into physiological insight.
- Refine the model if validation fails → return to Step 2.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Module-1 learning outcome:** *"Illustrate the steps involved in the mathematical modelling of physiological systems."* The six-step cycle above is the expected answer.

---

## 2.2 Classification of Mathematical Models

A single, all-encompassing classification does not exist; the KTU scheme recognises **five orthogonal dichotomies**:

### 2.2.1 Static vs Dynamic

- **Static (steady-state) model:** All derivatives with respect to time are zero. Describes equilibrium.
  - Example: Steady-state Fick principle for cardiac output:
    $$CO = \frac{VO_2}{C_aO_2 - C_vO_2}$$
    where $VO_2$ is oxygen consumption, $C_aO_2$ and $C_vO_2$ are arterial and venous oxygen contents.
- **Dynamic (transient) model:** Time derivatives retained. Describes how the system evolves.
  - Example: Windkessel model of arterial pressure:
    $$C\frac{dP}{dt} + \frac{P}{R} = Q(t)$$

### 2.2.2 Lumped vs Distributed

- **Lumped-parameter model:** Spatial variation ignored; the whole organ is one or a few "compartments" with uniform properties. Result → **ODEs**.
  - Example: One-compartment pharmacokinetic model:
    $$V\frac{dC}{dt} = -k\,C$$
- **Distributed-parameter model:** Variables depend on position $x$ and time $t$. Result → **PDEs**.
  - Example: Oxygen diffusion in a cylindrical tissue:
    $$\frac{\partial P}{\partial t} = D\,\frac{1}{r}\frac{\partial}{\partial r}\!\left(r\,\frac{\partial P}{\partial r}\right) - \dot{q}(P)$$

### 2.2.3 Linear vs Nonlinear

- **Linear:** Superposition and homogeneity hold. Easy to solve analytically; transfer functions exist.
  - Example: Linearized lung mechanics around FRC:
    $$L\frac{d^2V}{dt^2} + R\frac{dV}{dt} + \frac{V}{C} = P(t)$$
    where $L$ = inertance, $R$ = airway resistance, $C$ = compliance, $V$ = volume, $P$ = pressure.
- **Nonlinear:** At least one nonlinear term present. Usually requires numerical solution.
  - Example: Michaelis–Menten enzyme kinetics:
    $$v = \frac{V_{max}\,S}{K_m + S}$$

### 2.2.4 Deterministic vs Stochastic

- **Deterministic:** No randomness. Future is fully determined by present state and inputs.
- **Stochastic:** Includes random forcing (white noise, Poisson processes). Future is described by probability distributions.
  - Example: Random walk of an ion through a stochastic ion channel:
    $$dN(t) = \mu\,dt + \sigma\,dW(t)$$

### 2.2.5 Empirical vs Mechanistic

- **Empirical (phenomenological):** Curve-fit to data; parameters have no direct physical meaning.
- **Mechanistic (first-principles):** Derived from conservation laws and constitutive relations; parameters have physical meaning.
- **Hybrid (grey-box):** Combines both.

---

## 2.3 Conservation Laws — The Heart of the Mathematical Approach

### 2.3.1 Conservation of Mass (Continuity Equation)

For a control volume $V$ with surface $S$, normal vector $\mathbf{n}$ and fluid velocity $\mathbf{v}$:

$$\frac{d}{dt}\int_V \rho\,dV + \int_S \rho\,\mathbf{v}\cdot\mathbf{n}\,dS = 0$$

In physiological lumped form, for a single well-mixed compartment with volume $V$ (assumed constant), concentration $C(t)$ of a substance, inflow concentration $C_{in}$ and inflow rate $Q_{in}$:

$$V\frac{dC}{dt} = Q_{in}C_{in} - Q_{out}C + R_{gen} - R_{cons}$$

where $R_{gen}$ is the rate of generation (e.g., metabolic production) and $R_{cons}$ is the rate of consumption (e.g., metabolic clearance). If there is no generation/consumption and inflow = outflow ($Q$), this simplifies to:

$$V\frac{dC}{dt} = Q(C_{in} - C)$$

> [!NOTE]
> **This is the single most important equation in the entire course.** Every compartment model — for drugs, glucose, electrolytes, oxygen, hormones — begins here. Memorize it.

### 2.3.2 Conservation of Charge (Kirchhoff's Current Law in Excitable Membranes)

For an excitable cell with membrane capacitance $C_m$ and ionic currents $I_{ion}$:

$$C_m\frac{dV_m}{dt} = -\sum I_{ion}(V_m, t) + I_{stimulus}(t)$$

This is the foundation of the Hodgkin–Huxley model.

### 2.3.3 Conservation of Energy (First Law of Thermodynamics)

For a body with internal energy $U$, heat $Q$ added, and work $W$ done:

$$\frac{dU}{dt} = \dot{Q} - \dot{W}$$

Applied to body energy balance (metabolism, heat loss, work done):

$$M - W = E_{sk} + E_{res} + C_{resp}(T_{core} - T_{ambient})$$

where $M$ = metabolic rate, $W$ = external work, $E_{sk}$ = skin heat loss, $E_{res}$ = respiratory heat loss, $C_{resp}$ = respiratory heat conductance.

### 2.3.4 Conservation of Momentum (Newton's 2nd Law in Fluids)

For Newtonian fluid flow in a tube (Poiseuille flow):

$$Q = \frac{\pi r^4 \,\Delta P}{8 \mu L}$$

This is the constitutive relation linking flow $Q$, pressure difference $\Delta P$, tube radius $r$, fluid viscosity $\mu$, and length $L$ — the *hydraulic Ohm's law*.

---

## 2.4 Constitutive Laws in Physiology

| Law / Equation | Field | Expression |
|---|---|---|
| **Ohm's law (electrical)** | Current through resistor | $V = I R$ |
| **Ohm's law (hydraulic)** | Flow through vessel | $Q = \Delta P / R$ |
| **Hooke's law** | Elastic deformation | $\sigma = E \varepsilon$ |
| **Fick's law of diffusion** | Molecular flux | $J = -D\,\partial C / \partial x$ |
| **Fick's principle** | Organ blood flow | $Q = \dot{V}O_2 / (C_a - C_v)$ |
| **Henderson–Hasselbalch** | Blood pH | $pH = pK_a + \log\!\left(\frac{[HCO_3^-]}{[CO_2]}\right)$ |
| **Michaelis–Menten** | Enzyme kinetics | $v = V_{max}S / (K_m + S)$ |
| **Hill equation** | O₂–Hb saturation | $S = P^{n}/(P_{50}^{n} + P^{n})$ |
| **Nernst equation** | Equilibrium potential | $E = (RT/zF)\ln([ion]_{out}/[ion]_{in})$ |
| **Starling's law** | Capillary filtration | $J_v = L_p S [(P_c - P_i) - \sigma(\pi_c - \pi_i)]$ |
| **Laplace's law** | Wall tension in vessels | $T = P r / (2h)$ |
| **Poiseuille's law** | Viscous flow | $Q = \pi r^4 \Delta P / (8\mu L)$ |

---

## 2.6 Levels of Modelling in Physiology — The Multi-Scale View

A KTU 2024 examiner will sometimes ask: *"At which level is a given model formulated?"* The mathematical approach is the *same* at every level, but the equations look very different.

| Level | Spatial Scale | Typical State Variables | Typical Equations | Example Model |
|---|---|---|---|---|
| Molecular | 10⁻⁹ m | Ion concentrations, binding site occupancy | Algebraic / stochastic | O₂–Hb binding (Hill) |
| Cellular | 10⁻⁵ m | Membrane voltage, intracellular Ca²⁺ | ODEs (nonlinear) | Hodgkin–Huxley neuron |
| Tissue | 10⁻³ m | Tissue oxygen tension P(t,x) | PDEs (reaction–diffusion) | O₂ diffusion in myocardium |
| Organ | 10⁻¹ m | Pressure, volume, flow | ODEs (lumped compartments) | Windkessel, lung mechanics |
| Whole body | 10⁰ m | Arterial pressure, heart rate, temperature | Coupled ODEs | Guyton's circulatory model |

The "mathematical approach" is the *connecting thread* across all these scales.

---

## 2.7 Time-Domain vs Frequency-Domain Representations

The same physiological system can be described in two mathematically equivalent ways:

**Time-domain** (preferred for transient analysis, nonlinear systems):
$$\tau\frac{dy}{dt} + y = K\,u(t) \quad\Longleftrightarrow\quad y(t) = K(1 - e^{-t/\tau})$$

**Frequency-domain** (preferred for sinusoidal inputs, linear systems, control design):
$$G(s) = \frac{Y(s)}{U(s)} = \frac{K}{\tau s + 1}$$

For a sinusoidal input $u(t) = U_0\sin(\omega t)$ in steady state:
$$y(t) = \vert G(j\omega) \vert\,U_0\sin(\omega t + \angle G(j\omega))$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Bode plot insight:** A first-order low-pass physiological system (e.g., thermoregulation, drug dilution) has magnitude that rolls off at **−20 dB/decade** above the corner frequency $\omega_c = 1/\tau$, and phase that drops from 0° to −90°.

---

## 2.8 Engineering Utility of the Mathematical Approach

| Engineering Field | How Mathematical Physiology Is Used |
|---|---|
| **Biomedical instrumentation** | Designing ECG/EEG filters using transfer functions of electrode–tissue interface |
| **Prosthetic design** | Artificial kidney (dialysis) kinetics = 2-compartment urea model |
| **Drug delivery** | Closed-loop insulin pump = compartmental glucose–insulin ODE |
| **Anaesthesia control** | BIS-controlled propofol infusion = first-order PK + effect-site link |
| **Imaging** | Compartmental tracer-kinetic models in PET/MRI |
| **Rehabilitation engineering** | Muscle–tendon dynamics = 2nd-order TF with reflex feedback |
| **Ventilator design** | Lung mechanics model (resistance R + compliance C) drives pressure-controlled breaths |
| **Cardiac assist devices** | Afterload sensitivity of LVAD derived from Frank–Starling + Windkessel models |
| **Sports physiology** | VO₂max test interpretation uses Fick + lactate threshold models |
| **Telemedicine** | Patient-specific models for early-warning score systems |

---

## 2.9 Assumptions Commonly Made in Physiological Models (and Their Pitfalls)

| Assumption | When Valid | When It Breaks |
|---|---|---|
| Well-mixed compartment | Small organ, slow transport | Large organ, rapid injection (e.g., slug bolus in aorta) |
| Constant volume | Short time scale, small filtration | Long time scale, significant capillary leakage |
| Linear behaviour | Small perturbations around operating point | Large perturbations, saturation (e.g., Michaelis–Menten) |
| Newtonian fluid | Large vessels, high shear | Microcirculation (Fahraeus–Lindqvist effect) |
| Steady state | Slow processes, equilibrium experiments | Transient responses (exercise onset, drug bolus) |
| Single-compartment | Slow distribution, one dominant tissue | Multi-organ distribution (lipophilic drugs) |
| Constant temperature | In vitro, in controlled room | Exercise, fever, hypothermia |
| Independence of parameters | Local analysis | When systemic feedback alters parameters (Guyton) |

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU valuation pitfall:** Examiners deduct marks when students *list assumptions without justifying them*. Always pair an assumption with a brief statement of *when it is valid* and *when it fails*. This shows depth.
