## SECTION 5: KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A — 3-Mark Short-Answer Questions

**Q1. [KTU University Exam — May 2024]** Define the four primary mechanisms by which the human body loses heat to the environment. State one physiological factor that influences each.

**Model Answer:**

The four primary heat-loss mechanisms are:
1. **Radiation** — emission of infrared electromagnetic waves from the skin to cooler surrounding surfaces, governed by the Stefan–Boltzmann law. *Influencing factor:* skin blood flow (vasomotor tone) controlling $T_{sk}$.
2. **Conduction** — direct transfer of heat through physical contact, governed by Fourier's law. *Influencing factor:* thermal conductivity of the contact medium (e.g., air vs. water).
3. **Convection** — heat carried away by moving air or water across the skin, governed by Newton's law of cooling. *Influencing factor:* air velocity (forced vs. free convection).
4. **Evaporation** — heat absorbed during the phase change of water (sweat) to vapour, governed by the latent heat of vaporization. *Influencing factor:* ambient humidity and sweat gland activity.

---

**Q2. [KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]** Why is the human skin often modelled as a near-black-body (emissivity $\varepsilon \approx 0.95$) in thermoregulation models? Justify with the Stefan–Boltzmann law.

**Model Answer:**

The human skin emits infrared radiation very efficiently across the 3–50 µm band, with an emissivity of $\varepsilon \approx 0.95$ — close to a perfect black-body ($\varepsilon = 1$). This is because the skin's organic compounds (water, lipids, proteins) absorb and re-emit infrared strongly. In the Stefan–Boltzmann law $Q_r = \varepsilon \sigma A T^4$, an $\varepsilon$ of 0.95 means the skin loses ≈ 95 % of the theoretical maximum radiative heat flux. Modelling $\varepsilon < 0.95$ would underestimate radiative loss and lead to an inaccurate thermal balance.

---

### Part B — 14-Mark Questions (Internal Choice Pattern)

#### QUESTION A — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — May 2024]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Derive the linearised form of the radiative heat transfer equation used in human thermoregulation models, starting from the Stefan–Boltzmann law. Clearly state the approximation made and the resulting expression for the linearised radiation coefficient $h_r$.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A resting adult has body surface area $A = 1.7$ m², skin temperature $T_{sk} = 34\ \text{°C}$, mean radiant temperature of surroundings $T_{sur} = 22\ \text{°C}$, and skin emissivity $\varepsilon = 0.95$. Calculate (i) the linearised radiation coefficient $h_r$, and (ii) the net radiative heat loss $Q_r$ from the body. (Take $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$.)

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Step 1 — Start with the exact Stefan–Boltzmann equation:**

$$
Q_r = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_{sk}^4 - T_{sur}^4)
$$

**Step 2 — Factor the difference of fourth powers:**

$$
T_{sk}^4 - T_{sur}^4 = (T_{sk}-T_{sur})(T_{sk}+T_{sur})(T_{sk}^2+T_{sur}^2)
$$

**Step 3 — Approximate using the mean film temperature $T_m = (T_{sk}+T_{sur})/2$:**

$$
T_{sk}+T_{sur} = 2T_m, \qquad T_{sk}^2 + T_{sur}^2 \approx 2T_m^2
$$

**Step 4 — Substitute back:**

$$
T_{sk}^4 - T_{sur}^4 \approx 4T_m^3(T_{sk}-T_{sur})
$$

**Step 5 — Write the linearised equation:**

$$
Q_r = 4\varepsilon\sigma T_m^3 \cdot A \cdot (T_{sk}-T_{sur}) = h_r A (T_{sk}-T_{sur})
$$

$$
\boxed{\,h_r = 4\varepsilon\sigma T_m^3\,}
$$

**Approximation made:** $T_{sk}$ and $T_{sur}$ are close enough that their squares and fourth powers can be expanded to first order about $T_m$. This is valid because $\Delta T \ll T_m$ in human thermoregulation.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**(i) Linearised radiation coefficient:**

$$
T_{sk} = 34 + 273 = 307\ \text{K}, \qquad T_{sur} = 22 + 273 = 295\ \text{K}
$$

$$
T_m = \frac{307 + 295}{2} = 301\ \text{K}
$$

$$
h_r = 4 \times 0.95 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times (301)^3
$$

$$
(301)^3 = 2.727 \times 10^7\ \text{K}^3
$$

$$
h_r = 4 \times 0.95 \times 5.67 \times 10^{-8} \times 2.727 \times 10^7
$$

$$
h_r \approx 5.87\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}
$$

**(ii) Net radiative heat loss:**

$$
Q_r = h_r \cdot A \cdot (T_{sk} - T_{sur}) = 5.87 \times 1.7 \times (307 - 295)
$$

$$
Q_r = 5.87 \times 1.7 \times 12
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q_r \approx 119.7\ \text{W}\,}
$$

---

#### QUESTION B — 14 Marks `[KTU University Exam — Dec 2023]`

**(a) [7 Marks]** Explain, with appropriate equations, the mechanisms of heat loss by **convection** and **evaporation** from the human body. Discuss how the convective heat transfer coefficient $h_{cv}$ depends on the airflow regime.

**(b) [7 Marks]** A cyclist moving at 10 km/h has the following data: skin temperature $T_{sk} = 35\ \text{°C}$, air temperature $T_{air} = 25\ \text{°C}$, body surface area exposed to air $A = 1.5$ m², sweat rate $\dot{m}_{sw} = 0.4\ \text{g/min}$, and $h_{cv} = 15\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$ for the forced-convection regime. Estimate the convective heat loss and the evaporative heat loss. (Take $h_{fg} = 2400\ \text{kJ/kg}$ for water at skin temperature.)

**Model Solution to (a):**

**Convection:** Heat is transferred from the skin to a moving fluid (air) in contact with it. The rate of heat loss is given by Newton's law of cooling:

$$
Q_{cv} = h_{cv} \cdot A \cdot (T_{sk} - T_{air})
$$

The coefficient $h_{cv}$ is **not constant** — it depends on the flow regime:
- **Free (natural) convection:** buoyancy-driven flow, $Nu \approx 0.55 (Gr\,Pr)^{1/4}$, $h_{cv} \approx 2$–$4$ W/m²K.
- **Forced convection:** wind/external flow, $Nu \approx C\,Re^n Pr^{1/3}$ (Dittus–Boelter-type), $h_{cv} \approx 10$–$35$ W/m²K.
- The transition occurs at the critical Reynolds number, $Re_{crit} \approx 5 \times 10^5$ for flow over a flat plate.

**Evaporation:** Heat is lost when liquid water (sweat) on the skin absorbs its latent heat of vaporization and changes phase to vapour:

$$
Q_e = \dot{m}_{sw} \cdot h_{fg}
$$

This mechanism is unique because it works even when $T_{air} > T_{sk}$ (e.g., in a hot desert where the air is hotter than skin). The sweat rate is physiologically controlled by the hypothalamus via sympathetic sudomotor nerves.

---

**Model Solution to (b):**

**Convective heat loss:**

$$
\Delta T = T_{sk} - T_{air} = 35 - 25 = 10\ \text{K (or °C)}
$$

$$
Q_{cv} = h_{cv} \cdot A \cdot \Delta T = 15 \times 1.5 \times 10
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q_{cv} = 225\ \text{W}\,}
$$

**Evaporative heat loss:**

Convert sweat rate to kg/s:

$$
\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{0.4\ \text{g}}{60\ \text{s}} = 6.667 \times 10^{-6}\ \text{kg/s}
$$

Convert $h_{fg}$ to J/kg:

$$
h_{fg} = 2400\ \text{kJ/kg} = 2.4 \times 10^6\ \text{J/kg}
$$

$$
Q_e = \dot{m}_{sw} \cdot h_{fg} = 6.667 \times 10^{-6} \times 2.4 \times 10^6
$$

$$
\boxed{\,Q_e \approx 16\ \text{W}\,}
$$

**Interpretation:** Convection dominates the cyclist's heat loss (225 W) because the high air speed strips the thermal boundary layer efficiently. The evaporative loss is small here because the cyclist is not yet sweating heavily.

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls on Heat-Loss Questions:**
> 1. **Unit mismatch in $Q_r$:** Forgetting to convert °C to K before raising to the fourth power. The whole calculation collapses if $T = 35$ is plugged into $T^4$ directly. **Always: K first, then compute.**
> 2. **Confusing $h_r$ and $h_{cv}$:** Some students write the linearised radiation equation with the *convective* coefficient, or vice versa. The two are physically different mechanisms with different driving gradients.
> 3. **Sign convention errors:** In the heat balance, $M - W = \sum Q_{loss}$, losses are *positive* numbers flowing *out* of the body. Don't put a negative sign in front of $Q_r$ "because heat is leaving."
> 4. **Forgetting area in sweat problems:** The equation $Q_e = \dot{m} h_{fg}$ is in *total* W — don't divide by $A$ unless explicitly asked for *flux* (W/m²).
> 5. **Mixing up free vs. forced convection correlations:** The Nusselt–Rayleigh correlation ($Gr\,Pr$) and Nusselt–Reynolds correlation ($Re$) are **not** interchangeable. State the regime explicitly in your answer.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember

- **The four mechanisms — and the equations:**
  - **Radiation:** $Q_r = \varepsilon\sigma A (T_{sk}^4 - T_{sur}^4) \;\equiv\; h_r A (T_{sk} - T_{sur})$, with $h_r = 4\varepsilon\sigma T_m^3 \approx 5.8\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$.
  - **Conduction:** $Q_c = (kA/\Delta x)(T_{sk} - T_c)$ — small in air, dominant in water.
  - **Convection:** $Q_{cv} = h_{cv} A (T_{sk} - T_{air})$; $h_{cv} \propto v^{0.5}$ (forced) or $h_{cv} \propto \Delta T^{1/4}$ (free).
  - **Evaporation:** $Q_e = \dot{m}_{sw} h_{fg} = h_e A (P_{sk,sat} - P_{air})$; the *only* mechanism that works in hot/humid conditions.
- **Master constants:** $\sigma = 5.67 \times 10^{-8}\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}^4$, $\varepsilon_{skin} = 0.95$, $h_{fg} = 2.4 \times 10^6\ \text{J/kg}$, $A \approx 1.8\ \text{m}^2$.
- **Steady-state balance:** $M - W = Q_r + Q_c + Q_{cv} + Q_e + Q_{res}$ — at rest, ≈ 90–100 W.
- **Temperature hierarchy:** $T_{core}\ (37\ \text{°C}) > T_{sk}\ (33\text{–}35\ \text{°C}) > T_{air}\ (T_{sur})$ for heat to flow *out* of the body.
- **Physiological controllers:** Hypothalamus modulates (i) vasomotor tone → $T_{sk}$, (ii) sweat rate → $\dot{m}_{sw}$, (iii) shivering → $M$, (iv) behaviour → environment.
- **Engineering rule of thumb:** In a neutral indoor environment, the dry heat transfer coefficient is $h_{dry} = h_r + h_{cv} \approx 8$–$10\ \text{W/m}^2\text{K}$; adding insulation (clothing) multiplies this by a *clo*-dependent factor of 0.3–0.5.
- **Pitfall checklist (already covered in the warning callout):** Kelvin not Celsius, no sign confusion, $h_r \neq h_{cv}$, state convection regime, sweat in kg/s not g/min.

---

<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Models of heat transfer between subsystems of human body like skin core, body, and environment
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT864 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 3
> - **Topic:** Models of heat transfer between subsystems of human body like skin core, body, and environment

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
# 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition

**Thermoregulatory Modelling** is the mathematical representation of the heat transfer processes occurring between the human body's internal subsystems (core, muscle, fat, skin) and the external environment. It involves the formulation of energy balance equations that account for metabolic heat production, conductive/convective/radiative heat exchange, evaporative heat loss, and the active physiological control mechanisms (vasodilation, vasoconstriction, sweating, shivering).

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 Syllabus Definition (Module 3):** "Modelling of heat transfer between subsystems of human body" refers to the lumped-parameter and distributed-parameter representations of the body as a network of thermal compartments. Each compartment is characterized by its own mass, specific heat, temperature, and rate of heat exchange with neighboring compartments.

The fundamental model framework is built upon the **First Law of Thermodynamics** applied to a control volume representing a body segment:

$$C \frac{dT}{dt} = M - W - Q_{evap} - Q_{rad} - Q_{conv} - Q_{cond}$$

where $C$ is the thermal capacity $\left( \text{J/°C} \right)$, $T$ is the temperature (°C), $M$ is the metabolic heat production rate (W), $W$ is the external work (W), and $Q_{evap}$, $Q_{rad}$, $Q_{conv}$, $Q_{cond}$ are the evaporative, radiative, convective, and conductive heat losses respectively.

## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Analogy — The "House Heating System"**
> Imagine the human body as a **two-story house** with a furnace in the basement and radiators on every floor:
> - **Core (furnace + ground floor)** = The body's deep tissues (organs, brain, deep muscle) producing constant "metabolic heat" (~80–100 W for a resting adult). The core temperature is held at a very tight band of **36.5°C to 37.5°C** (this is the body's "set point").
> - **Skin (radiators on upper floor)** = The body's interface, which can "open" or "close" heat valves (blood flow). Skin temperature is *variable* (28°C to 35°C) and acts as the **radiator surface**.
> - **Pipes between floors** = The **circulatory system** (blood flow). It carries heat from the core to the skin.
> - **Outside environment (the winter night)** = Cold air, wind, and humidity. The skin loses heat by radiation to cold walls, convection to air, and evaporation of sweat.
> - **Thermostat** = The **hypothalamus** in the brain, which constantly senses core and skin temperatures and adjusts blood flow, sweat rate, and shivering.

**Key takeaway:** Just as a house is modelled as interconnected thermal zones (furnace, room, attic, outside), the human body is modelled as a network of **thermal compartments** connected by heat-transfer pathways.

## 1.3 Physical Constants and Standard Metrics

| Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | Units |
|-----------|--------|----------------|-------|
| Body core temperature | $T_{cr}$ | **37.0** | °C |
| Mean skin temperature | $T_{sk}$ | **33.0** (comfort) | °C |
| Basal metabolic rate | $M_{basal}$ | **80–100** (resting adult) | W |
| Specific heat of body tissue | $c_{tissue}$ | **3490** | J/(kg·°C) |
| Density of body tissue | $\rho$ | **1000–1060** | kg/m³ |
| Thermal conductivity of tissue | $k$ | **0.5** | W/(m·°C) |
| Blood specific heat | $c_{b}$ | **3600–3700** | J/(kg·°C) |
| Blood density | $\rho_{b}$ | **1060** | kg/m³ |
| Stefan-Boltzmann constant | $\sigma$ | **5.67 × 10⁻⁸** | W/(m²·K⁴) |
| Emissivity of skin | $\varepsilon$ | **0.95** | dimensionless |
| Stephan's wind chill index constant | $K$ | varies | (W/m²·°C·kph^(0.5)) |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU 2024 High-Yield Fact:** The body is **NOT** a single lumped mass. It is a **multi-compartment system** because core and skin temperatures differ by 2–6°C under varying environmental conditions. This difference is the *driving force* for heat transfer and MUST be modelled.

---

## 1.4 Heat Transfer Mechanisms in the Human Body

Heat moves between the body's subsystems and the environment via four classical mechanisms, all of which appear in KTU exam questions:

| Mechanism | Physical Description | KTU Favourite Equation | Direction (Cold Environment) |
|-----------|---------------------|------------------------|------------------------------|
| **Conduction** | Heat transfer through solid tissue layers (skin → fat → muscle) | $Q_{cond} = \frac{kA(T_1 - T_2)}{L}$ | Core → Skin (loss) |
| **Convection** | Heat transfer between skin surface and surrounding air/water | $Q_{conv} = h_c A (T_{sk} - T_{air})$ | Skin → Air (loss) |
| **Radiation** | Electromagnetic heat exchange between skin and surroundings | $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_{sk}^4 - T_{sur}^4)$ | Skin → Walls (loss) |
| **Evaporation** | Latent heat loss via sweat and respiration | $Q_{evap} = \dot{m}_{sw} h_{fg} + R_{resp} \cdot C_{res}(T_{ex} - T_{in})$ | Skin → Air (loss) |

> [!NOTE]
> **Conduction within the body is dominated by blood flow** (perfusion), not just tissue conduction. This is why the **Pennes' Bioheat Equation** (1948) replaces the standard conduction term with a perfusion term: a KTU-favourite equation.

---

## 1.5 Subsystems of the Human Body in Thermal Models

| Subsystem | Anatomical Equivalent | Typical Temperature | Why it is a separate compartment |
|-----------|----------------------|---------------------|----------------------------------|
| **Core** | Brain, heart, liver, deep muscle | **37.0 ± 0.5 °C** | Highly perfused, metabolically active, tightly regulated |
| **Muscle** | Skeletal muscle mass | 36–38 °C | High metabolic activity during exercise; large mass |
| **Fat layer** | Subcutaneous adipose tissue | 33–35 °C | Thermal insulation layer; low blood flow |
| **Skin** | Epidermis + dermis | 28–35 °C (variable) | Primary heat-loss interface; high temperature variability |
| **Hands & Feet** | Acral regions (arteriovenous anastomoses) | 25–33 °C | Specialized thermoregulatory blood flow control |
| **Head** | Skull contents | 37 °C | High metabolic rate; low thermal inertia in infants |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Why model the body as subsystems?**
> A single-lumped model (one temperature for the whole body) cannot explain:
> 1. Why feet are cold in winter while core is warm.
> 2. Why exercise raises core temperature by 1–2°C but skin temperature *decreases* (due to sweat evaporation).
> 3. Why a burn patient with damaged skin cannot regulate temperature even though the core is intact.
> 
> Hence, **lumped multi-node models** (Stolwijk, Gagge) and **distributed models** (Pennes) are necessary.

---

## 1.6 Historical Evolution of Thermal Models (KTU Context)

| Year | Model | Nodes | Contribution |
|------|-------|-------|--------------|
| 1948 | **Pennes** | Distributed | First bioheat equation — perfusion term |
| 1963 | **Crosbie et al.** | 1-node | Single-lump model |
| 1971 | **Stolwijk & Hardy** | 6-node | First multi-compartment model with control |
| 1976 | **Gagge et al.** | 2-node | SET* model — skin + core + setpoint |
| 1981 | **Wissler** | Multi-node | Detailed segmental model |
| 2001 | **Fiala et al.** | Multi-segment | Modern sophisticated model |

> [!TIP]
> **For KTU 2024 exams:** Focus on the **2-node (Gagge) model** and the **6-node (Stolwijk) model**, plus the **Pennes' bioheat equation**. These three are the syllabus core.

---

## 1.7 KTU 2024 Course Outcome Alignment

| CO | Description | How this topic maps |
|----|-------------|---------------------|
| **CO3** | Apply mathematical models to physiological heat transfer systems | Energy balance equation derivations |
| **CO4** | Analyze compartmental models of human thermoregulation | 2-node & 6-node analysis |
| **CO5** | Evaluate thermal models for clinical and environmental applications | Pennes' equation and clinical use |

---
<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
# 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

## 2.1 Theoretical Framework: Energy Balance

The modelling of heat transfer between body subsystems rests on the **conservation of energy principle** applied to each thermal compartment. For a generic compartment $i$ with temperature $T_i$, the energy balance is:

$$\rho_i V_i c_i \frac{dT_i}{dt} = \sum_j \text{Heat flow in from neighbours} - \sum_j \text{Heat flow out} + M_i - Q_{loss,i}$$

This is the **generalized node equation** that underlies all KTU thermal models.

## 2.2 Operational Logic — The 5 Steps of Building a Thermal Model

The following structured procedure is followed by biomedical engineers when constructing any thermoregulatory model:

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Step 1 — Compartmentalization:** Divide the body into $N$ thermal nodes (e.g., head, trunk, arms, legs, skin, core). Each node has a representative temperature $T_i$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 2 — Assign Thermal Properties:** For each node $i$, assign:
> - Mass $m_i$ (kg)
> - Specific heat $c_i$ (J/kg·°C)
> - Heat capacity $C_i = m_i c_i$ (J/°C)
> - Metabolic rate $M_i$ (W)
> - Blood perfusion rate $\dot{V}_{b,i}$ (m³/s)

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 3 — Define Heat Flow Pathways:** Between every pair of connected nodes $(i, j)$, define a conductance $K_{ij}$ (W/°C). The heat flow is $Q_{ij} = K_{ij}(T_i - T_j)$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 4 — Define Environmental Coupling:** Each node that contacts the environment has convective, radiative, and evaporative couplings. Define surface areas $A_i$, heat transfer coefficients $h_c$, $h_r$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Step 5 — Define the Control System:** The hypothalamus (controller) modulates blood flow, sweat rate, and shivering to maintain $T_{core} \approx 37°C$. The control law is typically proportional-integral (PI) or error-driven.

## 2.3 The Pennes' Bioheat Equation (1948) — Distributed Model

This is the foundational equation of bio-heat transfer. For a 1D tissue slab:

$$\rho c \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right) + \rho_b c_b w_b (T_a - T) + Q_m + Q_{ext}$$

where:
- $\rho, c, k$ = tissue density, specific heat, thermal conductivity
- $\rho_b, c_b$ = blood density, specific heat
- $w_b$ = volumetric blood perfusion rate (m³ blood / m³ tissue / s)
- $T_a$ = arterial blood temperature (≈ core temperature, **37°C**)
- $T$ = local tissue temperature
- $Q_m$ = volumetric metabolic heat generation (W/m³)
- $Q_{ext}$ = external heat source (e.g., hyperthermia applicator, W/m³)

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Insight — The "Why" of Pennes' Equation:**
> Standard Fourier conduction assumes a *static* medium. But tissue is **perfused** — blood carries heat from the core. Pennes cleverly **decoupled** the perfusion term: blood enters at $T_a$ and leaves at $T$ (local tissue temperature). This is a **sink/source** term that represents the convective heat exchange due to capillary blood flow. It is a *lumped* representation of a complex microvascular network.

### Pennes' Equation — Term-by-Term Interpretation

| Term | Physical Meaning | Typical Magnitude (muscle) |
|------|------------------|----------------------------|
| $\rho c \frac{\partial T}{\partial t}$ | Rate of heat storage in tissue | 0 (steady-state) or transient |
| $\frac{\partial}{\partial x}\left(k \frac{\partial T}{\partial x}\right)$ | Conductive heat diffusion | small in soft tissue |
| $\rho_b c_b w_b (T_a - T)$ | Perfusion — heat supplied by blood | **dominant** term in perfused tissue |
| $Q_m$ | Metabolic heat (cellular respiration) | ~700 W/m³ in resting muscle |
| $Q_{ext}$ | External heating (e.g., RF ablation, laser) | 0 unless therapy is applied |

### Steady-State Pennes' Equation

At thermal steady state $\left( \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = 0 \right)$:

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = -\frac{\rho_b c_b w_b}{k}(T_a - T) - \frac{Q_m}{k}$$

The general solution is of the form:

$$T(x) = T_a - \frac{Q_m}{k \cdot \kappa^2} + A \cosh(\kappa x) + B \sinh(\kappa x)$$

where $\kappa^2 = \frac{\rho_b c_b w_b}{k}$.

> [!WARNING]
> **Common KTU Mistake:** Students often forget to include the perfusion term and treat tissue as a *pure conductor*. Always remember: **in perfused tissue, perfusion dominates conduction by 10–100×** for high-blood-flow organs like the brain and kidney.

---

## 2.4 The 2-Node (Gagge) Model

The simplest realistic thermal model, introduced by **Gagge et al. (1976)**, represents the body as **two concentric compartments**:

1. **Core** (inner sphere): all deep tissues, organs, brain. Temperature $T_{cr}$.
2. **Skin** (outer shell): skin + subcutaneous fat. Temperature $T_{sk}$.

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical Analogy — The "Onion":** The 2-node model is like an **onion** with two layers. The inner layer (core) generates heat; the outer layer (skin) loses heat to the surroundings.

### Governing Equations — 2-Node Model

**Core compartment:**

$$C_{cr} \frac{dT_{cr}}{dt} = M - W - Q_{cr \to sk} - Q_{resp}$$

$$C_{cr} \frac{dT_{cr}}{dt} = M - W - K(T_{cr} - T_{sk}) - C_{resp}(T_{ex} - T_{in})$$

**Skin compartment:**

$$C_{sk} \frac{dT_{sk}}{dt} = Q_{cr \to sk} - Q_{sk \to env} - E_{sk}$$

$$C_{sk} \frac{dT_{sk}}{dt} = K(T_{cr} - T_{sk}) - (h_c + h_r) A (T_{sk} - T_{air}) - E_{sw} - E_{diff}$$

### Skin Compartment — Environmental Heat Loss

The total environmental heat loss from the skin is:

$$Q_{loss,env} = \left[ h_c (T_{sk} - T_{air}) + h_r (T_{sk} - T_{surr})\right] A + E_{sk}$$

where:
- $h_c$ = convective heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·°C)
- $h_r$ = linearized radiative heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·°C)
- $A$ = body surface area (m²) — typically **1.8 m²** (DuBois formula)
- $E_{sk}$ = evaporative heat loss from skin (W)
- $T_{surr}$ = mean radiant temperature of surroundings (°C)
- $T_{air}$ = ambient air temperature (°C)

### Linearized Radiation Coefficient

For small temperature differences, radiation is linearized to:

$$h_r = 4 \varepsilon \sigma (T_{sk} + T_{surr})/2)^3 \approx 4.7 \text{ W/m²·°C (typical)}$$

### Body Surface Area — DuBois Formula

$$A = 0.202 \cdot m^{0.425} \cdot h^{0.725}$$

where $m$ is body mass (kg) and $h$ is height (m).

> [!TIP]
> **For a standard adult (70 kg, 1.75 m):** $A \approx 0.202 \times 70^{0.425} \times 1.75^{0.725} \approx 1.85$ m²

### Combined Heat Loss Coefficient

Often, the dry (non-evaporative) heat loss is combined as:

$$h_{dry} = h_c + h_r$$

This gives:

$$Q_{dry} = h_{dry} A (T_{sk} - T_{air/oper})$$

where $T_{air/oper}$ is the **operative temperature**:

$$T_{op} = \frac{h_c T_{air} + h_r T_{surr}}{h_c + h_r}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Key Concept — Operative Temperature:** When radiation and convection differ, the body's "felt" temperature is the **operative temperature** $T_{op}$, a weighted average of $T_{air}$ and $T_{surr}$. This is used in HVAC and thermal comfort standards (ISO 7730, ASHRAE 55).

---

## 2.5 The 6-Node (Stolwijk & Hardy) Model

A more physiologically accurate model by **Stolwijk & Hardy (1971)**, consisting of **6 cylindrical segments** + skin:

| Node | Segment |
|------|---------|
| 1 | Head |
| 2 | Trunk |
| 3 | Arms |
| 4 | Hands |
| 5 | Legs |
| 6 | Feet |
| + | Common skin layer |

The general energy balance for each node $i$:

$$C_i \frac{dT_i}{dt} = M_i + Q_{i \to j} + Q_{i \to sk} - Q_{loss,i}$$

Inter-segmental heat flow:

$$Q_{i \to j} = K_{ij}(T_i - T_j)$$

Skin heat flow to environment:

$$Q_{sk \to env} = h A (T_{sk} - T_{air}) + \text{radiation} + \text{evaporation}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Stolwijk Model — Control System:**
> The model includes **vasomotor**, **sudomotor** (sweating), and **metabolic** control signals driven by thermal error signals:
> - Cold signal: $S_{cold} = \alpha_{cr}(T_{cr,set} - T_{cr}) + \alpha_{sk}(T_{sk,set} - T_{sk})$
> - Warm signal: $S_{warm} = \beta_{cr}(T_{cr} - T_{cr,set}) + \beta_{sk}(T_{sk} - T_{sk,set})$
> 
> Outputs: vasoconstriction, vasodilation, sweating, shivering.

---

## 2.6 Respiratory Heat Loss

Often forgotten but important for KTU:

$$Q_{resp} = \dot{V}_E \left[ c_p (T_{ex} - T_{in}) + \rho_a h_{fg} (W_{ex} - W_{in}) \right]$$

where:
- $\dot{V}_E$ = minute ventilation (L/min)
- $T_{ex}, T_{in}$ = expired, inspired air temperatures
- $W_{ex}, W_{in}$ = expired, inspired humidity ratios
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization of water (**2.43 × 10⁶ J/kg**)

In a simplified form:

$$Q_{resp} = 0.0014 M (34 - T_{air}) + 0.0173 M (5.87 - P_a)$$

where $P_a$ is ambient water vapor pressure (kPa) and $M$ is metabolic rate (W).

---

## 2.7 Evaporative Heat Loss from Skin

$$E_{sk} = E_{diff} + E_{sw}$$

- **Diffusion (insensible)**: $E_{diff} = h_e A (P_{sk,sat} - P_a)$
- **Sweating (sensible)**: $E_{sw} = \dot{m}_{sw} h_{fg} A$

where:
- $h_e$ = evaporative heat transfer coefficient (W/m²·Pa)
- $P_{sk,sat}$ = saturation vapor pressure at skin temperature (Pa)
- $P_a$ = ambient water vapor pressure (Pa)
- $\dot{m}_{sw}$ = sweat rate (kg/s·m²)
- $h_{fg}$ = latent heat of vaporization (**2.43 × 10⁶ J/kg** at 30°C)

### Lewis Relation

A common simplification in KTU problems:

$$h_e = h_c \cdot L_R$$

where $L_R \approx$ **16.5 K/kPa** is the **Lewis number** (Lewis relation). Thus:

$$E_{sk} = h_c L_R A (P_{sk,sat} - P_a)$$

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Trick:** If $T_{air} = T_{surr}$ (no radiative asymmetry), then $E_{sk}$ and $Q_{dry}$ share the same convective coefficient $h_c$.

---

## 2.8 KTU 2024 High-Yield Formula Sheet

| # | Equation | Meaning | Common Use |
|---|----------|---------|------------|
| 1 | $C \frac{dT}{dt} = M - W - Q_{evap} - Q_{dry}$ | Whole-body energy balance | First-principle model |
| 2 | $Q_{cond} = kA \frac{\Delta T}{L}$ | Fourier conduction (slab) | Pennes' equation |
| 3 | $Q_{conv} = h_c A (T_{sk} - T_{air})$ | Newton's law of cooling | Skin-air interface |
| 4 | $Q_{rad} = \varepsilon \sigma A (T_{sk}^4 - T_{surr}^4)$ | Stefan-Boltzmann radiation | Radiant exchange |
| 5 | $Q_{rad,lin} = h_r A (T_{sk} - T_{surr})$ | Linearized radiation | Combined with convection |
| 6 | $E_{sk} = h_e A (P_{sk,sat} - P_a)$ | Evaporative loss | Skin-air interface |
| 7 | $h_e = L_R h_c$ | Lewis relation | When $L_R \approx 16.5$ K/kPa |
| 8 | $C_i \frac{dT_i}{dt} = M_i + \sum_j K_{ij}(T_j - T_i) - Q_{loss,i}$ | Generic n-node balance | Stolwijk model |
| 9 | $\rho c \frac{\partial T}{\partial t} = \nabla \cdot (k \nabla T) + \rho_b c_b w_b (T_a - T) + Q_m + Q_{ext}$ | **Pennes' bioheat equation** | Distributed tissue model |
| 10 | $A_{body} = 0.202 m^{0.425} h^{0.725}$ | DuBois surface area | All skin equations |
| 11 | $T_{op} = \frac{h_c T_{air} + h_r T_{surr}}{h_c + h_r}$ | Operative temperature | Combined heat loss |
| 12 | $Q_{resp} = \dot{V}_E c_p (T_{ex} - T_{in}) + \dot{V}_E h_{fg} \rho_a (W_{ex} - W_{in})$ | Respiratory loss | Whole-body balance |

> [!NOTE]
> **Real-World Engineering Utility:**
> - **HVAC design** uses 2-node Gagge models to set building temperatures.
> - **Hyperthermia/cryotherapy** treatment planning uses Pennes' equation.
> - **Space suits, firefighting gear, diving suits** use multi-node thermal models to predict time-to-injury.
> - **Neonatal incubators** use Stolwijk-type models to maintain preterm infant thermal stability.

---

## 2.9 Steady-State Solution of 2-Node Model (Important for KTU)

At steady state, $\frac{dT_{cr}}{dt} = \frac{dT_{sk}}{dt} = 0$. The two equations reduce to:

**Core:**

$$M - W = K(T_{cr} - T_{sk}) + Q_{resp}$$

**Skin:**

$$K(T_{cr} - T_{sk}) = h_{dry} A (T_{sk} - T_{op}) + E_{sk}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Insight:** In steady state, **all metabolic heat production (minus work and respiratory loss)** is conducted from the core to the skin, and then lost to the environment. This is the thermal balance condition.

Eliminating the inter-compartment heat flow $K(T_{cr} - T_{sk})$:

$$M - W - Q_{resp} = h_{dry} A (T_{sk} - T_{op}) + E_{sk}$$

This single equation allows direct calculation of $T_{sk}$ for a given $M$, $T_{op}$, and $E_{sk}$.

> [!TIP]
> **Comfort condition:** At thermal neutrality, $E_{sk} = 0$ (no sweating) and $M - W = Q_{dry}$ (no regulatory effort). The skin temperature adjusts to satisfy this.

---
<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
# 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

## 3.1 Derivation 1: Steady-State 1D Pennes' Equation for a Tissue Slab

**Problem Statement:** A semi-infinite tissue slab of thickness $L = 0.03$ m (skin + fat) has a constant core-side surface temperature $T(0) = 37°C$ and the outer surface is exposed to air at $T(L) = T_{air}$. Find the temperature distribution at steady state.

**Given:**
- $k = 0.5$ W/m·°C
- $\rho_b = 1060$ kg/m³
- $c_b = 3700$ J/kg·°C
- $w_b = 0.0005$ m³/m³/s (resting muscle perfusion)
- $Q_m = 700$ W/m³
- $T_a = 37°C$ (arterial blood temperature)
- $T(L) = 30°C$

**Derivation — Step by Step:**

**Step 1:** Start with the 1D Pennes' equation (steady state, no external source):

$$0 = k \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \rho_b c_b w_b (T_a - T) + Q_m$$

**Step 2:** Rearrange into standard ODE form:

$$k \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - \rho_b c_b w_b (T - T_a) + Q_m = 0$$

$$k \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - \rho_b c_b w_b \cdot T + \rho_b c_b w_b T_a + Q_m = 0$$

**Step 3:** Define the perfusion constant $\kappa^2$:

$$\kappa^2 = \frac{\rho_b c_b w_b}{k}$$

Substituting values:

$$\kappa^2 = \frac{1060 \times 3700 \times 0.0005}{0.5} = \frac{1961}{0.5} = 3922 \text{ m}^{-2}$$

$$\kappa = \sqrt{3922} \approx 62.63 \text{ m}^{-1}$$

**Step 4:** The ODE becomes:

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - \kappa^2 T + \frac{\rho_b c_b w_b T_a + Q_m}{k} = 0$$

Let:

$$\Theta = \frac{\rho_b c_b w_b T_a + Q_m}{k}$$

Compute:

$$\Theta = \frac{1060 \times 3700 \times 0.0005 \times 37 + 700}{0.5} = \frac{72557 + 700}{0.5} = \frac{73257}{0.5} = 146514 \text{ °C/m}^2$$

**Step 5:** The ODE is:

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} - \kappa^2 T + \Theta = 0$$

**Step 6:** Solve the homogeneous part first: $\frac{d^2 T_h}{dx^2} - \kappa^2 T_h = 0$ gives:

$$T_h(x) = A \cosh(\kappa x) + B \sinh(\kappa x)$$

**Step 7:** Find a particular solution. Since the constant term is $\Theta$, try $T_p = C$ (constant). Substituting:

$$0 - \kappa^2 C + \Theta = 0 \implies C = \frac{\Theta}{\kappa^2} = \frac{\rho_b c_b w_b T_a + Q_m}{\rho_b c_b w_b}$$

**Step 8:** Compute the particular solution value:

$$C = \frac{1060 \times 3700 \times 0.0005 \times 37 + 700}{1060 \times 3700 \times 0.0005} = \frac{72557 + 700}{1961} = \frac{73257}{1961} = 37.357 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Physical interpretation:** The constant $C = 37.357°C$ represents the equilibrium tissue temperature in the *absence* of conduction to the surface — i.e., if the tissue was fully insulated and only perfusion + metabolism acted.

**Step 9:** General solution:

$$T(x) = A \cosh(\kappa x) + B \sinh(\kappa x) + 37.357$$

**Step 10:** Apply boundary condition at $x = 0$: $T(0) = 37°C$:

$$37 = A \cdot 1 + B \cdot 0 + 37.357 \implies A = -0.357 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 11:** Apply boundary condition at $x = L = 0.03$ m: $T(L) = 30°C$:

$$30 = -0.357 \cosh(62.63 \times 0.03) + B \sinh(62.63 \times 0.03) + 37.357$$

Compute the arguments:

$$\kappa L = 62.63 \times 0.03 = 1.879$$

$$\cosh(1.879) = \frac{e^{1.879} + e^{-1.879}}{2} = \frac{6.547 + 0.153}{2} = 3.350$$

$$\sinh(1.879) = \frac{e^{1.879} - e^{-1.879}}{2} = \frac{6.547 - 0.153}{2} = 3.197$$

**Step 12:** Substitute:

$$30 = -0.357 \times 3.350 + B \times 3.197 + 37.357$$

$$30 = -1.196 + 3.197 B + 37.357$$

$$30 - 37.357 + 1.196 = 3.197 B$$

$$-6.161 = 3.197 B \implies B = -1.927 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 13:** Final temperature profile:

$$\boxed{T(x) = -0.357 \cosh(62.63 x) - 1.927 \sinh(62.63 x) + 37.357}$$

**Step 14:** Validation: at $x = 0$, $T(0) = -0.357 + 0 + 37.357 = 37°C$ ✓; at $x = 0.03$, $T = -0.357(3.350) - 1.927(3.197) + 37.357 = -1.196 - 6.160 + 37.357 = 30.001°C$ ✓.

> [!TIP]
> **Note on exponential form:** Since $\kappa L$ is moderate (~1.88), both $\cosh$ and $\sinh$ are similar in magnitude, indicating the slab is "thermally thick" relative to the perfusion penetration depth $\delta = 1/\kappa \approx 0.016$ m.

---

## 3.2 Derivation 2: Steady-State 2-Node Gagge Model — Skin Temperature

**Problem Statement:** A resting adult is in a comfortable indoor environment. Given: $M = 80$ W (basal), $W = 0$ (no work), $Q_{resp} = 8$ W, $h_{dry} = 8$ W/m²·°C, $A = 1.8$ m², $E_{sk} = 0$ (no sweating), $T_{op} = 25°C$. Find the steady-state skin temperature $T_{sk}$ and core temperature $T_{cr}$.

**Step-by-Step Solution:**

**Step 1:** Apply the whole-body steady-state energy balance (no sweating, no respiratory humidity term):

$$M - W - Q_{resp} = h_{dry} A (T_{sk} - T_{op})$$

**Step 2:** Substitute values:

$$80 - 0 - 8 = 8 \times 1.8 \times (T_{sk} - 25)$$

$$72 = 14.4 (T_{sk} - 25)$$

**Step 3:** Solve for $T_{sk}$:

$$T_{sk} - 25 = \frac{72}{14.4} = 5.0 \implies T_{sk} = 30.0 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Comfort insight:** A skin temperature of 30°C corresponds to **thermal neutrality** — no regulatory effort is needed, and the person feels comfortable.

**Step 4:** To find $T_{cr}$, use the core energy balance:

$$M - W = K(T_{cr} - T_{sk}) + Q_{resp}$$

We need $K$, the core-to-skin conductance. A typical value is $K = 9.2$ W/°C (resting adult). Substituting:

$$80 - 0 = 9.2 (T_{cr} - 30) + 8$$

$$72 = 9.2 (T_{cr} - 30)$$

$$T_{cr} - 30 = \frac{72}{9.2} = 7.826$$

$$\boxed{T_{cr} = 37.83 \text{ °C}}$$

> [!WARNING]
> **Examination pitfall:** Many KTU students forget to include $Q_{resp}$ in the energy balance, leading to a small (~5%) error in $T_{cr}$. Always include respiratory losses.

---

## 3.3 Derivation 3: Sweat Rate Calculation in a Hot Environment

**Problem Statement:** A person exercises at $M - W = 400$ W in a hot environment where $T_{op} = 35°C$, $h_{dry} = 8$ W/m²·°C, $A = 1.8$ m², $T_{sk} = 35°C$ (skin temperature clamped by sweating), $h_{fg} = 2.43 \times 10^6$ J/kg. Compute the steady-state sweat rate (g/min).

**Step-by-Step:**

**Step 1:** Apply the steady-state energy balance. The total metabolic + skin loss must be balanced by evaporative cooling (skin is clamped at 35°C):

$$M - W = h_{dry} A (T_{sk} - T_{op}) + E_{sk}$$

**Step 2:** Compute the dry heat loss:

$$Q_{dry} = 8 \times 1.8 \times (35 - 35) = 0 \text{ W}$$

(The skin is at the operative temperature, so no net dry heat exchange.)

**Step 3:** The required evaporative loss equals the metabolic heat:

$$E_{sk} = M - W = 400 \text{ W}$$

**Step 4:** Convert to mass flow rate:

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = \frac{E_{sk}}{h_{fg}} = \frac{400}{2.43 \times 10^6} = 1.646 \times 10^{-4} \text{ kg/s}$$

**Step 5:** Convert to grams per minute:

$$\dot{m}_{sw} = 1.646 \times 10^{-4} \times 1000 \text{ g/kg} \times 60 \text{ s/min} = 9.88 \text{ g/min}$$

$$\boxed{\dot{m}_{sw} \approx 9.9 \text{ g/min}}$$

> [!TIP]
> **Clinical correlation:** Sustained sweating above ~1 L/hr (16.7 g/min) is unsustainable and leads to dehydration within 1–2 hours. This is the basis for occupational heat-stress guidelines (ACGIH, ISO 7243).

---

## 3.4 Derivation 4: Operative Temperature

**Given:** $T_{air} = 20°C$, $T_{surr} = 30°C$ (warm walls), $h_c = 3$ W/m²·°C, $h_r = 5$ W/m²·°C.

**Step 1:** Apply the definition:

$$T_{op} = \frac{h_c T_{air} + h_r T_{surr}}{h_c + h_r}$$

**Step 2:** Substitute:

$$T_{op} = \frac{3 \times 20 + 5 \times 30}{3 + 5} = \frac{60 + 150}{8} = \frac{210}{8} = 26.25 \text{ °C}$$

> [!NOTE]
> **Practical insight:** Even though air is 20°C, the radiant surfaces (walls) make the body feel as if it is in 26.25°C air. This is why cold-weather occupants near cold windows feel "drafts" — actually radiation loss to the cold surface, not air movement.

---

## 3.5 Derivation 5: Heat Transfer Coefficient from Pennes' Equation (Tissue Conductivity Enhancement)

**Concept:** In perfused tissue, the **effective thermal conductivity** is enhanced by blood flow. Show that:

$$k_{eff} = k (1 + \text{Pe})$$

where Pe is a perfusion Péclet number.

**Step 1:** Consider Pennes' equation at steady state with no metabolism:

$$0 = k \frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} + \rho_b c_b w_b (T_a - T)$$

**Step 2:** Assume a linear gradient $T(x) = T_a - Gx$. Then:

$$\frac{d^2 T}{dx^2} = 0$$

So the equation becomes:

$$0 = 0 + \rho_b c_b w_b (T_a - T_a + Gx) = \rho_b c_b w_b G x$$

This is inconsistent — linear profile is not a solution when perfusion acts.

**Step 3:** The correct asymptotic solution for thick tissue is exponential, with characteristic length $\delta = 1/\kappa$. For tissue thickness $L \gg \delta$, the effective heat transfer is dominated by perfusion, and the "effective" conductivity is:

$$k_{eff} = k \left( 1 + \frac{\rho_b c_b w_b L^2}{k} \right)$$

For $L = 0.05$ m and $w_b = 0.001$ m³/m³/s (active muscle):

$$k_{eff} = 0.5 \times \left( 1 + \frac{1060 \times 3700 \times 0.001 \times 0.0025}{0.5} \right) = 0.5 \times (1 + 19.6) = 10.3 \text{ W/m·°C}$$

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Key insight:** Perfusion can increase effective tissue conductivity by **20×** compared to passive conduction. This is why muscles and brain heat so uniformly.

---

## 3.6 Analytical Matrix: Model Selection Guide

| Scenario | Recommended Model | Why |
|----------|-------------------|-----|
| Whole-body thermal comfort study | **2-node Gagge** | Simple, fast, requires only 2 temperatures |
| Localized hyperthermia treatment | **Pennes' equation** | Spatial distribution matters; need perfusion |
| Detailed thermal regulation with shivering/sweating | **6-node Stolwijk** | Models physiological control signals |
| Exercise physiology | **Multi-node with muscle** | Muscle temperature changes independently |
| Cold-water immersion | **Pennes' with vasoconstriction** | Variable perfusion; boundary condition critical |
| Infant incubator design | **Multi-node with head detail** | Head has high surface-area-to-mass ratio |

---

## 3.7 Worked Example: Combined Heat Loss in a Room

**Given:**
- $T_{air} = 24°C$, $T_{surr} = 24°C$, $T_{sk} = 33°C$
- $A = 1.8$ m², $h_c = 4$ W/m²·°C, $h_r = 5$ W/m²·°C
- Subject is at rest, $M - W = 80$ W, no sweating, $Q_{resp} = 8$ W

**Step 1:** Compute dry heat loss:

$$Q_{dry} = (4 + 5) \times 1.8 \times (33 - 24) = 9 \times 1.8 \times 9 = 145.8 \text{ W}$$

**Step 2:** Compare to metabolic production:

$$M - W - Q_{resp} = 80 - 8 = 72 \text{ W}$$

**Step 3:** Imbalance: $Q_{dry} = 145.8$ W > $72$ W. The body is **losing more heat** than producing.

**Step 4:** Body responds by **vasoconstriction**, reducing $K$ (core-skin conductance) to retain heat. New steady-state $T_{sk}$ satisfies:

$$72 = 9 \times 1.8 \times (T_{sk} - 24)$$

$$T_{sk} = 24 + \frac{72}{16.2} = 24 + 4.44 = 28.44 \text{ °C}$$

**Step 5:** Interpretation: Skin cools to 28.4°C, person feels slightly cool but stable.

> [!TIP]
> **KTU 2024 Question Pattern:** The examiner will often give you an environmental scenario and ask you to **predict the regulatory response** and **new steady-state temperatures**. Practice this!

## 4.7 Comparison Table: KTU Thermal Models at a Glance

| Model | Nodes | Equations | Best For | Limitations |
|-------|-------|-----------|----------|-------------|
| **1-node Lumped** | 1 | 1 ODE | Crude estimates | Ignores core-skin difference |
| **2-node Gagge** | 2 | 2 ODEs | Thermal comfort, HVAC | No segmental detail |
| **6-node Stolwijk** | 6 | 6 ODEs | Exercise, cold stress | Complex, many parameters |
| **Pennes' distributed** | ∞ | 1 PDE | Local heating, hyperthermia | Needs spatial data, perfusion map |
| **Multi-segment Fiala** | 15+ | 15+ ODEs | Research, individual-specific | High computational cost |
