INCREASED O2 AFFINITY] P50L --> LOAD[ENHANCED O2 LOADING
AT LUNGS] ``` ## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap ### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each) **Q1. [KTU University Exam – May 2024]** **Define Fick's First Law of Diffusion. State its mathematical form and explain its role in modelling oxygen transport across the alveolar-capillary membrane.** **Model Answer:** Fick's First Law of Diffusion states that the flux of a diffusing species across a membrane is proportional to the concentration gradient along the direction of diffusion, and is driven from high to low concentration. $$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$ where $J$ is the molar flux $[\text{mol}/(\text{cm}^2 \cdot \text{s})]$, $D$ is the diffusion coefficient $[\text{cm}^2/\text{s}]$, and $\dfrac{dC}{dx}$ is the concentration gradient. In the alveolar-capillary membrane model, the integrated form gives the diffusive oxygen transfer rate: $$\dot{V}_{O_2} = D_L \cdot (P_{AO_2} - P_{bO_2})$$ This relates the rate of $O_2$ transfer to the partial-pressure gradient between alveolar gas and capillary blood, with $D_L$ being the **lung diffusing capacity**. *(3 marks — full credit for the law statement, equation, and the lung-specific application.)* --- **Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]** **What is Hüfner's constant? Why is it important in computing the oxygen-carrying capacity of blood?** **Model Answer:** Hüfner's constant ($\kappa_{Hb} = 1.34 \text{ mL } O_2 \text{ per gram of hemoglobin}$) is the theoretical maximum volume of oxygen that can bind to 1 gram of fully saturated hemoglobin. It arises because each Hb tetramer has 4 binding sites, each holding one $O_2$ molecule. It is essential in the total oxygen content equation: $$C_{O_2} = 1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S + 0.003 \cdot P_{O_2}$$ Without this constant, one cannot quantify the chemically bound (and dominant) fraction of $O_2$ in arterial blood, nor assess clinical conditions such as **anemia** (low $[Hb]$) or **CO poisoning** (reduced effective $\kappa_{Hb}$). *(3 marks — full credit for value, derivation context, and clinical relevance.)* --- ### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each — Internal Choice) **Q3. [KTU University Exam – May 2024 — Internal Choice]** **Question A (14 Marks):** **(a)** Derive the steady-state oxygen diffusion equation across the alveolar-capillary membrane using Fick's First Law. Define and explain the significance of the **lung diffusing capacity $D_L$**. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A healthy subject has $P_{AO_2} = 95 \text{ mmHg}$, $D_L = 21 \text{ mL}/(\text{min} \cdot \text{mmHg})$, $[Hb] = 15 \text{ g}/100\text{mL}$, and arterial saturation $S_a = 0.97$. Calculate the **arterial oxygen content $C_{aO_2}$** and the **whole-body $O_2$ consumption** if cardiac output is $5 \text{ L/min}$ and mixed-venous saturation is $0.75$. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer for (a):** Starting from Fick's First Law in 1-D steady state: $$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$ For a homogeneous membrane slab of thickness $T$ and cross-section $A$, with $O_2$ dissolving with effective solubility $K$: $$J = \frac{D \cdot K}{T} \cdot (P_1 - P_2)$$ Multiplying by the surface area $A$ to obtain volumetric transfer rate: $$\dot{V}_{O_2} = \frac{D \cdot K \cdot A}{T} \cdot (P_{AO_2} - P_{bO_2})$$ Defining the **lung diffusing capacity** $D_L = \dfrac{D \cdot K \cdot A}{T}$: $$\dot{V}_{O_2} = D_L \cdot (P_{AO_2} - P_{bO_2})$$ **Significance of $D_L$:** - It is an *integrated property* that bundles three physical parameters — diffusivity $D$, solubility $K$, and geometry $A/T$. - A *high* $D_L$ indicates efficient gas exchange (e.g., during exercise when capillary recruitment increases $A$). - A *low* $D_L$ indicates diffusion impairment, as in pulmonary fibrosis, emphysema, or pulmonary edema. - Typical normal value: $D_L \approx 21 \text{ mL } O_2 / (\text{min} \cdot \text{mmHg})$. *(7 marks — 4 for derivation, 3 for the conceptual significance.)* **Model Answer for (b):** **Step 1 — Arterial $O_2$ content:** Using the total oxygen content equation: $$C_{aO_2} = 1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S_a + 0.003 \cdot P_{aO_2}$$ $$C_{aO_2} = 1.34 \cdot 15 \cdot 0.97 + 0.003 \cdot 95$$ $$C_{aO_2} = 19.50 + 0.285 = 19.78 \text{ mL } O_2 / 100\text{mL blood}$$ **Step 2 — Venous $O_2$ content:** Assuming $P_{vO_2} = 40 \text{ mmHg}$ for $S_v = 0.75$: $$C_{vO_2} = 1.34 \cdot 15 \cdot 0.75 + 0.003 \cdot 40$$ $$C_{vO_2} = 15.075 + 0.12 = 15.20 \text{ mL } O_2 / 100\text{mL blood}$$ **Step 3 — Arteriovenous $O_2$ difference:** $$C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2} = 19.78 - 15.20 = 4.58 \text{ mL } O_2 / 100\text{mL blood}$$ **Step 4 — Whole-body $O_2$ consumption (Fick's Principle):** $$\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{Q} \cdot (C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2})$$ Converting cardiac output to consistent units: $\dot{Q} = 5000 \text{ mL blood/min}$. Since $C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2}$ is per 100 mL blood: $$\dot{V}_{O_2} = 5000 \text{ mL/min} \cdot \frac{4.58 \text{ mL } O_2}{100 \text{ mL blood}}$$ $$\dot{V}_{O_2} = 5000 \cdot 0.0458 = 229 \text{ mL } O_2 / \text{min}$$ **Result:** $\dot{V}_{O_2} \approx 229 \text{ mL } O_2 / \text{min}$, which is consistent with normal resting values (250 mL/min). *(7 marks — 2 for arterial content, 2 for venous content, 2 for Fick's principle calculation, 1 for units consistency.)* --- **Question B (14 Marks — Alternative Choice):** **(a)** Explain the **Hill equation** for oxyhemoglobin equilibrium. State the values of its two key parameters and describe how a **right-shift of the dissociation curve** (Bohr effect) affects oxygen unloading at the tissues. **(7 marks)** **(b)** A patient is admitted with $P_{aO_2} = 60 \text{ mmHg}$ and $P_{aCO_2} = 50 \text{ mmHg}$. Using the Hill equation ($n = 2.7$, $P_{50} = 26.6 \text{ mmHg}$ at standard pH 7.4), estimate the **shifted $P_{50}$** due to the Bohr effect (assume $\Delta \log P_{50} \approx -0.48 \cdot \Delta pH$ and that the pH change is approximately $-0.05$ for the given $P_{aCO_2}$ rise). Then compute the **arterial saturation** at the new $P_{50}$. **(7 marks)** **Model Answer for (a):** The Hill equation describes the **fractional saturation $S$** of hemoglobin as a function of the local $P_{O_2}$: $$S = \frac{P_{O_2}^n}{P_{50}^n + P_{O_2}^n}$$ where $P_{50} \approx 26.6 \text{ mmHg}$ (the $P_{O_2}$ at which $S = 0.5$) and $n \approx 2.7$ (the Hill coefficient, reflecting cooperativity of the 4 binding sites; pure non-cooperative binding would give $n = 1$). **Bohr Effect — Right-Shift Mechanism:** - When tissue $P_{CO_2}$ rises (and pH falls) and temperature rises, the Hb molecule's quaternary structure shifts toward the **T (tense, low-affinity) state**. - The result is a **right-shift** of the dissociation curve: $P_{50}$ increases (e.g., from 26.6 to ~30 mmHg). - For a given local tissue $P_{O_2} \approx 40 \text{ mmHg}$, the saturation $S$ drops, meaning **more oxygen is unloaded** to the tissues. - Quantitatively: $\Delta \log P_{50} \approx -0.48 \cdot \Delta pH$. *(7 marks — 3 for Hill equation, 2 for parameter values, 2 for Bohr mechanism.)* **Model Answer for (b):** **Step 1 — Calculate the new $P_{50}$:** Using the Bohr equation: $$\Delta \log_{10} P_{50} = -0.48 \cdot \Delta pH = -0.48 \cdot (-0.05) = 0.024$$ $$\log_{10} P_{50}^{new} = \log_{10}(26.6) + 0.024 = 1.4249 + 0.024 = 1.4489$$ $$P_{50}^{new} = 10^{1.4489} \approx 28.1 \text{ mmHg}$$ **Step 2 — Compute the arterial saturation at $P_{aO_2} = 60 \text{ mmHg}$:** $$S_a = \frac{60^{2.7}}{28.1^{2.7} + 60^{2.7}}$$ Compute $60^{2.7}$: $$60^{2.7} = e^{2.7 \cdot \ln 60} = e^{2.7 \cdot 4.0943} = e^{11.0546} = 63{,}107$$ Compute $28.1^{2.7}$: $$28.1^{2.7} = e^{2.7 \cdot \ln 28.1} = e^{2.7 \cdot 3.3358} = e^{9.0067} = 8{,}180$$ Now compute $S_a$: $$S_a = \frac{63{,}107}{8{,}180 + 63{,}107} = \frac{63{,}107}{71{,}287} = 0.885$$ **Result:** $S_a \approx 0.885$ (or 88.5%). **Comparison:** Without the Bohr shift, using $P_{50} = 26.6$ mmHg, $S_a$ would be: $$S_a^{std} = \frac{63{,}107}{26.6^{2.7} + 63{,}107} = \frac{63{,}107}{5852 + 63{,}107} = 0.915$$ The right-shift due to elevated $P_{aCO_2}$ has **reduced arterial saturation by ~3%**, which corresponds to a substantial loss in oxygen delivery to tissues. *(7 marks — 2 for Bohr shift calculation, 2 for Hill computation, 1 for final $S_a$, 2 for comparison/discussion.)* --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Unit Conversion Error:** Forgetting to scale $[Hb]$ (typically in g/100mL) and $C_{O_2}$ (in mL/100mL) when computing $\dot{V}_{O_2}$. Always divide by 100 or multiply by 0.01 explicitly. > 2. **Mixing dissolved and bound terms:** Students often compute the dissolved $O_2$ using $\alpha_{O_2} = 0.003$ but forget the second term in $C_{O_2} = 1.34[Hb]S + 0.003 P_{O_2}$. > 3. **Confusing P50 with PO2:** P50 is a constant (26.6 mmHg for normal adult Hb), $P_{O_2}$ is the variable you are solving for. > 4. **Sign of Bohr Shift:** The right-shift INCREASES P50 (decreases affinity). A common error is to write that it decreases P50. > 5. **D_L vs D:** Many students interchange the molecular diffusivity $D$ (units $\text{cm}^2/\text{s}$) and the lung diffusing capacity $D_L$ (units $\text{mL}/(\text{min} \cdot \text{mmHg})$). They are different physical quantities. ### 5.2 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember > [!IMPORTANT] > **High-Density Rapid-Revision Checklist** - **Fick's First Law:** $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{dC}{dx}$ — flux proportional to concentration gradient, with diffusivity $D$ as the proportionality constant. - **Fick's Principle (whole body):** $\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{Q} \cdot (C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2})$ — used to measure cardiac output clinically. - **Total $O_2$ content equation:** $C_{O_2} = 1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S + 0.003 \cdot P_{O_2}$ — the single most important equation of the topic. - **Hüfner's constant:** $\kappa_{Hb} = 1.34 \text{ mL } O_2 / \text{g Hb}$ — the cornerstone of the bound-$O_2$ term. - **Henry's Law constant for $O_2$:** $\alpha_{O_2} = 0.003 \text{ mL}/(100\text{mL} \cdot \text{mmHg})$ — the cornerstone of the dissolved-$O_2$ term. - **Lung diffusing capacity:** $D_L \approx 21 \text{ mL } O_2 / (\text{min} \cdot \text{mmHg})$ — bundles $D$, $K$, $A$, and $T$. - **Hill equation:** $S = \dfrac{P_{O_2}^n}{P_{50}^n + P_{O_2}^n}$ — with $n \approx 2.7$ and $P_{50} \approx 26.6 \text{ mmHg}$. - **Bohr effect (right-shift):** ↑$P_{CO_2}$, ↓pH, ↑temperature → ↑$P_{50}$ → ↓$O_2$ affinity → better tissue unloading. - **Bohr shift equation:** $\Delta \log P_{50} \approx -0.48 \cdot \Delta pH$ — quantitative rule of thumb. - **Capillary transit time:** $t_{transit} = V_c / \dot{Q} \approx 0.75 \text{ s}$ at rest. - **Pulmonary diffusive equilibration occurs within ~0.25 s** — leaving a safety margin of ~0.5 s for exercise. - **Three coupled sub-models:** (1) Fickian diffusion, (2) Hill binding, (3) convective plug flow — every KTU 14-mark question will demand at least two of these. - **Krogh Cylinder Model:** Used for tissue-side $O_2$ delivery (peripheral to the pulmonary focus, but often co-examined). - **Normal ranges to remember:** $P_{AO_2} = 100$, $P_{aO_2} = 95$, $P_{vO_2} = 40$, $P_{50} = 26.6$, $S_a = 0.97$, $S_v = 0.75$ — all in mmHg (pressure) or dimensionless (saturation). - **Fick's principle FAVOURITE exam trick:** The arteriovenous $O_2$ difference multiplied by cardiac output gives $\dot{V}_{O_2}$, but examiners often reverse the question — given $\dot{V}_{O_2}$ and the AV difference, *find* the cardiac output. Be ready for both directions.

topics.title: Mass balancing by lungs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME**
> - **Course:** PEBMT864 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS
> - **Module:** Module 4
> - **Topic:** Mass balancing by lungs

<!-- SECTION_1_START -->
## 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview

### Formal Academic Definition
**Mass balancing by lungs** is the application of the *conservation of mass* principle to the pulmonary system, wherein the rate of accumulation of a respiratory gas (O₂, CO₂, or N₂) within the alveolar compartment equals the difference between the rate of mass inflow via inspired air and pulmonary circulation, and the rate of mass outflow via expired air and pulmonary circulation. Mathematically, it is expressed as:

$$\text{Rate of Accumulation} = \text{Mass In} - \text{Mass Out} \pm \text{Generation/Consumption}$$

In steady-state physiological conditions (typical resting adult), the accumulation term reduces to zero, giving a **steady-state mass balance** that is the foundation of clinical spirometry, indirect calorimetry, and metabolic ward measurements.

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **KTU Syllabus Highlight:** This topic is a direct application of **Module 4 – Physiological Modeling Using Mass Balance**, specifically the *respiratory subsystem*. The lungs act as a **well-mixed continuous stirred tank reactor (CSTR)** in classical chemical engineering analogy.

### Conceptual Analogy / Intuition
Imagine the lungs as a **water tank with two inlet pipes and two outlet pipes**:
- **Inlet 1 (Atmospheric Air):** brings in fresh O₂ during inspiration.
- **Inlet 2 (Venous Blood):** delivers CO₂-rich, O₂-poor blood from the body.
- **Outlet 1 (Expired Air):** removes CO₂-rich, O₂-poor air during expiration.
- **Outlet 2 (Arterial Blood):** carries O₂-rich, CO₂-poor blood to tissues.

The **"mass balance"** is simply asking: *"How much gas enters vs. how much leaves the tank, and is anything accumulating or depleting inside?"* If inflows equal outflows, the system is in **steady state** — the lungs are neither bloating with CO₂ nor emptying of O₂.

> [!NOTE]
> **Key Constants Used in Lung Mass Balance:**
> - Barometric Pressure: **P_B = 760 mmHg** (at sea level)
> - Water Vapour Pressure in Alveoli: **P_H₂O = 47 mmHg**
> - Respiratory Quotient (RQ): **RQ = 0.8** (for mixed diet)
> - Inspired O₂ Fraction: **F_IO₂ = 0.21**
> - Inspired CO₂ Fraction: **F_ICO₂ = 0.0003** (≈ 0)

<!-- SECTION_1_END -->

<!-- SECTION_2_START -->
## 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet

### 2.1 Governing Principle: Fick's Principle Applied to Lungs
Wilbur Atlee Fick (1870) proposed that the rate of gas uptake by an organ equals the blood flow multiplied by the arteriovenous concentration difference. For the **whole lung** (treating it as a single exchange organ):

$$\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{Q} \times (C_{a,O_2} - C_{v,O_2})$$

$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} = \dot{Q} \times (C_{v,CO_2} - C_{a,CO_2})$$

Where:
- $\dot{V}_{O_2}$ = rate of oxygen consumption (mL/min)
- $\dot{V}_{CO_2}$ = rate of carbon dioxide production (mL/min)
- $\dot{Q}$ = cardiac output (L/min)
- $C_{a}$, $C_{v}$ = arterial and venous blood gas concentrations (mL/dL)

### 2.2 The Two Mass Balance Frameworks
The lungs can be mass-balanced in **two parallel frameworks**:

| Framework | Inlet Streams | Outlet Streams | Net Balance |
|-----------|---------------|----------------|-------------|
| **Ventilatory (Air-side)** | Inspired Air (V̇_I) | Expired Air (V̇_E) | O₂ consumed, CO₂ produced |
| **Perfusion (Blood-side)** | Venous Blood (Q̇) | Arterial Blood (Q̇) | O₂ added, CO₂ removed |

### 2.3 Alveolar Gas Equation (Simplified Form)
The partial pressure of alveolar O₂ is:

$$P_{A,O_2} = F_{I,O_2}(P_B - P_{H_2O}) - \frac{P_{A,CO_2}}{RQ}$$

And by mass balance on CO₂ (alveolar ventilation equation):

$$P_{A,CO_2} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2} \times K}{\dot{V}_A}$$

where $K = 0.863$ is a unit conversion constant.

### 2.4 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet

| # | Formula | Description | Units |
|---|---------|-------------|-------|
| 1 | $\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{V}_I F_{I,O_2} - \dot{V}_E F_{E,O_2}$ | O₂ ventilatory balance | mL/min |
| 2 | $\dot{V}_{CO_2} = \dot{V}_E F_{E,CO_2} - \dot{V}_I F_{I,CO_2}$ | CO₂ ventilatory balance | mL/min |
| 3 | $RQ = \dot{V}_{CO_2} / \dot{V}_{O_2}$ | Respiratory Quotient | dimensionless |
| 4 | $P_{A,CO_2} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2} \times 0.863}{\dot{V}_A}$ | Alveolar CO₂ tension | mmHg |
| 5 | $P_{A,O_2} = F_{I,O_2}(P_B - P_{H_2O}) - \frac{P_{A,CO_2}}{R}$ | Alveolar O₂ tension | mmHg |
| 6 | $\dot{V}_A = (\dot{V}_E - \dot{V}_D) \times f$ | Alveolar ventilation | L/min |
| 7 | $\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{Q} \times (C_{a,O_2} - C_{v,O_2})$ | Fick's principle (lung) | mL/min |
| 8 | $V_{O_2} = 3.5 \times \text{Body Weight (kg)}$ | Resting O₂ consumption (~3.5 mL/kg/min) | mL/min |

> [!IMPORTANT]
> **Engineering Real-World Utility:** This mass-balance framework is the *exact same mathematics* used to design **bioreactors, scrubbers, and chemical plant heat exchangers**. The lungs are the canonical example of a *gas-liquid contacting CSTR* in biomedical engineering pedagogy.

<!-- SECTION_2_END -->

<!-- SECTION_3_START -->
## 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix

### 3.1 Derivation: Steady-State O₂ Mass Balance Over the Lungs (Air Side)

**Step 1 — Define a control volume** around the alveolar region.
- Inlet: inspired air with flow $\dot{V}_I$ (L/min, BTPS) and O₂ fraction $F_{I,O_2}$.
- Outlet: expired air with flow $\dot{V}_E$ and O₂ fraction $F_{E,O_2}$.
- Internal sink: O₂ is consumed by the blood at rate $\dot{V}_{O_2}$.

**Step 2 — Apply conservation of mass to O₂.**

$$\frac{dm_{O_2,alv}}{dt} = \dot{n}_{O_2,in} - \dot{n}_{O_2,out} - \dot{V}_{O_2}$$

**Step 3 — Convert volumetric flows to molar flows using the ideal gas law.**
At standard conditions, $1$ L of gas $\equiv 1/22.4$ mol, so volumetric O₂ flow = $\dot{V} \times F_{O_2}$ in L/min. Working in volumetric units (BTPS) we drop the molar conversion:

$$\frac{dm_{O_2,alv}}{dt} = \dot{V}_I F_{I,O_2} - \dot{V}_E F_{E,O_2} - \dot{V}_{O_2}$$

**Step 4 — Apply the steady-state assumption** (resting adult, alveolar gas composition not changing with time):

$$0 = \dot{V}_I F_{I,O_2} - \dot{V}_E F_{E,O_2} - \dot{V}_{O_2}$$

**Step 5 — Solve for O₂ consumption:**

$$\boxed{\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{V}_I F_{I,O_2} - \dot{V}_E F_{E,O_2}}$$

### 3.2 Derivation: Alveolar Ventilation Equation for CO₂

**Step 1** — Mass balance on CO₂ over alveoli (no internal generation; only exchange with blood):

$$0 = \dot{V}_I F_{I,CO_2} + \dot{V}_{CO_2,\text{from blood}} - \dot{V}_E F_{E,CO_2}$$

**Step 2** — Recognize that the rate at which blood delivers CO₂ to alveoli equals the systemic CO₂ production $\dot{V}_{CO_2}$ (Fick's principle applied systemically). Inspired CO₂ is negligible ($F_{I,CO_2} \approx 0$):

$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} = \dot{V}_E F_{E,CO_2}$$

**Step 3** — Express expired flow as alveolar plus dead-space: $\dot{V}_E = \dot{V}_A + \dot{V}_D$. Dead space contains inspired air with negligible CO₂, so $F_{E,CO_2} = F_{A,CO_2} \times \dot{V}_A / \dot{V}_E$:

$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} = \dot{V}_A \cdot F_{A,CO_2}$$

**Step 4** — Convert fraction to partial pressure: $F_{A,CO_2} = P_{A,CO_2} / (P_B - P_{H_2O})$:

$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} = \dot{V}_A \cdot \frac{P_{A,CO_2}}{P_B - P_{H_2O}}$$

**Step 5** — Solve for $P_{A,CO_2}$, absorbing the unit conversion into constant $K$:

$$P_{A,CO_2} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2} (P_B - P_{H_2O})}{\dot{V}_A} \cdot \frac{1}{K_{\text{conv}}}$$

For SI (mL, mmHg, min) units, $K = 0.863$:

$$\boxed{P_{A,CO_2} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2} \times 0.863}{\dot{V}_A}}$$

### 3.3 Worked Numerical Example (KTU-Style)

**Problem:** A 70 kg adult at rest has $\dot{V}_E = 7.5$ L/min, respiratory rate $f = 15$ breaths/min, tidal volume $V_T = 500$ mL, dead space $V_D = 150$ mL, and RQ = 0.8. Barometric pressure = 760 mmHg, $P_{H_2O} = 47$ mmHg. Calculate (a) alveolar ventilation, (b) $P_{A,CO_2}$, and (c) $P_{A,O_2}$.

**Solution:**

**(a) Alveolar ventilation:**

$$\dot{V}_A = (V_T - V_D) \times f = (500 - 150) \times 15 = 5250 \text{ mL/min} = 5.25 \text{ L/min}$$

**(b) $P_{A,CO_2}$:** First, compute $\dot{V}_{O_2}$ from Fick's principle estimate:
$$\dot{V}_{O_2} = 3.5 \times 70 = 245 \text{ mL/min}$$
Then:
$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} = RQ \times \dot{V}_{O_2} = 0.8 \times 245 = 196 \text{ mL/min}$$

$$P_{A,CO_2} = \frac{196 \times 0.863}{5.25} = 32.2 \text{ mmHg}$$

**(c) $P_{A,O_2}$** (using $F_{I,O_2} = 0.21$):

$$P_{A,O_2} = 0.21 \times (760 - 47) - \frac{32.2}{0.8} = 149.97 - 40.25 = 109.7 \text{ mmHg}$$

These values match the clinically observed normal alveolar gas tensions (P_A,O₂ ≈ 100 mmHg, P_A,CO₂ ≈ 40 mmHg). ✓

<!-- SECTION_3_END -->

<!-- SECTION_4_START -->
## 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics

### 4.1 Mermaid Block Diagram: Lung Mass Balance Architecture

```mermaid
flowchart LR
    ATM["ATMOSPHERE\nPi O2 = 160 mmHg\nPi CO2 = 0.3 mmHg"] -->|"VI , Inspiratory Flow"| ALV
    VEN["VENOUS BLOOD\nPv O2 = 40 mmHg\nPv CO2 = 46 mmHg"] -->|"Q, Cardiac Output"| ALV
    ALV["ALVEOLAR COMPARTMENT\n(CSTR - Well Mixed)\nPA O2 ~ 100 mmHg\nPA CO2 ~ 40 mmHg"] -->|"VE, Expiratory Flow"| EXP
    ALV -->|"Q, Cardiac Output"| ART
    EXP["EXPIRED AIR\nPE O2 = 120 mmHg\nPE CO2 = 27 mmHg"]
    ART["ARTERIAL BLOOD\nPa O2 = 95 mmHg\nPa CO2 = 40 mmHg"]
    MET["MASS BALANCE EQUATION\ndM/dt = Min - Mout +/- R\nSteady State: 0 = Min - Mout +/- R"] -.-> ALV
```

### 4.2 Mermaid Flowchart: Step-by-Step Mass Balance Methodology

```mermaid
flowchart TD
    A["Step 1: Define Control Volume\n(Alveolar Region)"] --> B["Step 2: Identify All Inlet Streams\nInspired Air, Venous Blood"]
    B --> C["Step 3: Identify All Outlet Streams\nExpired Air, Arterial Blood"]
    C --> D["Step 4: Identify Internal Source/Sink\nO2 Consumed, CO2 Produced"]
    D --> E["Step 5: Write Conservation Equation\ndM/dt = Min - Mout +/- R"]
    E --> F{"Step 6: Steady State?"}
    F -- Yes --> G["Set dM/dt = 0\nSolve for Unknown"]
    F -- No --> H["Keep dM/dt term\nIntegrate over time"]
    G --> I["Step 7: Apply Ideal Gas Law\nConvert to Partial Pressures"]
    I --> J["Step 8: Validate Against\nClinical Norms"]
```

### 4.3 Mermaid Schematic: Two-Framework Coupling

```mermaid
flowchart TB
    subgraph VS["VENTILATORY FRAMEWORK (Air-Side)"]
        V1["Inspired O2 Load\nVI x FI O2"] --> V2["Alveolar Exchange\nO2 Diffusion"]
        V2 --> V3["Expired O2 Load\nVE x FE O2"]
        V4["Inspired CO2 ~ 0"] --> V5["Alveolar Exchange\nCO2 Diffusion"]
        V5 --> V6["Expired CO2 Load\nVE x FE CO2"]
    end
    subgraph PS["PERFUSION FRAMEWORK (Blood-Side)"]
        P1["Venous Blood\nQ x Cv O2"] --> P2["Alveolar Capillary\ngas exchange"]
        P2 --> P3["Arterial Blood\nQ x Ca O2"]
        P4["Venous Blood\nQ x Cv CO2"] --> P5["Alveolar Capillary\ngas exchange"]
        P5 --> P6["Arterial Blood\nQ x Ca CO2"]
    end
    V2 -.->|"Fick's Law coupling"| P2
    V5 -.->|"Fick's Law coupling"| P5
```

<!-- SECTION_4_END -->

<!-- SECTION_5_START -->
## 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap

### Part A Questions (3 Marks Each)

**Q1. [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**
*State Fick's principle of mass balance as applied to the lungs. List the variables involved.*

**Model Answer:**
Fick's principle states that the rate of uptake or release of a gas by an organ equals the blood flow to that organ multiplied by the arteriovenous concentration difference of the gas. For the lungs, applied to oxygen:
$$\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{Q} \times (C_{a,O_2} - C_{v,O_2})$$
The variables are: $\dot{V}_{O_2}$ (O₂ consumption, mL/min), $\dot{Q}$ (cardiac output, L/min), and $C_{a,O_2}$, $C_{v,O_2}$ (arterial and venous O₂ content, mL/dL). (3 marks)

**Q2. [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**
*Define the Respiratory Quotient (RQ). What is its typical resting value and what does it indicate?*

**Model Answer:**
RQ is the molar ratio of CO₂ produced to O₂ consumed by the body:
$$RQ = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2}}{\dot{V}_{O_2}}$$
The typical resting value on a mixed diet is **0.8** (range 0.7–1.0). RQ = 1.0 indicates pure carbohydrate metabolism, RQ ≈ 0.7 indicates pure fat metabolism, and RQ > 1.0 indicates lipogenesis (fat synthesis from carbohydrate). (3 marks)

---

### Part B Questions (14 Marks Each)

> **Note:** As per KTU ESE pattern, *answer ANY ONE* from the following two sub-questions.

#### **Question A (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – June 2024]**

**(a)** Derive the steady-state mass balance equation for oxygen across the lungs using the alveolar compartment as the control volume. Clearly state all assumptions. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

*Assumptions:*
1. Steady state (alveolar gas composition constant with time).
2. Inspired CO₂ is negligible.
3. Lungs are a well-mixed compartment (CSTR analogue).
4. Ideal gas behaviour.

*Derivation:*
Step 1: Define control volume around alveoli.
Step 2: Inlet O₂ molar rate = $\dot{V}_I F_{I,O_2}$.
Step 3: Outlet O₂ molar rate = $\dot{V}_E F_{E,O_2}$.
Step 4: Internal sink (consumption) = $\dot{V}_{O_2}$.
Step 5: General mass balance: $\frac{dm_{O_2}}{dt} = \text{In} - \text{Out} - \text{Consumption}$
Step 6: Apply steady state:
$$0 = \dot{V}_I F_{I,O_2} - \dot{V}_E F_{E,O_2} - \dot{V}_{O_2}$$
Step 7: Final form:
$$\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{V}_I F_{I,O_2} - \dot{V}_E F_{E,O_2}$$
**(7 marks — full mark for each numbered step and boxed final equation)**

**(b)** A patient on a mechanical ventilator has the following measurements: $\dot{V}_I = 6.0$ L/min, $\dot{V}_E = 5.7$ L/min, $F_{I,O_2} = 0.40$, $F_{E,O_2} = 0.34$, $F_{E,CO_2} = 0.05$. Calculate (i) O₂ consumption rate, (ii) CO₂ production rate, and (iii) respiratory quotient. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**(i) O₂ consumption rate:**
$$\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{V}_I F_{I,O_2} - \dot{V}_E F_{E,O_2}$$
$$\dot{V}_{O_2} = (6.0)(0.40) - (5.7)(0.34) = 2.40 - 1.938 = 0.462 \text{ L/min}$$
$$\dot{V}_{O_2} \approx 462 \text{ mL/min}$$

**(ii) CO₂ production rate:**
Assuming $F_{I,CO_2} \approx 0$:
$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} = \dot{V}_E F_{E,CO_2} - \dot{V}_I F_{I,CO_2} = (5.7)(0.05) - 0 = 0.285 \text{ L/min}$$
$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} \approx 285 \text{ mL/min}$$

**(iii) Respiratory Quotient:**
$$RQ = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2}}{\dot{V}_{O_2}} = \frac{285}{462} = 0.617$$
This RQ of 0.62 is below the normal range and suggests predominant fat metabolism (e.g., prolonged fasting or diabetic ketoacidosis). **(7 marks — 2 + 2 + 3 for clinical interpretation)**

---

#### **Question B (14 Marks) — [KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]**

**(a)** Derive the alveolar gas equation for $P_{A,CO_2}$ starting from the mass balance on CO₂. State the physical significance of each term. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

Step 1: Mass balance on CO₂ in alveoli (steady state):
$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} = \dot{V}_A \cdot F_{A,CO_2}$$
where $\dot{V}_{CO_2}$ is CO₂ production delivered to alveoli from blood, and $\dot{V}_A$ is alveolar ventilation. $F_{A,CO_2}$ is the alveolar CO₂ fraction.

Step 2: Convert fraction to partial pressure using Dalton's law:
$$F_{A,CO_2} = \frac{P_{A,CO_2}}{P_B - P_{H_2O}}$$

Step 3: Substitute:
$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} = \dot{V}_A \cdot \frac{P_{A,CO_2}}{P_B - P_{H_2O}}$$

Step 4: Rearrange for $P_{A,CO_2}$ and absorb constants:
$$P_{A,CO_2} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2} \times 0.863}{\dot{V}_A}$$
The constant $0.863$ is the unit-conversion factor when $\dot{V}_{CO_2}$ is in mL/min, $\dot{V}_A$ in L/min, and pressure in mmHg. **(7 marks)**

**(b)** A healthy 60 kg female subject exercises on a treadmill. Her O₂ consumption rises to 1500 mL/min and RQ is 0.9. Her alveolar ventilation is measured as 60 L/min. Compute her alveolar $P_{O_2}$ and $P_{CO_2}$. Take $P_B = 760$ mmHg, $P_{H_2O} = 47$ mmHg, $F_{I,O_2} = 0.21$. **(7 marks)**

**Model Solution:**

**Step 1 — Compute $\dot{V}_{CO_2}$:**
$$\dot{V}_{CO_2} = RQ \times \dot{V}_{O_2} = 0.9 \times 1500 = 1350 \text{ mL/min}$$

**Step 2 — Compute $P_{A,CO_2}$:**
$$P_{A,CO_2} = \frac{1350 \times 0.863}{60} = \frac{1165.05}{60} = 19.42 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Step 3 — Compute $P_{A,O_2}$:**
$$P_{A,O_2} = F_{I,O_2}(P_B - P_{H_2O}) - \frac{P_{A,CO_2}}{R}$$
$$P_{A,O_2} = 0.21 \times (760 - 47) - \frac{19.42}{0.9}$$
$$P_{A,O_2} = 149.97 - 21.58 = 128.39 \text{ mmHg}$$

**Clinical note:** Hyperventilation during exercise reduces $P_{A,CO_2}$ and elevates $P_{A,O_2}$, exactly as predicted. **(7 marks)**

---

> [!WARNING]
> **KTU Examiner's Valuation Pitfall Callout:**
> 1. **Forgetting to apply steady state** — Many students write the full unsteady mass balance and then stop. The KTU examiner expects the *final steady-state equation* as the answer. Marks are docked if $\frac{dM}{dt}$ is not explicitly set to zero with justification.
> 2. **Confusing $\dot{V}_E$ with $\dot{V}_A$** — Alveolar ventilation is *not* minute ventilation. Always subtract dead space: $\dot{V}_A = (V_T - V_D) \times f$. Using $\dot{V}_E$ in the alveolar gas equation is a 2-mark deduction.
> 3. **Wrong RQ interpretation** — A common error: RQ > 1 is *not* "impossible" or "hyperventilation"; it specifically indicates **lipogenesis** (de-novo fat synthesis). Examiners look for this nuanced answer.
> 4. **Unit conversion oversight** — When using $K = 0.863$, the inputs MUST be mL/min and L/min respectively. Mixing units (e.g., L/min for both) is a recurring error.
> 5. **Ignoring water vapour pressure** — $P_B - P_{H_2O} = 713$ mmHg, *not* 760. KTU 2024 problems frequently test this; expect a 1-mark deduction if missed.

---

### Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist)

- ✅ The **lungs are modelled as a CSTR** in mass-balance analysis.
- ✅ **Fick's principle** is the cornerstone: gas transfer = blood flow × (A–V concentration difference).
- ✅ **Two mass-balance frameworks** exist: ventilatory (air-side) and perfusion (blood-side); they must agree numerically at steady state.
- ✅ Key **steady-state O₂ balance**: $\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{V}_I F_{I,O_2} - \dot{V}_E F_{E,O_2}$.
- ✅ Key **steady-state CO₂ balance**: $\dot{V}_{CO_2} = \dot{V}_E F_{E,CO_2}$ (inspired CO₂ ≈ 0).
- ✅ **Respiratory Quotient (RQ)** = $\dot{V}_{CO_2} / \dot{V}_{O_2}$; normal = 0.8; range 0.7–1.0.
- ✅ **Alveolar CO₂ equation**: $P_{A,CO_2} = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2} \times 0.863}{\dot{V}_A}$ (units critical).
- ✅ **Alveolar O₂ equation**: $P_{A,O_2} = F_{I,O_2}(P_B - P_{H_2O}) - \frac{P_{A,CO_2}}{RQ}$.
- ✅ Standard constants to memorize: $P_B = 760$ mmHg, $P_{H_2O} = 47$ mmHg, $F_{I,O_2} = 0.21$.
- ✅ $\dot{V}_A = (V_T - V_D) \times f$ — alveolar ventilation ≠ minute ventilation.
- ✅ Typical resting $\dot{V}_{O_2} \approx 3.5$ mL/kg/min (Haldane's standard).
- ✅ At **steady state**, $\frac{dM}{dt} = 0$ — the most critical assumption in KTU problems.
- ✅ The **ideal gas law / Dalton's law** converts fractions to partial pressures: $F_i = P_i / (P_B - P_{H_2O})$.
<!-- SECTION_5_END -->

topics.title: Gas transport mechanisms of lungs
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT864 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Gas transport mechanisms of lungs # 1. Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Formal Academic Definition **Gas transport mechanisms of the lungs** refer to the integrated physiological and biophysical processes by which respiratory gases—primarily **oxygen (O₂)** and **carbon dioxide (CO₂)**—are exchanged across the alveolar-capillary membrane and subsequently carried between the lungs and peripheral tissues via the blood circulatory system. The complete mechanism encompasses three sequential physiological stages: 1. **Pulmonary ventilation** (bulk convective flow of air into/out of alveoli) 2. **Alveolar-capillary diffusion** (passive molecular diffusion across the respiratory membrane) 3. **Perfusion-mediated gas carriage** (transport of O₂ and CO₂ in blood, either in physical solution or chemically bound to haemoglobin, bicarbonate, and carbamino compounds) Mathematically, this transport system is described using the **mass balance of gas flux** governed by Fick's First Law of Diffusion and the **oxygen-haemoglobin equilibrium binding kinetics** governed by the Hill-Adair equation. > [!IMPORTANT] > **KTU 2024 Syllabus Highlight:** In the modelling context, "gas transport" is not just a descriptive physiology topic — it requires mathematical modelling of diffusion, binding curves, and compartment dynamics using ODEs and equilibrium equations. --- ## 1.2 Conceptual Analogy / Intuition > [!NOTE] > **Real-World Analogy — "The Respiratory Courier Service"** > > Imagine the lungs as a **massive international airport cargo hub**: > - **Alveoli** = Loading bays where cargo is swapped between planes (air) and trucks (blood). > - **Haemoglobin (Hb)** = Specialised fleet of **delivery trucks**, each capable of carrying up to **4 oxygen containers** (O₂ molecules) at a time. > - **Red Blood Cells (RBCs)** = Truck containers, with Hb acting as the internal racks. > - **Bloodstream (arteries/veins)** = Highway network connecting airport → city (tissues) → airport. > - **CO₂** = Return cargo (waste) that must be brought back to the airport for export (exhalation). > - **Partial pressure gradient** = The "delivery urgency" — high urgency (high gradient) means faster loading/unloading. > - **Fick's Law** = The truck-unloading speed limit at the warehouse dock (depends on door size, distance, and concentration difference). > > If the truck fleet is damaged (e.g., **CO poisoning**, **anaemia**), cargo capacity drops even if the airport is functioning perfectly — this is the heart of why gas transport modelling matters. --- ## 1.3 Key Physical Constants and Standard Metrics | Parameter | Symbol | Standard Value | |---|---|---| | Atmospheric pressure (sea level) | $P_{atm}$ | **760 mmHg** (= 101.325 kPa) | | Alveolar partial pressure of O₂ | $P_{AO_2}$ | **~104 mmHg** | | Arterial partial pressure of O₂ | $P_{aO_2}$ | **~95–100 mmHg** | | Venous partial pressure of O₂ | $P_{vO_2}$ | **~40 mmHg** | | Alveolar partial pressure of CO₂ | $P_{ACO_2}$ | **~40 mmHg** | | Arterial partial pressure of CO₂ | $P_{aCO_2}$ | **~40 mmHg** | | Venous partial pressure of CO₂ | $P_{vCO_2}$ | **~46 mmHg** | | Tissue partial pressure of O₂ | $P_{tO_2}$ | **~20–40 mmHg** | | Tissue partial pressure of CO₂ | $P_{tCO_2}$ | **~45–55 mmHg** | | O₂ solubility in blood (plasma) | $\alpha_{O_2}$ | **0.0031 mL/(100 mL·mmHg)** | | CO₂ solubility in blood (plasma) | $\alpha_{CO_2}$ | **0.067 mL/(100 mL·mmHg)** | | Haemoglobin concentration | [Hb] | **15 g/dL** (normal adult) | | O₂ binding capacity of Hb | – | **1.34 mL O₂ / g Hb** | | Cardiac output (rest) | $\dot{Q}$ | **5 L/min** | | O₂ consumption (rest) | $\dot{V}_{O_2}$ | **250 mL/min** | | CO₂ production (rest) | $\dot{V}_{CO_2}$ | **200 mL/min** | | Respiratory quotient | RQ | **0.8** (at rest) | # 2. Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 Operational Concept Breakdown — The 4-Stage Gas Transport Pipeline ### **Stage 1: Convective Ventilation (Bulk Air Movement)** - Air moves into the alveoli through the tracheobronchial tree due to the **pressure gradient** generated by the respiratory muscles (diaphragm, intercostals). - Governed by the equation of motion for the respiratory system: $$P_{mus} = E \cdot V + R \cdot \dot{V} + P_{expiratory}$$ where $E$ is elastance, $V$ is lung volume, $R$ is airway resistance, and $\dot{V}$ is flow rate. - **Why it matters:** Without adequate ventilation, no amount of alveolar surface area or Hb content can compensate — the entire gas transport chain collapses. ### **Stage 2: Diffusion Across the Respiratory Membrane** - O₂ and CO₂ diffuse passively across the **alveolar-capillary barrier** (~0.5 µm thick). - Governed by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**: $$\dot{V}_{gas} = \frac{A \cdot D \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{T}$$ - **Why it matters:** The diffusion rate sets the upper limit on how fast gases can equilibrate between alveolar air and pulmonary capillary blood. ### **Stage 3: Chemical Binding & Carriage in Blood** - **O₂ transport:** ~98.5% bound to haemoglobin (oxyhaemoglobin, HbO₂), only ~1.5% dissolved in plasma. - **CO₂ transport:** ~70% as bicarbonate ($HCO_3^-$), ~23% as carbamino-haemoglobin (HbCO₂), ~7% dissolved in plasma. - **Why it matters:** The chemical binding multiplies blood's gas-carrying capacity by ~70× for O₂ and ~20× for CO₂. ### **Stage 4: Tissue-Level Unloading & Return Transport** - At peripheral tissues, the local environment (low pO₂, high pCO₂, low pH, high temperature, high 2,3-BPG) shifts the **oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve rightward** (Bohr effect), promoting O₂ release. - CO₂ diffuses into RBCs and is rapidly hydrated to carbonic acid by **carbonic anhydrase**, then dissociates to $HCO_3^-$ and $H^+$. --- ## 2.2 KTU Formula Sheet / Cheat Sheet | # | Formula / Law | Equation | Engineering / Physiological Use | |---|---|---|---| | 1 | Fick's First Law of Diffusion | $\dot{V}_{gas} = \dfrac{A \cdot D \cdot (P_1 - P_2)}{T}$ | Computes alveolar gas flux across respiratory membrane | | 2 | Fick's Law (molar form) | $J = -D \cdot \dfrac{\partial C}{\partial x}$ | Models concentration-driven transport in tissue capillaries | | 3 | Henry's Law | $C_{dissolved} = \alpha \cdot P$ | Computes physically dissolved gas concentration in plasma | | 4 | Alveolar Gas Equation | $P_{AO_2} = P_{IO_2} - \dfrac{P_{ACO_2}}{R}$ | Predicts alveolar O₂ partial pressure for given ventilation | | 5 | O₂ Content in Blood | $C_{O_2} = (1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S_{O_2}) + (0.0031 \cdot P_{O_2})$ | Total O₂ carrying capacity (chemical + dissolved) | | 6 | CO₂ Content in Blood | $C_{CO_2} = (2.226 \cdot S_{CO_2}) + \text{(carbamino)} + \text{(dissolved)}$ | Total CO₂ carriage — basis for CO₂ dissociation curve | | 7 | Hill Equation (Hb-O₂ binding) | $S_{O_2} = \dfrac{(P_{O_2})^n}{(P_{50})^n + (P_{O_2})^n}$ | Models sigmoidal O₂-Hb saturation curve, $n \approx 2.7$ | | 8 | Bohr Effect (pH shift) | $\Delta \log P_{50} \approx -0.48 \cdot \Delta pH$ | Quantifies rightward shift of dissociation curve | | 9 | Henderson-Hasselbalch Equation | $pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{[CO_2]}\right)$ | Links blood pH, $HCO_3^-$, and $pCO_2$ | | 10 | Carbonic Anhydrase Reaction | $CO_2 + H_2O \xrightleftharpoons{CA} H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$ | Core CO₂ hydration / $HCO_3^-$ formation step | | 11 | Respiratory Quotient | $RQ = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{CO_2}}{\dot{V}_{O_2}}$ | Determines metabolic substrate and $P_{AO_2}$ | | 12 | Pulmonary Diffusion Capacity | $D_L = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{gas}}{(P_A - P_{\bar{v}})}$ | Measures lung efficiency for gas transfer | | 13 | Mass Balance for Tissue O₂ | $\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{Q} \cdot (C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2})$ | Fick principle for tissue O₂ consumption | | 14 | Chloride (Hamburger) Shift | $HCO_3^- \text{(out)} \leftrightarrow Cl^- \text{(in)}$ in RBC | Maintains electrochemical balance during CO₂ transport | | 15 | Haldane Effect | $\uparrow P_{O_2} \Rightarrow \downarrow \text{CO}_2 \text{ carriage by Hb}$ | Promotes CO₂ release in lungs, pickup in tissues | > [!NOTE] > **Note on Notation:** In all the above equations, the absolute value notation is written as $\vert x \vert$ to avoid breaking the markdown table syntax in rendered KTU notes. --- ## 2.3 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Mechanical Ventilator Design:** Uses the alveolar gas equation to set optimal PEEP, FiO₂, and tidal volume in ICU ventilators. - **Extracorporeal Membrane Oxygenation (ECMO):** Diffusion equation and membrane area calculations are direct applications of Fick's law in hollow-fibre oxygenator design. - **Pulse Oximeter Signal Processing:** The Beer-Lambert law extension uses $S_{O_2}$ vs. $P_{O_2}$ calibration curves derived from the Hill equation. - **Anaesthesia Machine Engineering:** Flow-meter calibration relies on $P_{IO_2} = F_{IO_2} \cdot (P_B - P_{H_2O})$ derived from alveolar gas equation. - **High-Altitude / Aerospace Medicine:** Hypoxia models use shifted O₂-Hb curves to predict oxygenation at low barometric pressures. - **Sports Physiology & Wearable Devices:** $C_{O_2}$ equations are used in lactate threshold and VO₂ max estimation. # 3. Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation: Fick's First Law of Diffusion Applied to the Alveolar-Capillary Membrane ### **Starting Premise** Fick's First Law states that the diffusive flux $J$ of a gas across a membrane is proportional to the concentration gradient: $$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$ where: - $J$ = molar flux per unit area [mol/(cm²·s)] - $D$ = diffusion coefficient of gas in membrane [cm²/s] - $C$ = gas concentration [mol/cm³] - $x$ = position coordinate [cm] ### **Step 1: Convert Concentration Gradient to Partial Pressure Gradient** By Henry's Law, $C = \alpha \cdot P$, where $\alpha$ is the Bunsen solubility coefficient. Therefore: $$\frac{dC}{dx} = \alpha \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$ ### **Step 2: Apply to a Membrane of Thickness T** Assuming a linear pressure drop across the alveolar-capillary membrane of thickness $T$ (≈ 0.5 µm), the gradient becomes: $$\frac{dP}{dx} = \frac{P_A - P_C}{T}$$ where $P_A$ = alveolar partial pressure, $P_C$ = capillary blood partial pressure. ### **Step 3: Express Total Molar Flow Rate** Multiplying the flux by the membrane area $A$: $$ \begin{aligned} \dot{n}_{gas} &= J \cdot A \\ &= -D \cdot A \cdot \frac{dC}{dx} \\ &= -D \cdot A \cdot \alpha \cdot \frac{dP}{dx} \\ &= D \cdot A \cdot \alpha \cdot \frac{(P_A - P_C)}{T} \end{aligned} $$ ### **Step 4: Define the Pulmonary Diffusing Capacity $D_L$** Since $D$, $\alpha$, and $T$ are constants for a given gas and membrane, group them as a single parameter: $$D_L = \frac{D \cdot A \cdot \alpha}{T}$$ ### **Step 5: Final Form — Fick's Law for the Lung** $$ \dot{V}_{gas} = D_L \cdot (P_A - P_{\bar{v}}) $$ This is the **Fick equation for pulmonary gas transfer** that KTU expects you to derive. --- ## 3.2 Derivation: Alveolar Gas Equation (Idealised, Steady-State) ### **Premise** Oxygen and CO₂ in the alveolus are in dynamic equilibrium between **ventilation inflow**, **diffusion inflow from capillaries**, and **gas removal by blood**. ### **Step 1: Express Inspired O₂ Partial Pressure** $$P_{IO_2} = F_{IO_2} \cdot (P_B - P_{H_2O})$$ At sea level with $F_{IO_2} = 0.21$, $P_B = 760$ mmHg, $P_{H_2O} = 47$ mmHg: $$P_{IO_2} = 0.21 \cdot (760 - 47) = 0.21 \cdot 713 = 149.7 \approx 150 \text{ mmHg}$$ ### **Step 2: Apply Steady-State O₂ Mass Balance** At steady state, O₂ entering alveolus = O₂ leaving via blood: $$\dot{V}_A \cdot F_{IO_2} = \dot{V}_A \cdot F_{AO_2} + \dot{V}_{O_2}$$ where $\dot{V}_A$ is alveolar ventilation, $F_{AO_2}$ is alveolar O₂ fraction, and $\dot{V}_{O_2}$ is O₂ uptake. ### **Step 3: Apply the Same Balance for CO₂** $$\dot{V}_A \cdot F_{ACO_2} = \dot{V}_{CO_2}$$ since inspired CO₂ is negligible (≈ 0). ### **Step 4: Use the Respiratory Quotient** The ratio of CO₂ production to O₂ consumption: $$RQ = \frac{\dot{V}_{CO_2}}{\dot{V}_{O_2}}$$ From step 3: $\dot{V}_A = \dfrac{\dot{V}_{CO_2}}{F_{ACO_2}}$. Substituting in the O₂ equation and dividing by $\dot{V}_A$: $$F_{IO_2} = F_{AO_2} + \frac{\dot{V}_{O_2}}{\dot{V}_A} = F_{AO_2} + \frac{F_{AO_2} \cdot RQ \cdot \dot{V}_{CO_2}/\dot{V}_{CO_2}}{1}$$ After algebraic manipulation (in terms of partial pressures): $$ \begin{aligned} P_{IO_2} &= P_{AO_2} + \frac{P_{ACO_2}}{RQ} - P_{ACO_2} \\ P_{AO_2} &= P_{IO_2} - \frac{P_{ACO_2}}{R} \\ \end{aligned} $$ where $R$ is the respiratory exchange ratio (≈ RQ in steady state). **This is the KTU-canonical alveolar gas equation.** ### **Step 5: Verify with Typical Values** With $P_{IO_2} = 150$ mmHg, $P_{ACO_2} = 40$ mmHg, $R = 0.8$: $$P_{AO_2} = 150 - \frac{40}{0.8} = 150 - 50 = 100 \text{ mmHg}$$ This matches the standard physiological value of **~100 mmHg** — confirming the derivation. --- ## 3.3 Derivation: Hill Equation for O₂-Haemoglobin Binding ### **Step 1: Cooperative Binding Reaction** Hb binds O₂ at 4 sites cooperatively: $$Hb + 4\,O_2 \rightleftharpoons Hb(O_2)_4$$ ### **Step 2: Empirical Hill Form** The fractional saturation $S$ of haemoglobin is given empirically by: $$S = \frac{(P_{O_2})^n}{K_d + (P_{O_2})^n}$$ ### **Step 3: Re-parameterise Using $P_{50}$** By definition, $S = 0.5$ when $P_{O_2} = P_{50}$, so $K_d = (P_{50})^n$. Substituting: $$ \boxed{\,S_{O_2} = \frac{(P_{O_2})^n}{(P_{50})^n + (P_{O_2})^n}\,} $$ with $n \approx 2.7$ (Hill coefficient for Hb) and $P_{50} \approx 26.6$ mmHg in standard conditions. ### **Step 4: Verification at Key Physiological Points** - At $P_{O_2} = 100$ mmHg: $S = \dfrac{100^{2.7}}{26.6^{2.7} + 100^{2.7}} \approx 0.97$ ✓ - At $P_{O_2} = 40$ mmHg (mixed venous): $S \approx 0.75$ ✓ - At $P_{O_2} = 26.6$ mmHg: $S = 0.5$ ✓ (by definition of $P_{50}$) --- ## 3.4 Analytical Summary Matrix — When to Use Which Equation | Scenario | Governing Equation | Key Variable | KTU Tip | |---|---|---|---| | Designing oxygenator membrane | Fick's Law | $A, D, T$ | Membrane area scales linearly with required $D_L$ | | Predicting alveolar O₂ level | Alveolar Gas Equation | $P_{ACO_2}, R$ | Use $R = 0.8$ unless told otherwise | | Estimating blood O₂ content | $C_{O_2}$ equation | $S_{O_2}, [Hb]$ | Dissolved term is negligible for Hb, NOT for plasma | | Modelling Bohr/Haldane effects | Hill + shift equations | pH, $P_{CO_2}$, 2,3-BPG | Shift right (low pH) = more unloading at tissues | | Acid-base balance | Henderson-Hasselbalch | $[HCO_3^-], P_{CO_2}$ | Kidney regulates numerator, lung regulates denominator | | Tissue O₂ consumption | Fick Principle | $\dot{Q}, C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2}$ | Used in cardiac output measurement (in vivo) | # 4. Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 System-Level Block Diagram: Complete Pulmonary Gas Transport Pipeline ```mermaid flowchart LR A[ATMOSPHERIC AIR
PiO2 = 150 mmHg] --> B[UPPER AIRWAYS
Humidification
T = 37 C] B --> C[TRACHEA and BRONCHI
Convective Bulk Flow] C --> D[BRONCHIOLES and ALVEOLAR DUCTS
Diffusion begins] D --> E[ALVEOLAR SPACE
PAO2 = 100 mmHg
PACO2 = 40 mmHg] E --> F[ALVEOLAR CAPILLARY MEMBRANE
Thickness T = 0.5 um
Fick's Law Diffusion] F --> G[PULMONARY CAPILLARY BLOOD
PaO2 rises to 95 mmHg
PaCO2 falls to 40 mmHg] G --> H[SYSTEMIC ARTERIES
Hb saturation 97 percent] H --> I[SYSTEMIC CAPILLARIES
Tissue O2 unloading
Bohr effect active] I --> J[TISSUE CELLS
Mitochondrial O2 consumption
PtiO2 approx 20 mmHg] J --> K[CO2 PRODUCTION
PtiCO2 approx 50 mmHg] K --> L[VENOUS BLOOD
PvO2 = 40 mmHg
PvCO2 = 46 mmHg] L --> M[PULMONARY ARTERIES] M --> F E --> N[EXHALATION
PAW removal
PEO2 = 120 mmHg] N --> O[ATMOSPHERE] ``` --- ## 4.2 O₂ Transport Cascade — From Alveolus to Mitochondrion ```mermaid flowchart TD A[Alveolar Air
PAO2 = 100 mmHg] --> B{Respiratory Membrane
Fick Diffusion} B --> C[Pulmonary Capillary Plasma
Dissolved O2 only
alpha PO2 = 0.0031] C --> D[Red Blood Cell
Hb binds 4 O2 cooperatively] D --> E[Oxyhaemoglobin HbO2
98.5 percent of total O2] E --> F[Systemic Arterial Blood
PaO2 = 95 mmHg
SaO2 = 97 percent] F --> G[Tissue Capillary
Low PO2 High PCO2 Low pH] G --> H{Bohr Effect
Rightward Curve Shift} H --> I[O2 Unloading
SaO2 falls to 75 percent] I --> J[Interstitial Fluid
PO2 approx 30 mmHg] J --> K[Cell Membrane Diffusion] K --> L[Mitochondria
PO2 approx 5 to 20 mmHg
Electron Transport Chain] L --> M[ATP Production
VO2 = 250 mL per min] ``` --- ## 4.3 CO₂ Transport Pathways in Blood (Quantitative Distribution) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Tissue Cells
CO2 production
PtiCO2 = 50 mmHg] --> B[Diffusion into Plasma
7 percent as dissolved CO2
alpha CO2 = 0.067] B --> C[Entry into RBC
Carbonic Anhydrase Catalysis] C --> D[CO2 plus H2O
forms H2CO3] D --> E[Dissociation
H plus HCO3 minus] E --> F[HCO3 minus 70 percent
Exits RBC via Band 3
Cl minus enters Hamburger shift] E --> G[H plus 23 percent
Buffered by Hb
Haldane effect] C --> H[Carbamino Compounds
HbNH2 plus CO2
HbNHCOOH 23 percent] H --> I[Venous Blood
PvCO2 = 46 mmHg] F --> I G --> I B --> I I --> J[Pulmonary Capillary
Cl minus shifts out
H plus recombines] J --> K[CO2 Regenerated
and Exhaled
PACO2 = 40 mmHg] K --> L[Alveolus] L --> M[Expired Air
PECO2 = 27 mmHg] ``` --- ## 4.4 Oxygen-Haemoglobin Dissociation Curve (Block Schematic) ```mermaid flowchart LR A[Low PO2 below 20 mmHg
Mixed Venous] --> B[Steep Unloading Zone
20 to 60 mmHg] B --> C[Plateau Loading Zone
60 to 100 mmHg] C --> D[High PO2 above 100 mmHg
Hyperbaric] A1[Rightward Shift
Bohr Effect
High PCO2 Low pH High Temp High 2,3 BPG] -.-> B C1[Leftward Shift
Low PCO2 High pH Low Temp Low 2,3 BPG
Fetal Hb CO poisoning] -.-> C B --> E[Tissue Unloading
SaO2 97 to 75 percent] C --> F[Pulmonary Loading
SaO2 75 to 97 percent] D --> G[Hyperbaric Reserve
Minimal additional loading] ``` # 5. KTU 2024 Scheme Examination Question Bank & Topic Recap --- ## 📘 PART A — Short Answer Questions (3 Marks Each) ### **Question 1: Define the Alveolar Gas Equation and State Its Physiological Significance.** `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Alveolar Gas Equation** relates the alveolar partial pressure of oxygen ($P_{AO_2}$) to the inspired O₂ fraction, barometric pressure, alveolar CO₂ partial pressure, and the respiratory quotient: $$P_{AO_2} = P_{IO_2} - \frac{P_{ACO_2}}{R} = F_{IO_2}(P_B - P_{H_2O}) - \frac{P_{ACO_2}}{R}$$ **Significance (2 marks):** - It quantifies how alveolar oxygenation depends on **ventilation** ($P_{ACO_2}$ term) and **metabolism** ($R$). - It is fundamental in **mechanical ventilation settings** (FiO₂, PEEP) and in diagnosing the **A-a gradient** (alveolar-arterial O₂ difference). - It explains why **hypoventilation** (high $P_{ACO_2}$) causes hypoxaemia. --- ### **Question 2: What is the Bohr Effect? Mention Its Physiological Importance.** `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 marks):** The **Bohr Effect** describes the **rightward shift** of the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve when blood pH decreases (or $P_{CO_2}$ increases, temperature rises, or 2,3-BPG concentration increases). Quantitatively: $$\Delta \log P_{50} \approx -0.48 \cdot \Delta pH$$ **Importance (2 marks):** 1. **At tissues** (high $P_{CO_2}$, low pH) → curve shifts right → **enhanced O₂ unloading** to actively metabolising cells. 2. **At lungs** (low $P_{CO_2}$, high pH) → curve shifts left → **enhanced O₂ loading** onto Hb. 3. Couples **acid-base balance** with **oxygen delivery**, making O₂ supply matched to local metabolic demand. --- ## 📗 PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each) > **Internal Choice:** Answer **either (A) or (B)**. Each sub-part carries 7 marks. --- ### **🅰️ Question A (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - May 2024]` **(a)** Derive Fick's First Law of Diffusion as applied to the alveolar-capillary membrane. Define the term **Pulmonary Diffusing Capacity ($D_L$)** and explain how it differs between O₂ and CO₂. **(7 marks)** **(b)** With the help of a neat block diagram, explain the four stages of gas transport in the human respiratory system. For each stage, state the governing physical/chemical principle. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to (a):** **Step 1 — Fick's Law Statement (1 mark):** Fick's First Law of Diffusion states that the molar flux $J$ of a gas through a thin membrane is proportional to the concentration gradient: $$J = -D \cdot \frac{dC}{dx}$$ **Step 2 — Convert to Partial Pressure Form (1 mark):** By Henry's Law, $C = \alpha \cdot P$, so: $$J = -D \cdot \alpha \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$ **Step 3 — Apply Across Membrane of Thickness T (2 marks):** For the alveolar-capillary membrane, assuming linear pressure drop from alveolar side ($P_A$) to capillary side ($P_C$): $$\dot{n}_{gas} = \frac{D \cdot \alpha \cdot A \cdot (P_A - P_C)}{T}$$ **Step 4 — Define $D_L$ (2 marks):** Grouping the constants: $$D_L = \frac{D \cdot A \cdot \alpha}{T}$$ Thus: $\dot{V}_{gas} = D_L \cdot (P_A - P_{\bar{v}})$ **Step 5 — O₂ vs. CO₂ Diffusing Capacity (1 mark):** Typical values: $D_{L,O_2} \approx 21$ mL/(min·mmHg); $D_{L,CO_2} \approx 400$ mL/(min·mmHg). CO₂ diffuses ~20× faster than O₂ due to its much higher solubility in the membrane ($\alpha_{CO_2} \gg \alpha_{O_2}$). --- #### **Model Solution to (b):** | Stage | Location | Governing Principle | |---|---|---| | 1. Convective Ventilation | Trachea → Alveoli | Pressure-driven bulk flow (equation of motion) | | 2. Alveolar Diffusion | Respiratory Membrane | Fick's First Law of Diffusion | | 3. Chemical Binding | RBCs (Hb) | Hill-Adair cooperative binding | | 4. Tissue Unloading | Systemic Capillaries | Bohr Effect + concentration gradient | A labelled block diagram is required (refer to Section 4.1 schematic). For each stage, mention the corresponding mathematical equation. --- ### **🅱️ Question B (14 Marks)** — `[KTU University Exam - Dec 2023]` **(a)** Derive the **Alveolar Gas Equation** from first principles. A patient is breathing 40% oxygen at sea level. If the alveolar $P_{CO_2}$ is 38 mmHg and R = 0.8, compute the alveolar $P_{O_2}$. **(7 marks)** **(b)** Explain the various mechanisms of **CO₂ transport in blood** with a quantitative breakdown. State and explain the **Haldane Effect**. **(7 marks)** --- #### **Model Solution to (a):** **Step 1 — Mass Balance for O₂ (2 marks):** $\dot{V}_A \cdot F_{IO_2} = \dot{V}_A \cdot F_{AO_2} + \dot{V}_{O_2}$ **Step 2 — Mass Balance for CO₂ (2 marks):** $\dot{V}_A \cdot F_{ACO_2} = \dot{V}_{CO_2}$ (inspired CO₂ negligible) **Step 3 — Use RQ and Convert to Partial Pressures (2 marks):** After algebraic manipulation: $$P_{AO_2} = P_{IO_2} - \frac{P_{ACO_2}}{R}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical Calculation (1 mark):** $$ \begin{aligned} P_{IO_2} &= 0.40 \cdot (760 - 47) = 0.40 \cdot 713 = 285.2 \text{ mmHg} \\ P_{AO_2} &= 285.2 - \frac{38}{0.8} = 285.2 - 47.5 = 237.7 \text{ mmHg} \end{aligned} $$ **Answer: $P_{AO_2} \approx 237.7$ mmHg** (substantially higher than the normal 100 mmHg due to 40% O₂ supplementation). --- #### **Model Solution to (b):** **CO₂ Transport Mechanisms (5 marks):** | Mechanism | % of Total | Equation/Process | |---|---|---| | Dissolved in plasma | ~7% | $C = \alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2}$ | | Bicarbonate ($HCO_3^-$) | ~70% | $CO_2 + H_2O \xrightleftharpoons{CA} H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$ | | Carbamino-haemoglobin | ~23% | $Hb\text{-}NH_2 + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons Hb\text{-}NH\text{-}COOH$ | **Haldane Effect (2 marks):** The Haldane Effect states that **oxygenation of haemoglobin in the lungs promotes the release of CO₂**, while **deoxygenation at tissues promotes CO₂ uptake**. Mechanistically, oxygenated Hb is a **weaker acid** and binds fewer H⁺ ions, thereby displacing H⁺ from Hb. These free H⁺ combine with $HCO_3^-$ to form $H_2CO_3$, which is dehydrated by carbonic anhydrase to release CO₂ for exhalation. This is the **physiological mirror of the Bohr Effect**. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Mark-Deduction Pitfalls** > 1. **Confusing Bohr and Haldane Effects:** Bohr = effect of pH/$P_{CO_2}$ on O₂ binding. Haldane = effect of O₂ saturation on CO₂ carriage. Mixing these up costs **2–3 marks** instantly. > 2. **Forgetting the dissolved-O₂ term in $C_{O_2}$:** Even though it is small (1.5%), the term $0.0031 \cdot P_{O_2}$ is required for full credit in derivations. > 3. **Wrong sign in Fick's Law:** The direction of flow is **from high to low partial pressure**; sign errors are penalised. > 4. **Using $P_B$ instead of $(P_B - P_{H_2O})$** in the alveolar gas equation — students forget the 47 mmHg water vapour correction. > 5. **Omitting units** in numerical answers (e.g., mmHg for partial pressure) — KTU examiners strictly enforce unit notation. > 6. **Not stating the Hill coefficient** $n \approx 2.7$ when writing the Hill equation — partial credit is given for the correct numerical exponent. --- ## 🔁 Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember - ✅ **Gas transport** = ventilation + diffusion + chemical binding + tissue unloading. - ✅ **Alveolar Gas Equation:** $P_{AO_2} = F_{IO_2}(P_B - P_{H_2O}) - \dfrac{P_{ACO_2}}{R}$. Standard value = **100 mmHg**. - ✅ **Fick's Law of Diffusion:** $\dot{V}_{gas} = \dfrac{D \cdot A \cdot \alpha \cdot \Delta P}{T}$; defines **Pulmonary Diffusing Capacity $D_L$**. - ✅ **Henry's Law** converts partial pressure to dissolved concentration: $C = \alpha P$. - ✅ **O₂ carriage:** 98.5% as HbO₂ (Hill equation, $n \approx 2.7$, $P_{50} \approx 26.6$ mmHg), 1.5% dissolved. - ✅ **CO₂ carriage:** 70% $HCO_3^-$, 23% carbamino-Hb, 7% dissolved. **Carbonic anhydrase** is the rate-limiting enzyme. - ✅ **Bohr Effect** → rightward shift of O₂-Hb curve with ↑$P_{CO_2}$, ↓pH, ↑T, ↑2,3-BPG → favours tissue O₂ unloading. - ✅ **Haldane Effect** → oxygenation of Hb in lungs displaces H⁺ → favours CO₂ release for exhalation. - ✅ **Chloride (Hamburger) Shift** maintains electrochemical balance when $HCO_3^-$ leaves RBCs at lungs. - ✅ **Fick Principle (tissue):** $\dot{V}_{O_2} = \dot{Q} \cdot (C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2})$. - ✅ **Respiratory Quotient $RQ = 0.8$** at rest on mixed diet; carbohydrate metabolism → 1.0; fat → 0.7. - ✅ **Hill equation** predicts $S_{O_2} = 0.97$ at $P_{O_2} = 100$ mmHg, $S_{O_2} = 0.75$ at $P_{O_2} = 40$ mmHg. - ✅ Standard Hb concentration: **15 g/dL**; O₂ binding capacity: **1.34 mL/g Hb**; dissolved-O₂ coefficient: **0.0031 mL O₂/(100 mL blood·mmHg)**. - ✅ CO₂ solubility is ~**20× greater** than O₂ in blood, making $D_{L,CO_2} \gg D_{L,O_2}$ — never write the reverse.

topics.title: Oxygen and carbon dioxide transport in blood and tissues
topics.content:
> **APJ ABDUL KALAM TECHNOLOGICAL UNIVERSITY (KTU) | 2024 SCHEME** > - **Course:** PEBMT864 - MODELLING OF PHYSIOLOGICAL SYSTEMS > - **Module:** Module 4 > - **Topic:** Oxygen and carbon dioxide transport in blood and tissues # SECTION 1: Core Technical Definition & Intuitive Overview ## 1.1 Oxygen Transport in Blood and Tissues — Formal Definition **Oxygen transport** refers to the complete physiological process by which molecular oxygen ($O_2$) is carried from the atmospheric air across the alveolar-capillary membrane, bound to haemoglobin within erythrocytes, delivered via the systemic circulation, and finally released to the mitochondria of peripheral tissue cells down a partial pressure gradient. In the context of the **KTU 2024 Scheme (PEBMT864)**, the mathematical modelling of this process involves the coupling of: 1. **Convective transport** — bulk movement of $O_2$ dissolved in plasma and bound to haemoglobin ($Hb$). 2. **Diffusive transport** — passive movement of $O_2$ across the alveolar wall, capillary endothelium, interstitial space, and the sarcolemma, governed by **Fick's First Law of Diffusion**. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Key Definition (KTU Syllabus Terminology):** > *Oxygen Content of Blood* ($C_{O_2}$) is defined as the total volume of $O_2$ (in mL) carried per 100 mL (or per Litre) of blood, comprising both the **dissolved fraction** and the **haemoglobin-bound fraction**. The standard metric is **mL $O_2$ per 100 mL blood** (vol %), and normal arterial oxygen content is approximately **20.4 mL/dL** with a haemoglobin concentration of **15 g/dL**. ## 1.2 Carbon Dioxide Transport in Blood and Tissues — Formal Definition **Carbon dioxide transport** is the physiological and biochemical process by which metabolic waste $CO_2$ produced in tissue mitochondria is conveyed via three distinct mechanisms (dissolved, bicarbonate, carbamino) through the venous blood back to the lungs for exhalation. $CO_2$ is carried in three forms: - **Dissolved $CO_2$** in plasma water (~5–10% of total) - **Bicarbonate ions** ($HCO_3^-$) in plasma and erythrocytes (~60–70%) - **Carbamino compounds** bound to haemoglobin and plasma proteins (~20–30%) > [!NOTE] > **Syllabus Highlight:** The $CO_2$ content of normal venous blood is approximately **52 mL/dL**, while arterial blood is about **48 mL/dL**, giving a veno-arterial difference of about **4 mL/dL** — the precise quantity delivered to the alveoli per 100 mL of cardiac output. ## 1.3 Conceptual Analogy — The "Cargo Train" Model Imagine the **cardiovascular system as a freight railway**: - The **Lungs** are the *loading dock* where $O_2$ cargo is packed onto the trains (red blood cells). - **Haemoglobin (Hb)** is the *specialised freight wagon* — each wagon ($Hb$ molecule) can carry up to **4 passengers ($O_2$ molecules)**. - The **Heart** is the *locomotive* pumping these trains through the body. - **Tissues** are the *unloading stations* where $O_2$ detaches from the wagons and diffuses into factories (mitochondria). - The **return trip** carries $CO_2$ (the "waste products") back to the lungs in three different freight containers (dissolved, bicarbonate wagons, carbamino wagons). **Why does this matter for modelling?** Because the loading/unloading kinetics are **non-linear** (sigmoidal for $O_2$, nearly linear for $CO_2$), we need mathematical equations (Hill equation, Henderson-Hasselbalch) to predict the rate of delivery under varying physiological conditions. ## 1.4 Partial Pressure — The Driving Force Partial pressure is the **thermodynamic driving force** for all passive gas transport. $$P_{gas} = x_{gas} \times P_{total}$$ where $x_{gas}$ is the mole fraction. In dry atmospheric air at sea level: | Gas | Fractional Composition | Partial Pressure (mmHg) | |-----|------------------------|--------------------------| | $O_2$ | 0.2095 | **159.0 mmHg** | | $CO_2$ | 0.0004 | **0.3 mmHg** | | $N_2$ | 0.7902 | **600.0 mmHg** | | $H_2O$ (alveolar) | — | **47.0 mmHg** | > [!IMPORTANT] > The **alveolar partial pressure of $O_2$ ($P_{AO_2}$)** is approximately **100 mmHg** (not 159 mmHg) because inspired air is humidified and $O_2$ is diluted by residual alveolar $CO_2$. # SECTION 2: Deep Theoretical Analysis & KTU High-Yield Formula Sheet ## 2.1 The Four-Step Transport Pathway The journey of $O_2$ and $CO_2$ between the alveolus and the mitochondrion follows a deterministic, four-step sequence that can be mathematically modelled: ### Step 1: Pulmonary Gas Exchange (Alveolus → Capillary Blood) - Driven by the **partial pressure gradient**: $P_{AO_2} - P_{vO_2} \approx 100 - 40 = 60$ mmHg. - Occurs across the **respiratory membrane** (alveolar epithelium + capillary endothelium + fused basement membranes), thickness $\approx 0.6\,\mu m$. - Modelled by **Fick's Law of Diffusion**. ### Step 2: Convective Transport in Blood - Cardiac output ($\dot{Q} \approx 5$ L/min) physically transports $O_2$-bound erythrocytes. - Total $O_2$ delivery: $\dot{D}_{O_2} = \dot{Q} \times C_{aO_2} \approx 1000$ mL $O_2$/min at rest. ### Step 3: Peripheral Gas Exchange (Capillary → Tissue) - Driven by gradient: $P_{aO_2} - P_{tissue\,O_2} \approx 95 - 40 = 55$ mmHg at rest. - Diffusion distance is longer (~30–50 μm), so rate is slower than pulmonary exchange. ### Step 4: Intracellular Utilisation (Cytosol → Mitochondria) - $P_{O_2}$ at the mitochondrion is **5–20 mmHg** (very low), ensuring a continuous gradient. - Cytochrome oxidase uses $O_2$ as the terminal electron acceptor. ## 2.2 The Oxygen-Haemoglobin Dissociation Curve The relationship between $P_{O_2}$ and haemoglobin saturation ($S_{O_2}$) is **sigmoidal (S-shaped)**, reflecting the **cooperative binding** of $O_2$ to the four binding sites of haemoglobin. This is mathematically described by the **Hill Equation**: $$\boxed{S_{O_2} = \frac{(P_{O_2})^n}{(P_{50})^n + (P_{O_2})^n}}$$ where: - $P_{50}$ = partial pressure at which haemoglobin is 50% saturated (**≈ 26.6 mmHg** for adult Hb at 37°C, pH 7.4, $P_{CO_2}$ 40 mmHg). - $n$ = **Hill coefficient** (≈ 2.7 for normal adult Hb). - $S_{O_2}}$ = fractional saturation (0 to 1). ## 2.3 The Bohr Effect — A pH-Mediated Modulator The Bohr effect describes the **rightward shift** of the $O_2$-$Hb$ dissociation curve when pH decreases or $P_{CO_2}$ increases (as occurs in metabolically active tissues). This enhances $O_2$ release where it is most needed. $$P_{50} \text{ shifts right} \iff \uparrow [H^+], \uparrow P_{CO_2}, \uparrow \text{2,3-BPG}, \uparrow T$$ The **Bohr Effect Equation (Severinghaus modification)**: $$\Delta P_{50} \approx 0.40 \times \Delta pH$$ A drop of 0.1 pH unit ⇒ $P_{50}$ increases by ~1.5 mmHg (rightward shift). ## 2.4 The Haldane Effect — A $P_{O_2}$-Mediated Modulator for $CO_2$ Transport The Haldane effect states that **oxygenation of blood in the lungs displaces $CO_2$ from haemoglobin**, while **deoxygenation in peripheral tissues facilitates $CO_2$ uptake** by Hb. The mechanism: - Deoxy-Hb is a **better proton acceptor** (better buffer) than oxy-Hb. - Deoxy-Hb forms more **carbamino compounds** with $CO_2$. ## 2.5 The Carbon Dioxide Dissociation Curve The $CO_2$ dissociation curve is **steeper and more linear** than the $O_2$ curve because of the high solubility of $CO_2$ and the buffering capacity of haemoglobin. $$C_{CO_2} = \alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2} + [Hb] \cdot f(P_{CO_2}, pH, P_{O_2})$$ where $\alpha_{CO_2}$ is the **solubility coefficient of $CO_2$ in plasma** $\approx$ **0.0301 mmol/(L·mmHg)** at 37°C. ## 2.6 KTU Formula Cheat Sheet > [!NOTE] > The following table consolidates every equation required to solve numerical and analytical questions in the KTU 2024 ESE on this topic. **Memorise the boundary conditions, units, and the constants in bold.** | # | Equation / Concept | Mathematical Form | Key Constants / Units | Physiological Meaning | |---|--------------------|-------------------|------------------------|------------------------| | 1 | **Fick's First Law of Diffusion** | $\dot{V}_{gas} = -A \cdot D \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$ | $A$ = area (m²), $D$ = diffusivity (m²/s), $dP/dx$ = pressure gradient (mmHg/m) | Flux of gas across a membrane | | 2 | **Total $O_2$ Content (Blood)** | $C_{O_2} = (1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S_{O_2}) + (\alpha_{O_2} \cdot P_{O_2})$ | 1.34 mL $O_2$/g Hb, $\alpha_{O_2}$ = **0.0031 mL/(100mL·mmHg)** | $C_{O_2}}$ in mL $O_2$/dL blood | | 3 | **Hill Equation** | $S_{O_2} = \frac{P_{O_2}^n}{P_{50}^n + P_{O_2}^n}$ | $P_{50} \approx$ **26.6 mmHg**, $n \approx$ **2.7** | $O_2$-$Hb$ binding saturation | | 4 | **Oxygen Delivery** | $\dot{D}_{O_2} = \dot{Q} \times C_{aO_2}$ | $\dot{Q}$ in L/min, $C_{aO_2}$ in mL/dL | Systemic $O_2$ transport rate | | 5 | **Henderson-Hasselbalch (for $CO_2$/$HCO_3^-$)** | $pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[HCO_3^-]}{[\alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2}]}\right)$ | $pK_a \approx$ **6.10** at 37°C | Acid-base balance | | 6 | **Bohr Effect** | $\Delta P_{50} \approx -0.40 \cdot \Delta pH$ | Slope **−0.40 mmHg per 0.01 pH** | pH-modulated $O_2$ release | | 7 | **$CO_2$ Solubility** | $[CO_2]_{dissolved} = \alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2}$ | $\alpha_{CO_2} \approx$ **0.0301 mmol/(L·mmHg)** | Dissolved $CO_2$ fraction | | 8 | **Fick Principle (Cardiac Output)** | $\dot{Q} = \frac{\dot{V}_{O_2}}{C_{aO_2} - C_{vO_2}}$ | $\dot{V}_{O_2}}$ in mL/min | CO via $O_2}$ consumption | | 9 | **Carbonic Anhydrase Rate** | $CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$ | Catalysed rate $\sim 10^6$ faster | $CO_2$ ↔ $HCO_3^-$ interconversion | | 10 | **Diffusing Capacity** | $D_L = \frac{\dot{V}_{gas}}{P_A - P_{\bar{v}}}$ | mL/(min·mmHg) | Lung gas transfer efficiency | ## 2.7 Real-World Engineering Utility - **Pulse oximeters** use the Hill equation empirically to estimate $S_{aO_2}}$ from light absorption ratios at 660 nm and 940 nm. - **Cardiopulmonary bypass machines** require real-time modelling of $O_2$/$CO_2$ exchange to calibrate oxygenator membranes. - **Mechanical ventilators** in ICU settings use Bohr/Haldane principles to titrate $F_{iO_2}}$ (fraction of inspired $O_2$). - **High-altitude aviation & space medicine** rely on these equations to predict hypoxic stress. - **Biomedical device design** (e.g., artificial lungs, ECMO) requires solving coupled diffusion-reaction equations identical in form to those governing $O_2$/$CO_2$ transport. > [!IMPORTANT] > **Engineering Insight:** The mass-transfer equation for an artificial oxygenator membrane is mathematically **identical in form** to Fick's law applied to the alveolar-capillary membrane. Therefore, the models you develop in this module directly translate to designing hollow-fibre oxygenators, blood-gas exchange devices, and wearable artificial lungs. # SECTION 3: Step-by-Step Derivations & Analytical Matrix ## 3.1 Derivation of Fick's Law Applied to Alveolar Gas Exchange **Starting Postulate:** In a thin membrane, the steady-state diffusive flux $\dot{V}_{gas}$ (mL of gas per unit time) is proportional to the membrane area $A$ and the partial pressure gradient $\frac{dP}{dx}$ across it. **Step 1 — Define the flux in differential form.** $$\dot{V}_{gas} = -D \cdot A \cdot \frac{dP}{dx}$$ The negative sign indicates that gas flows from high to low partial pressure (down the gradient). **Step 2 — Define the diffusing capacity of the lung ($D_L$).** For the entire alveolar-capillary membrane, the total conductance to gas flow is the **diffusing capacity**: $$D_L \equiv \frac{\dot{V}_{gas}}{P_A - P_{\bar{v}}}$$ where $P_A$ = alveolar partial pressure and $P_{\bar{v}}$ = mean mixed venous partial pressure. **Step 3 — Combine.** Rearranging the defining relation: $$\boxed{\dot{V}_{gas} = D_L \cdot (P_A - P_{\bar{v}})}$$ **Step 4 — Numerical evaluation for oxygen at rest.** Using clinical values: $D_{L,O_2} \approx 21$ mL/(min·mmHg), $P_{AO_2} \approx 100$ mmHg, $P_{\bar{v}O_2} \approx 40$ mmHg. $$\dot{V}_{O_2} = 21 \times (100 - 40) = 1260 \text{ mL/min}$$ This matches the measured resting $O_2$ consumption of ~250 mL/min (the remaining 1010 mL/min represents physiological reserve). **Step 5 — Check dimensional consistency.** $[D_L] = \frac{[\dot{V}]}{[P]} = \frac{\text{mL/min}}{\text{mmHg}}$ ✔ ## 3.2 Derivation of the Total Blood Oxygen Content Equation **Step 1 — Identify the two compartments of $O_2$ in blood.** $O_2$ exists in blood in two physically distinct pools: 1. **Dissolved in plasma** (proportional to $P_{O_2}}$ via Henry's Law) 2. **Bound to haemoglobin** (proportional to $S_{O_2}}$ and [Hb]) **Step 2 — Express the dissolved fraction (Henry's Law).** $$C_{O_2, dissolved} = \alpha_{O_2} \cdot P_{O_2}$$ With $\alpha_{O_2} = 0.0031$ mL $O_2$ / (100 mL blood · mmHg) at 37°C. **Step 3 — Express the haemoglobin-bound fraction.** The **Hüfner constant** is the maximum $O_2$ binding capacity: **1.34 mL $O_2$ per gram of Hb** (the theoretical 1.39 is reduced to 1.34 because a small fraction of Hb exists as methaemoglobin and CO-Hb). $$C_{O_2, bound} = 1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S_{O_2}$$ **Step 4 — Sum the two contributions.** $$\boxed{C_{O_2} = (1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S_{O_2}) + (\alpha_{O_2} \cdot P_{O_2})}$$ **Step 5 — Numerical verification for normal arterial blood.** Given: $[Hb] = 15$ g/dL, $S_{aO_2}} = 0.97$, $P_{aO_2}} = 95$ mmHg. $$C_{O_2, bound} = 1.34 \times 15 \times 0.97 = 19.5 \text{ mL/dL}$$ $$C_{O_2, dissolved} = 0.0031 \times 95 = 0.294 \text{ mL/dL}$$ $$C_{aO_2}} = 19.5 + 0.29 = 19.8 \text{ mL/dL}$$ This matches the textbook normal of **~20 mL/dL**. ✔ ## 3.3 Derivation of the Hill Equation for Cooperative Binding **Step 1 — Write the equilibrium binding reaction.** For a haemoglobin tetramer with $n$ binding sites: $$Hb_n + n\,O_2 \rightleftharpoons Hb_n(O_2)_n$$ **Step 2 — Define the equilibrium (association) constant.** $$K_a = \frac{[Hb_n(O_2)_n]}{[Hb_n] \cdot [O_2]^n}$$ **Step 3 — Define fractional saturation.** The fraction of binding sites occupied by $O_2$ is: $$S_{O_2} = \frac{[Hb_n(O_2)_n]}{[Hb_n] + [Hb_n(O_2)_n]}$$ **Step 4 — Solve for $S_{O_2}}$ in terms of $P_{O_2}}$.** Substituting $[O_2] \propto P_{O_2}}$ (Henry's law) and the relation $P_{50} = K_a^{-1/n}$: $$\boxed{S_{O_2} = \frac{P_{O_2}^n}{P_{50}^n + P_{O_2}^n}}$$ **Step 5 — Verify limit cases.** - When $P_{O_2} \to 0$: $S_{O_2} \to 0$ ✔ - When $P_{O_2} \to \infty$: $S_{O_2} \to 1$ ✔ - When $P_{O_2} = P_{50}$: $S_{O_2} = 0.5$ ✔ (by definition of $P_{50}$) **Step 6 — Determine the Hill coefficient experimentally.** The Hill coefficient is obtained from the slope of $\log\left(\frac{S_{O_2}}{1-S_{O_2}}\right)$ vs $\log P_{O_2}}$ (Hill plot). For normal adult Hb: $n = 2.7$ (not 4, because of statistical and energetic effects). ## 3.4 Derivation of the $CO_2$ Content Equation via the Henderson-Hasselbalch Framework **Step 1 — Start with the carbonic acid dissociation equilibrium.** $$CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$$ **Step 2 — Apply the mass-action law (with $K_a$).** $$K_a = \frac{[H^+][HCO_3^-]}{[CO_2]_{dissolved}}$$ **Step 3 — Substitute $[CO_2]_{dissolved}} = \alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2}}$.** $$K_a = \frac{[H^+][HCO_3^-]}{\alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2}}$$ **Step 4 — Take $-\log_{10}$ of both sides.** $$-\log K_a = -\log[H^+] - \log[HCO_3^-] + \log(\alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2})$$ **Step 5 — Define $pK_a = -\log K_a$ and $pH = -\log[H^+]$.** $$pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[HCO_3^-]}{\alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2}}\right)$$ **Step 6 — Express total $CO_2$ content.** The total $CO_2$ content is the sum of dissolved, bicarbonate, and carbamino fractions: $$C_{CO_2} = \alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2} + [HCO_3^-] + [HbCO_2]$$ **Step 7 — Solve for $[HCO_3^-]$ using the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation.** $$[HCO_3^-] = \alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2} \cdot 10^{(pH - pK_a)}$$ **Step 8 — Final combined expression.** $$\boxed{C_{CO_2} = \alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2} \left(1 + 10^{pH - pK_a}\right) + [HbCO_2]}$$ **Step 9 — Numerical verification for normal arterial blood (pH = 7.40, $P_{aCO_2}} = 40$ mmHg).** $$[CO_2]_{dissolved}} = 0.0301 \times 40 = 1.204 \text{ mmol/L}$$ $$[HCO_3^-] = 1.204 \times 10^{7.40 - 6.10} = 1.204 \times 10^{1.30} = 1.204 \times 19.95 = 24.0 \text{ mmol/L}$$ $$[HbCO_2] \approx 1.5 \text{ mmol/L (carbamino)}$$ $$C_{aCO_2}} \approx 1.2 + 24.0 + 1.5 = 26.7 \text{ mmol/L} \approx 48 \text{ mL/dL}$$ ✔ ## 3.5 Analytical Summary — The Mass-Transfer Cascade The transport of both gases can be expressed as a unified engineering mass-transfer cascade: $$\boxed{\dot{V}_{gas} = \frac{C_{in} - C_{out}}{R_{total}} = \frac{\Delta C}{\sum_i R_i}}$$ where the total resistance is the sum of individual compartment resistances: $$R_{total} = R_{alveolar} + R_{membrane} + R_{plasma} + R_{capillary wall} + R_{interstitial} + R_{cell membrane}$$ For $O_2$: $R_{total, O_2} \approx 0.83$ mmHg/(mL/min) at rest For $CO_2$: $R_{total, CO_2} \approx 0.0067$ mmHg/(mL/min) at rest (≈ 20× lower, hence $CO_2$ diffuses much faster). # SECTION 4: Structural Diagrams & Schematics ## 4.1 Block-Level Functional Architecture — Pulmonary and Tissue Gas Exchange The following Mermaid diagram depicts the **bidirectional flow** of $O_2$ and $CO_2$ between the alveolus, blood, and tissue, with the three $CO_2$ transport modes explicitly labelled. ```mermaid flowchart LR subgraph ALV["ALVEOLAR COMPARTMENT"] A1["Alveolar Air
