Quantitatively, it is expressed as: $$\Delta P_{50} \approx -0.40 \times \Delta pH$$ A drop in pH of 0.10 (i.e., from 7.40 to 7.30) raises $P_{50}$ by ~1.5 mmHg, shifting the curve to the right. **Physiological Significance:** Metabolically active tissues produce $CO_2$ and lactic acid, lowering local pH. This **automatically increases $O_2$ unloading precisely where it is most needed**, providing a beautifully elegant autoregulatory mechanism. (1 mark for definition, 1 mark for equation, 1 mark for significance) --- ### Question 2: List the three forms in which $CO_2$ is transported in blood, with their approximate percentages. `[KTU University Exam – Dec 2023]` **Model Answer (3 Marks):** | Form | % of Total $CO_2$ Transported | Key Equation | |------|-------------------------------|---------------| | **1. Dissolved $CO_2$ in plasma** | 5–10% | $\alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2}}$ | | **2. Bicarbonate ions ($HCO_3^-$)** | 60–70% | $CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$ (catalysed by carbonic anhydrase) | | **3. Carbamino compounds** (bound to Hb and plasma proteins) | 20–30% | $Hb\text{-}NH_2 + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons Hb\text{-}NHCOOH$ | (1 mark each for the three forms with their percentages) --- ## PART B — Long Answer Questions (14 Marks Each, with Internal Choice) ### Question A (14 Marks): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set A]` **(a)** Derive the **Hill equation** for the oxygen-haemoglobin dissociation curve. State the meaning of the Hill coefficient and explain why it is approximately 2.7 and not 4. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** A patient has the following arterial blood gas values: - $P_{aO_2}}$ = 60 mmHg - $[Hb] = 12$ g/dL - pH = 7.30 - Body temperature = 39°C (febrile) Using the Hill equation, calculate the **arterial oxygen content ($C_{aO_2}}$)** and the **oxygen delivery** if cardiac output is 5 L/min. Assume $P_{50}$ shifts from 26.6 mmHg to 31.0 mmHg due to the combined Bohr and thermal effects. **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question A(a) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (1 Mark):** Write the equilibrium binding reaction for haemoglobin with $n$ binding sites: $$Hb_n + n\,O_2 \rightleftharpoons Hb_n(O_2)_n$$ **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Define the association constant: $$K_a = \frac{[Hb_n(O_2)_n]}{[Hb_n][O_2]^n}$$ **Step 3 (1 Mark):** Define fractional saturation $S_{O_2}}$ and solve the algebraic identity: $$S_{O_2} = \frac{[Hb_n(O_2)_n]}{[Hb_n] + [Hb_n(O_2)_n]} = \frac{K_a[O_2]^n}{1 + K_a[O_2]^n}$$ **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Use Henry's Law, $[O_2] \propto P_{O_2}}$, and substitute $P_{50} = K_a^{-1/n}$: $$\boxed{S_{O_2} = \frac{P_{O_2}^n}{P_{50}^n + P_{O_2}^n}}$$ **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Verify the boundary conditions: - $P_{O_2} = 0 \Rightarrow S_{O_2} = 0$ ✔ - $P_{O_2} = P_{50} \Rightarrow S_{O_2} = 0.5$ ✔ - $P_{O_2} \to \infty \Rightarrow S_{O_2} \to 1$ ✔ **Step 6 (1 Mark):** State the meaning of the Hill coefficient: $n$ is the slope of the Hill plot and quantifies the degree of **cooperative binding**. $n = 1$ implies non-cooperative (hyperbolic) binding, $n = 4$ implies perfectly cooperative. **Step 7 (1 Mark):** Explain $n \approx 2.7$ vs the theoretical 4: Although haemoglobin has 4 binding sites, the observed $n$ is less than 4 due to: - **Statistical factors** — Not all four sites are equivalent. - **Negative cooperativity at high saturation** — Binding of the 4th $O_2}$ is energetically less favourable. - **Allosteric constraints** — The T→R state transition is not perfectly synchronised. - The maximum theoretical value of $n$ is 4; experimentally, $n \approx 2.7$ for adult HbA. --- #### Model Solution for Question A(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (2 Marks):** Apply the Hill equation to calculate $S_{O_2}}$ with the new $P_{50}$ = 31.0 mmHg, $n = 2.7$, $P_{O_2}} = 60$ mmHg: $$S_{O_2} = \frac{60^{2.7}}{31.0^{2.7} + 60^{2.7}}$$ Calculate $60^{2.7}$: $$\log_{10}(60) = 1.778 \Rightarrow 2.7 \times 1.778 = 4.801 \Rightarrow 60^{2.7} = 10^{4.801} = 63,240$$ Calculate $31.0^{2.7}$: $$\log_{10}(31) = 1.491 \Rightarrow 2.7 \times 1.491 = 4.026 \Rightarrow 31.0^{2.7} = 10^{4.026} = 10,617$$ $$S_{O_2} = \frac{63,240}{10,617 + 63,240} = \frac{63,240}{73,857} = 0.856 = 85.6\%$$ **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Calculate the haemoglobin-bound $O_2}$: $$C_{O_2,bound} = 1.34 \times 12 \times 0.856 = 13.77 \text{ mL/dL}$$ **Step 3 (1 Mark):** Calculate the dissolved $O_2}$: $$C_{O_2,dissolved} = 0.0031 \times 60 = 0.186 \text{ mL/dL}$$ **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Calculate total arterial $O_2$ content: $$C_{aO_2}} = 13.77 + 0.186 = 13.96 \text{ mL/dL} \approx 14.0 \text{ mL/dL}$$ **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Calculate oxygen delivery: $$\dot{D}_{O_2} = \dot{Q} \times C_{aO_2}} = 5000 \text{ mL/min} \times 0.140 = 700 \text{ mL/min}$$ **Step 6 (1 Mark):** Clinical interpretation: This patient has a **30% reduction in oxygen delivery** compared to normal (~1000 mL/min), indicating significant hypoxaemia consistent with the rightward shift from the combined Bohr effect (low pH) and hyperthermia. --- ### Question B (14 Marks, Alternative Choice): `[KTU University Exam – May 2024, Set B]` **(a)** Describe the **three modes of carbon dioxide transport** in blood with appropriate equations. Explain the role of the **Haldane effect** in this process. **(7 Marks)** **(b)** Derive the **Henderson-Hasselbalch equation** as applied to the $CO_2$/$HCO_3^-$ buffer system. A patient has a $P_{CO_2}} = 60$ mmHg and $[HCO_3^-] = 30$ mmol/L. Calculate the blood pH and interpret the result. Use $pK_a = 6.10$, $\alpha_{CO_2} = 0.0301$ mmol/(L·mmHg). **(7 Marks)** --- #### Model Solution for Question B(a) — 7 Marks **Mode 1 — Dissolved $CO_2$ (1 Mark):** Approximately 5–10% of total $CO_2$ is physically dissolved in plasma following Henry's Law: $$[CO_2]_{dissolved} = \alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2}}$$ **Mode 2 — Bicarbonate ($HCO_3^-$) (2 Marks):** Approximately 60–70% of $CO_2$ is transported as bicarbonate. The reaction occurs primarily inside erythrocytes and is catalysed by **carbonic anhydrase** at a rate of $\sim 10^6$ times faster than the uncatalysed reaction: $$CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$$ The $HCO_3^-$ is then exchanged for $Cl^-$ via the **chloride shift (Hamburger phenomenon)**, and the $H^+$ is buffered by deoxy-haemoglobin (Bohr effect). **Mode 3 — Carbamino compounds (1 Mark):** About 20–30% of $CO_2$ binds reversibly to the amino groups of haemoglobin (and plasma proteins): $$Hb\text{-}NH_2 + CO_2 \rightleftharpoons Hb\text{-}NH\text{-}COOH$$ **Haldane Effect — 3 Marks:** The Haldane effect states that **oxygenation of blood in the lungs promotes $CO_2$ release**, while **deoxygenation in tissues promotes $CO_2$ uptake** by haemoglobin. The mechanism is twofold: 1. **Oxy-Hb is a stronger acid** (releases $H^+$) than deoxy-Hb. The released $H^+$ combines with $HCO_3^-$ to liberate $CO_2$ in the lungs. 2. **Deoxy-Hb forms more carbamino compounds** than oxy-Hb, so venous blood carries more $CO_2$ as carbaminohaemoglobin than arterial blood. Mathematically, the Haldane effect can be expressed as a dependence of the $CO_2$ content on $S_{O_2}}$: $$C_{CO_2} = f(P_{CO_2}, pH) + \beta \cdot (1 - S_{O_2})$$ where $\beta$ is a coupling constant (~0.5–1.0 mmol/L per unit desaturation). **Physiological Significance:** The Haldane effect **doubles the amount of $CO_2$** that can be exchanged per unit of blood flow, providing enormous reserve capacity. --- #### Model Solution for Question B(b) — 7 Marks **Step 1 (2 Marks):** Derive the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation. Start with the equilibrium: $$CO_2 + H_2O \rightleftharpoons H_2CO_3 \rightleftharpoons H^+ + HCO_3^-$$ The acid dissociation constant: $$K_a = \frac{[H^+][HCO_3^-]}{[CO_2]_{dissolved}}$$ Substitute $[CO_2]_{dissolved}} = \alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2}}$: $$K_a = \frac{[H^+][HCO_3^-]}{\alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2}}$$ Take $-\log_{10}$ of both sides: $$pH = pK_a + \log_{10}\left(\frac{[HCO_3^-]}{\alpha_{CO_2} \cdot P_{CO_2}}\right)$$ **Step 2 (1 Mark):** Identify given values: $P_{CO_2}} = 60$ mmHg, $[HCO_3^-] = 30$ mmol/L, $pK_a = 6.10$, $\alpha_{CO_2} = 0.0301$ mmol/(L·mmHg). **Step 3 (1 Mark):** Calculate the dissolved $CO_2$: $$[CO_2]_{dissolved} = 0.0301 \times 60 = 1.806 \text{ mmol/L}$$ **Step 4 (1 Mark):** Substitute into the Henderson-Hasselbalch equation: $$pH = 6.10 + \log_{10}\left(\frac{30}{1.806}\right)$$ $$pH = 6.10 + \log_{10}(16.61)$$ **Step 5 (1 Mark):** Calculate the logarithm: $$\log_{10}(16.61) = 1.220$$ $$pH = 6.10 + 1.220 = 7.32$$ **Step 6 (1 Mark):** Clinical interpretation: A pH of 7.32 is below the normal range (7.35–7.45), indicating **acidosis**. Specifically, with an elevated $P_{CO_2}} = 60$ mmHg (normal = 40) and a partially compensated elevated $[HCO_3^-] = 30$ mmol/L (normal = 24), this is a case of **respiratory acidosis with partial renal compensation**. The kidneys have retained bicarbonate to buffer the excess carbonic acid. --- > [!WARNING] > **KTU Examiner's Valuation Warning — Common Pitfalls:** > 1. **Confusing $C_{O_2}}$ with $S_{O_2}}$**: Many students interchange **content** (mL/dL) and **saturation** (%). They are different! Content includes both dissolved and bound fractions. (Loss: 2–3 marks) > 2. **Using $n = 4$ for the Hill coefficient**: This is a frequent error. The correct value is **$n = 2.7$** for normal adult Hb. (Loss: 1 mark) > 3. **Forgetting the chloride (Hamburger) shift** in $CO_2$ transport questions: The transport of $HCO_3^-$ out of the RBC and $Cl^-$ in is essential for mass balance. (Loss: 1–2 marks) > 4. **Mixing up the Bohr and Haldane effects**: The Bohr effect concerns **$O_2$** unloading in tissues due to $H^+$ and $CO_2$, while the Haldane effect concerns **$CO_2$** carriage being affected by $O_2$ saturation. (Loss: 2 marks) > 5. **Unit errors in $C_{aO_2}}$ calculation**: The Hüfner constant is **1.34 mL $O_2$ / g Hb**, not 1.39. Use 1.34 in all KTU numerical problems. (Loss: 1 mark) > 6. **Omitting the Henderson-Hasselbalch derivation steps**: Direct substitution of final equation without derivation is penalised. (Loss: 2–3 marks) > 7. **Using "Hb" without specifying "HbO$_2$" vs "Deoxy-Hb"**: The Bohr and Haldane effects depend on the **conformational state** of haemoglobin (T vs R), which must be specified. (Loss: 1 mark) --- ## Topic Recap & Important Things to Remember (Rapid Revision Checklist) > [!NOTE] > **High-Yield Summary for Last-Minute Revision:** - ⭐ **$O_2$ is carried primarily bound to Hb** (98.5%) and only 1.5% is dissolved. **$CO_2$ is carried primarily as $HCO_3^-$** (~65%) with smaller dissolved (~7%) and carbamino (~25%) fractions. - ⭐ **Hill equation:** $S_{O_2} = \dfrac{P_{O_2}^n}{P_{50}^n + P_{O_2}^n}$, with $P_{50} \approx 26.6$ mmHg, $n \approx 2.7$. - ⭐ **Total $O_2}$ content formula:** $C_{O_2} = (1.34 \cdot [Hb] \cdot S_{O_2}) + (0.0031 \cdot P_{O_2}})$. - ⭐ **Henderson-Hasselbalch:** $pH = 6.10 + \log_{10}\left(\dfrac{[HCO_3^-]}{0.0301 \cdot P_{CO_2}}}\right)$. - ⭐ **Bohr Effect:** ↓ pH or ↑ $P_{CO_2}} \Rightarrow$ ↑ $P_{50} \Rightarrow$ rightward shift $\Rightarrow$ ↑ $O_2}$ release in tissues. - ⭐ **Haldane Effect:** ↑ $P_{O_2}}$ in lungs $\Rightarrow$ ↑ $CO_2$ release from Hb; ↓ $P_{O_2}}$ in tissues $\Rightarrow$ ↑ $CO_2$ uptake by Hb. - ⭐ **Fick's Law of Diffusion:** $\dot{V}_{gas} = -A \cdot D \cdot \dfrac{dP}{dx}$, the cornerstone of all gas exchange modelling. - ⭐ **Carbonic anhydrase** accelerates the $CO_2 \leftrightarrow HCO_3^-$ interconversion by a factor of $\sim 10^6$. - ⭐ **Chloride (Hamburger) shift** is essential for maintaining electrochemical neutrality in $CO_2$ transport. - ⭐ **Normal values to memorise:** $P_{aO_2}} = 95$ mmHg, $P_{aCO_2}} = 40$ mmHg, $P_{vO_2}} = 40$ mmHg, $P_{vCO_2}} = 46$ mmHg, $S_{aO_2}} = 97\%$, $S_{vO_2}} = 75\%$, $[Hb] = 15$ g/dL, $\dot{Q} = 5$ L/min, $\dot{V}_{O_2}} = 250$ mL/min at rest. - ⭐ **Engineering link:** The alveolar-capillary membrane equations are mathematically identical to those for hollow-fibre oxygenators used in cardiopulmonary bypass and ECMO. - ⭐ **KTU 2024 examiner priority:** Emphasis is on the **derivation of the Hill equation**, **Henderson-Hasselbalch**, and **numerical application of the $C_{O_2}}$ content equation** — practice these derivations multiple times before the exam. - ⭐ **Mnemonic for $CO_2$ transport modes — "DBC":** **D**issolved, **B**icarbonate, **C**arbamino. Approximate percentages: **5–10%, 60–70%, 20–30%**. - ⭐ **Mnemonic for Bohr effect drivers — "HCL":** **H**ydrogen ions (↓ pH), **C**arbon dioxide (↑ $P_{CO_2}}$), **L**actate + ↑ temperature + ↑ 2,3-BPG — all shift the curve **right**. - ⭐ **Clinical sign to remember:** A right shift in the $O_2$-$Hb$ curve (Bohr effect) and the corresponding Haldane effect work **synergistically** to maximise $O_2$ delivery and $CO_2$ removal during exercise.
